metadata.title: The Third Book of Sentences
metadata.author: Taio of Zaragoza
known_issues: Some section numbering follows Gregorian sources and may not align with manuscript divisions
Greek transliterations represent best approximation from damaged Latin text
The Latin text has 'confidunt' in chunk 312 which appears unclear or possibly corrupt in this context.
Biblical reference in chunk 299 gives Hebrews 12:11 but modern numbering would be 12:14.
Several textual variants noted in editorial footnotes (e.g., 'non manufactam' vs variants)
Some biblical citations compressed for audio clarity
Some passages contain complex theological vocabulary that has multiple valid translation approaches
Citation formulas abbreviated in source may vary from modern critical editions
Editorial notes reference multiple manuscript traditions (Vindoc., Bellov., etc.) not fully catalogued here
Biblical reference in chunk 330 gives 1 Corinthians 15:4 but should be 13:4.
Chunk 485 begins mid-work continuation from previous volume
Greek transliteration 'homoousios' does not appear in this section but protocol established
Several footnotes in the Latin reference variant manuscript readings and editorial differences that may affect interpretation
Chunk 336 contains a difficult phrase 'invisibilis, vel despectus apparuit' where the meaning is contextually ambiguous.
Chapter 54 ends abruptly—verify if this is actual conclusion of Book 3 or if material missing
The John 4:18 citation in chunk 274 appears incorrect; likely should be 1 John 4:18 for 'perfect love casts out fear'
The phrase 'Sudet in manu tua eleemosyna' in chunk 413 appears to be from Sirach/Ecclesiasticus but is not a verbatim quote in standard editions.
The attribution in chunk 374 gives 'book of Solomon' but the text is from 1 Samuel 15:22.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: God's Will and Permission in Human Affairs
chunks.latin: LIBER TERTIUS. CAPUT PRIMUM. De voluntate Dei ac permissu.
chunks.english: Book Three. Chapter One: God's Will and Permission.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Justus et misericors Deus mortalium acta disponens, alia concedit propitius, alia permittit iratus: [atque ea, quae permittit,] sic tolerat, ut haec in sui consilii usum vertat.
chunks.english: The just and merciful God, ordering the actions of mortals, grants some things in kindness and permits others in wrath. Yet even what he permits, he tolerates in such a way that he turns it to serve his purpose.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia VI, 18, n. 33

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Miro modo fit ut quod sine voluntate Dei agitur, voluntati Dei contrarium non sit; quia dum in bonum usum mala facta vertuntur, ejus consilio militant etiam quae ejus consilio repugnant.
chunks.english: In a marvelous way, what happens without God's will does not contradict his will—because when evil deeds are turned to good use, even things that resist his plan end up serving his plan.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum est: Magna opera Domini, exquisita in omnes voluntates ejus (Ps. CX, 2).
chunks.english: As Scripture says: The works of the Lord are great, sought out in all his purposes—from the Psalms, chapter one hundred ten, verse two.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic namque ejus opera magna sunt, ut per omne quod ab hominibus agitur, ejus voluntas exquiratur. Nam saepe inde perficitur unde repelli putabatur.
chunks.english: His works are great in precisely this way: that through everything people do, his will is accomplished. Often what seemed like it would thwart his purpose actually brings it to fulfillment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Rursum scriptum est: Omnia quaecunque voluit Dominus fecit in coelo et in terra (Ps. CXXXIV, 6).
chunks.english: Again Scripture says: Whatever the Lord willed, he accomplished in heaven and on earth—from the Psalms, chapter one hundred thirty-four, verse six.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc Salomon ait: Non est sapientia, non est prudentia, non est consilium contra Dominum (Prov. XXI, 39).
chunks.english: From this Solomon declares: There is no wisdom, no prudence, no counsel against the Lord—from Proverbs, chapter twenty-one, verse thirty-nine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Restat ergo ut in cunctis quae agimus vim supernae voluntatis inquiramus, cui videlicet cognitae debet nostra actio devote famulari, et quasi ducem sui itineris persequi, ne ei etiam nolens serviat, si hanc superbiens declinat.
chunks.english: Therefore, in everything we do, we should search out the force of God's heavenly will. Once we recognize it, our actions should serve it devotedly and follow it as the guide of our journey—lest by proudly turning away from it, we end up serving it unwillingly anyway.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vitari enim vis superni consilii nequaquam potest; sed magna sibi virtute hanc temperat, qui se sub ejus nutibus refrenat: ejusque sibi pondera levigat, qui hanc subjecto cordis humero volens portat.
chunks.english: The power of God's plan cannot be avoided. But the person who restrains himself beneath God's direction controls that power with great virtue. And whoever willingly bears God's plan on the humble shoulder of his heart lightens its weight for himself.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT II. De dispensatione divina.
chunks.english: Chapter Two: God's Dispensation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magna est omnipotentis Dei dispensatio, et plerumque contingit ut quibus majora dona praestat, quaedam minora non tribuat, ut semper eorum animus habeat unde ipse se reprehendat.
chunks.english: Great is the dispensation of almighty God. It often happens that to those he gives greater gifts, he does not grant certain smaller ones—so that their soul always has something for which to reproach itself.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Dialogues III, 14

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque electi appetunt perfecti esse, nec possunt; elaborant in hoc quod a Domino acceperunt, nec tamen laborando praevalent. In his autem quae accepta habent se minime extollunt, et discunt quia ex semetipsis majora bona non habent, qui in semetipsis vincere parva vitia non possunt.
chunks.english: Often the chosen desire to be perfect, but cannot. They work hard with what they have received from the Lord, yet even working hard, they do not prevail. But in what they have received, they do not exalt themselves—and they learn that they do not possess greater goods from themselves, since they cannot conquer even small faults by themselves.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Perducto Dominus ad terram promissionis populo, cunctos fortes atque praepotentes adversarios ejus exstinguens, Philisthaeos atque Chananaeos diutius reservavit, ut, sicut scriptum est, in eis experiretur Israel (Judic. III, 4).
chunks.english: When the Lord brought his people to the promised land and destroyed all their strong and mighty enemies, he nevertheless reserved the Philistines and Canaanites for a longer time—so that, as Scripture says in the book of Judges, chapter three, verse four, Israel might be tested through them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam, ut dictum est, eis etiam Dominus quibus magna dona tribuit parva quaedam reprehensibilia relinquit, ut semper habeant contra quod bellum gerant, et devictis magnis hostibus, mentem non erigant, quando eos adhuc adversarii etiam minime fatigant.
chunks.english: Sometimes, as we have said, the Lord leaves certain small faults even in those to whom he grants great gifts—so they always have something to fight against, and after conquering great enemies, they do not become proud when even small adversaries still harass them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Miro modo fit ut una eademque mens et virtute polleat, et ex infirmitate lassescat, quatenus et ex parte constructa sit, et ex parte se conspiciat esse destructam; ut per bonum quod quaerit et habere non valet, illud servet humiliter quod habet.
chunks.english: In a marvelous way, one and the same mind both flourishes with virtue and grows weary with weakness—so that it is partly built up and partly sees itself torn down. Through the good it seeks but cannot possess, it humbly preserves what it does have.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid igitur mirum quod hoc de homine dicimus, quando illa superna regio in civibus suis ex parte damna pertulit, et ex parte fortiter stetit, ut electi angelorum spiritus dum alios per superbiam cecidisse conspicerent, ipsi tanto robustius quanto humilius starent?
chunks.english: Why is it surprising that we say this about human beings, when even that heavenly realm suffered partial losses among its citizens and partly stood firm? The chosen angelic spirits, seeing others fall through pride, stood all the more strongly precisely because they stood more humbly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic ergo et in unaquaque anima agitur, ut in humilitatis custodia aliquando ad lucra maxima ex minimo damno servetur.
chunks.english: So it is also with each individual soul: sometimes in the safeguard of humility, it is preserved for the greatest gains through the smallest loss.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque hi qui magnis jam virtutibus splendent, adhuc de obscuritate culpae aliquid renitentes sustinent, ut etiam magna vitae claritate luceant, et tamen adhuc noctis reliquias nolentes trahant.
chunks.english: Often those who already shine with great virtues still endure something resisting them from the darkness of fault—so that even while they shine with great clarity of life, they still unwillingly drag along remnants of night.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia IV, 24, n. 44

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magna dispensatione omnipotentis Dei agitur, ut mens proficiens ad virtutem justitiae sua melius infirmitate roboretur, et inde verius in bonis luceat unde eam etiam nolentem parva reprehensibilia humiliter obscurant.
chunks.english: It is accomplished by the great dispensation of almighty God that a mind advancing toward the virtue of righteousness is better strengthened by its own weakness, and shines more truly in goodness precisely because small faults humbly obscure it even against its will.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum Israelitico populo percepta repromissionis terra partiretur, Ephraim tribui Chananaeus gentilis populus non occisus, sed factus tributarius dicitur, sicut scriptum est: Habitavit Chananaeus in medio Ephraim tributarius (Josue XVI, 10).
chunks.english: When the land of promise was divided among the Israelite people, the Canaanite gentile population was not killed but made tributary to the tribe of Ephraim, as it is written in the book of Joshua, chapter sixteen, verse ten: The Canaanite dwelt in the midst of Ephraim as a tributary.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid Chananaeus, gentilis videlicet populus, nisi vitium signat? Et saepe in magnis virtutibus terram repromissionis ingredimur, quia spe intima de aeternitate roboramur.
chunks.english: What does the Canaanite—that is, a gentile people—signify if not vice? And often we enter the land of promise through great virtues, because we are strengthened by inner hope concerning eternity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed dum inter acta sublimia vitia quaedam parva retinemus, quasi Chananaeum vivere in terra nostra concedimus. Chananaeus igitur tributarius efficitur, quia hoc ipsum vitium quod subigere non possumus ad usum nostrae utilitatis humiliter retorquemus, ut eo de se mens et in summis vilia sentiat, quo suis viribus etiam parva quae appetit non expugnat.
chunks.english: But when amid sublime deeds we retain certain small vices, we allow the Canaanite, so to speak, to live in our land. The Canaanite therefore becomes tributary, because the very vice we cannot conquer, we humbly twist to our own advantage—so that precisely because the mind cannot conquer even the small things it desires by its own strength, it feels itself worthless even in the highest things.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT III. Qualiter sacra Scriptura maledicti promat sententiam.
chunks.english: Chapter Three: How Sacred Scripture Pronounces the Sentence of Curse.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum certo novimus quod maledictum Scriptura sacra prohibet, cur recte aliquando fieri dicimus quod vitari eodem sacro eloquio non ignoramus?
chunks.english: Since we know with certainty that sacred Scripture forbids cursing, why do we say that sometimes what we know should be avoided according to that same sacred text is rightly done?
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia IV, 1, n. 1

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed sciendum nobis est quod Scriptura sacra duobus modis maledictum memorat, aliud videlicet quod approbat, aliud quod damnat. Aliter enim maledictum profertur judicio justitiae, aliter livore vindictae.
chunks.english: But we must understand that sacred Scripture mentions cursing in two ways: one that it approves, another that it condemns. A curse is pronounced one way by the judgment of justice, another way by the spite of vengeance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Maledictum judicio justitiae, ipso primo homine peccante, prolatum est, cum audivit: Maledicta terra in operibus tuis (Gen. III, 17).
chunks.english: A curse by the judgment of justice was pronounced when the first human being sinned, when he heard: Cursed is the earth in your works—from Genesis, chapter three, verse seventeen.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Maledictum justitiae judicio profertur, cum ad Abraham dicitur: Maledicam maledicentibus tibi (Gen. XII, 3).
chunks.english: A curse is pronounced by the judgment of justice when it is said to Abraham: I will curse those who curse you—from Genesis, chapter twelve, verse three.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Rursum quia maledictum non judicio justitiae, sed livore vindictae promitur, voce Pauli praedicantis admonemur, qui ait: Benedicite, et nolite maledicere (Rom. XII, 14). Et rursum: Neque maledici regnum Dei possidebunt (I Cor. VI, 10).
chunks.english: Again, because a curse is uttered not by the judgment of justice but by the spite of revenge, we are warned by the voice of Paul the preacher, who says in his letter to the Romans, chapter twelve, verse fourteen: Bless, and do not curse. And again in Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter six, verse ten: Neither will cursers possess the kingdom of God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Deus omnipotens maledicere dicitur, et tamen maledicere homo prohibetur; quia quod homo agit malitia vindictae, Deus non facit nisi examine et virtute justitiae.
chunks.english: Almighty God is said to curse, and yet a human being is forbidden to curse—because what a human being does through the malice of revenge, God does not do except through the examination and power of justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum sancti viri maledictionis sententiam proferunt, non ad hanc ex voto ultionis, sed ex justitia examinis erumpunt. Intus enim subtile Dei judicium aspiciunt, et mala foras exsurgentia, quia maledicto debeant feriri, cognoscunt: et eo in maledicto non peccant, quo ab interno judicio non discordant.
chunks.english: When holy men pronounce a sentence of curse, they do not break forth into it from a vow of vengeance, but from the justice of examination. For inwardly they see God's subtle judgment, and they recognize that evils arising outwardly deserve to be struck with a curse. And they do not sin in cursing precisely because they do not disagree with the internal judgment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Petrus apostolus in offerentem sibi pecunias Simonem sententiam maledictionis intorsit, dicens: Pecunia tua tecum sit in perditionem (Act. VIII, 20).
chunks.english: The apostle Peter hurled a sentence of curse against Simon who offered him money, saying in the book of Acts, chapter eight, verse twenty: May your money go with you to destruction.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui enim non ait, est, sed sit, non indicativo, sed optativo modo se haec divisse signavit.
chunks.english: For the one who did not say 'is' but 'may it be' indicated that he spoke these words not in the indicative mood but in the optative.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Elias denique duobus quinquagenariis ad se venientibus dixit: Si homo Dei sum, descendat ignis de coelo, et consumat vos (IV Reg. I, 10).
chunks.english: Likewise Elijah said to two captains of fifty coming to him, as recorded in the fourth book of Kings, chapter one, verse ten: If I am a man of God, let fire descend from heaven and consume you.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quorum utrorumque sententia quanta veritatis ratione convaluit, terminus causae monstravit. Nam et Simon aeterna perditione interiit, et duos quinquagenarios desuper veniens flamma consumpsit.
chunks.english: The outcome of the case showed with what rational truth the sentence of both prevailed. For Simon perished in eternal destruction, and the flame coming from above consumed the two captains of fifty.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Virtus subsequens testificatur qua mente maledictionis sententia promitur. Cum enim et maledicentis innocentia permanet, et tamen eum qui maledicitur usque ad interitum maledictio absorbet, ex utriusque partis fine colligitur quia ab uno et intimo judice in reum sententia sumpta jaculatur.
chunks.english: The power that follows testifies with what mind the sentence of curse is uttered. For when both the innocence of the one cursing remains, and yet the curse absorbs unto destruction the one who is cursed, it is gathered from the outcome of both parties that the sentence is hurled against the guilty party taken from the one intimate judge.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si subtiliter beati uniuscujusque, qualiter in Scriptura sacra maledicti promat sententiam, verba pensamus, non est ejus maledictio ex malitia delinquentis, sed ex rectitudine judicis; non est ira commoti, sed doctrina tranquilli.
chunks.english: If we carefully weigh the words of each blessed man regarding how he pronounces the sentence of curse in sacred Scripture, his curse does not arise from the malice of an offender but from the righteousness of a judge—not the anger of one disturbed but the teaching of one tranquil.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui enim tam recta maledicens intulit, non perturbationis vitio succubuit, sed doctrinae magisterium impendit.
chunks.english: For the one who pronounced such a righteous curse did not succumb to the vice of disturbance but exercised the office of teaching.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Five Ages of the World and Humanity
chunks.latin: CAPUT IV. De quinque aetatibus mundi atque hominibus.
chunks.english: Chapter Four: The Five Ages of the World and of Human Beings.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum magnopere est quia sicut in corpore, ita etiam sunt incrementa aetatis in mente. Prima quippe hominis aetas infantia est, cum etsi innocenter vivit, nescit tamen fari innocentiam quam habet.
chunks.english: It is very important to know that just as there are stages of growth in the body, so also are there stages of growth in the mind. The first age of a human being is infancy, when even if one lives innocently, one does not yet know how to speak about the innocence one has.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XI, 46, n. 62

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ac deinde pueritia sequitur, in qua jam valet dicere quod vult; cui succedit adolescentia, quae videlicet prima est aetas in operatione: quam juventus sequitur, scilicet apta fortitudini; ac postmodum senectus, etiam per tempus jam congrua maturitati.
chunks.english: And then follows childhood, in which one is already able to say what one wants. This is succeeded by adolescence, which is indeed the first age in action. Then follows youth, that is, the age suited to strength. And afterward old age, now suitable in time to maturity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Primam aetatem aptam bonis actibus adolescentiam novimus; et justi viri, cum in magna mentis maturitate proficiunt, nonnunquam ad memoriam actionum suarum initium reducunt; seque tantum de suis primordiis reprehendunt, quantum ex gravitate mentis altius profecerint, quia eo indiscretos se fuisse inveniunt, quo discretionis arcem postmodum plenius consequuntur.
chunks.english: We know adolescence to be the first age suited to good deeds. And just men, when they advance in great maturity of mind, sometimes bring back to memory the beginning of their actions. And they reproach themselves about their beginnings precisely to the degree that they have advanced higher in weightiness of mind—because they find themselves to have been indiscrete precisely to the degree that they afterward more fully attain the summit of discretion.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mane quippe intellectus nostri pueritia est. Hora autem tertia adolescentia intelligi potest, quia quasi jam sol in altum proficit, dum calor aetatis crescit.
chunks.english: Morning is, so to speak, the childhood of our understanding. The third hour can be understood as adolescence, because the sun is already advancing on high, as it were, while the warmth of age increases.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, In Evangelium I, Homily 19, n. 2

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sexta vero juventus est; quia velut in centro sol figitur, dum in ea plenitudo roboris solidatur. Nona autem senectus intelligitur, in qua velut sol ab alto axe descendit, quia aetas a calore juventutis deficit.
chunks.english: The sixth hour is youth, because the sun is fixed as if in the center while in it the fullness of strength is solidified. The ninth hour is understood as old age, in which the sun descends as if from its high axis, because age fails from the heat of youth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Undecima vero hora est ea aetas, quae decrepita, vel veterana dicitur. Unde Graeci valde seniores, non gerontas, sed presbyteros appellant, ut plus quam senes esse insinuent quos provectiores vocant.
chunks.english: The eleventh hour is that age which is called decrepit or very old. Hence the Greeks call the very elderly not gerontas but presbyteros, to suggest that those they call more advanced are more than merely old.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum infantia ad pueritiam, pueritia ad adolescentiam, adolescentia ad juventutem, juventus ad senectutem, senectus transit ad mortem, in cursu vitae praesentis ipsis suis augmentis homo ad detrimenta impellitur; et inde semper deficit, unde se proficere in spatium vitae credit.
chunks.english: As infancy passes to childhood, childhood to adolescence, adolescence to youth, youth to old age, and old age passes to death, in the course of present life the human being is driven toward losses by his very growth. And he always declines precisely from the source where he believes himself to be advancing in the span of life.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XI, 50, n. 68

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fixum in hoc mundo statum habere non possumus, ubi transituri venimus; atque hoc ipsum nostrum vivere, quotidie a vita transire est.
chunks.english: We cannot have a fixed state in this world, where we have come in order to pass through. And this very living of ours is a daily passing away from life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mens etenim mutabilitatis suae pondere ad aliud semper impellitur quam est, et nisi in statu suo arcta custodiae disciplina teneatur, semper in deteriora dilabitur.
chunks.english: For the mind is always driven by the weight of its changeability to something other than what it is. And unless it is held in its state by the strict discipline of custody, it always slips into worse things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quae enim semper stantem deseruit, statum quem habere potuit amisit. Ad vitam videlicet bonam dum alius in pueritia, alius in adolescentia, alius in juventute, alius in senectute, atque alius in decrepita aetate perducitur, quasi diversis horis operarii ad vineam vocantur.
chunks.english: For what always abandoned its standing has lost the stability it could have had. When one person is led to the good life in childhood, another in adolescence, another in youth, another in old age, and yet another in decrepit age, they are called as workers to the vineyard, as it were, at different hours.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, In Evangelium I, Homily 19, n. 1

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sancta Ecclesia ab Abel justo usque ad ultimum electum, qui in fine mundi nasciturus est, quot sanctos protulit, quasi tot palmites misit.
chunks.english: From righteous Abel to the last chosen one who will be born at the end of the world, the holy Church has sent forth as many branches, so to speak, as it has brought forth saints.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Evangelicus paterfamilias ad excolendam vineam suam mane, hora tertia, sexta, nona, et undecima operarios conducit; quia a mundi hujus initio usque in finem ad erudiendam plebem fidelium praedicatores congregare non desistit.
chunks.english: The gospel's master of the household hires workers to cultivate his vineyard at morning, at the third hour, at the sixth, at the ninth, and at the eleventh hour—because from the beginning of this world until its end, he does not cease to gather preachers to instruct the people of the faithful.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mane etenim mundi fuit cum sequenti hora ab Adam usque ad Noe. Hora vero tertia a Noe usque Ad Abraham. Sexta quoque ab Abraham usque ad Moysem. Nona autem a Moyse usque ad adventum Domini. Undecima vero ab adventu Domini usque ad finem mundi.
chunks.english: Morning of the world was with the following hour from Adam to Noah. The third hour was from Noah to Abraham. The sixth from Abraham to Moses. The ninth from Moses to the coming of the Lord. And the eleventh from the coming of the Lord to the end of the world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In qua praedicatores sancti apostoli missi sunt, qui mercedem plenam tarde venientes acceperunt. Ad erudiendam ergo Dominus plebem suam, quasi ad excolendam vineam suam, nullo tempore destitit operarios mittere; quia et prius per Patres, et postmodum per legis doctores et prophetas, et ad extremum per apostolos plebis suae mores excoluit, et quasi per operarios in vineae cultura magnopere laboravit.
chunks.english: In this hour, the holy apostles were sent as preachers, who received full wages though coming late. Therefore the Lord, to instruct his people—as if to cultivate his vineyard—never ceased at any time to send workers. For both earlier through the Fathers, and afterward through teachers of the law and prophets, and finally through the apostles, he cultivated the customs of his people and labored greatly, as through workers, in the cultivation of his vineyard.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Brevity and Misery of Present Life
chunks.latin: CAPUT V. De brevitate vel miseria vitae praesentis.
chunks.english: Chapter Five: The Brevity and Misery of Present Life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid sunt nationes in mundo nisi quidam flores in campo? Tendamus oculos cordis in hac latitudine mundi praesentis, et ecce quasi tot floribus, quot hominibus plenus est.
chunks.english: What are the nations in the world but certain flowers in a field? Let us direct the eyes of our heart toward this breadth of the present world, and behold, it is full of as many flowers, so to speak, as there are human beings.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XI, 50, n. 67-68

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vita itaque in carne, flos in feno est. Unde bene per Psalmistam dicitur: Homo sicut fenum dies ejus: et sicut flos agri ita florebit (Ps. CII, 15). Isaias quoque ait: Omnis caro fenum, et omnis gloria ejus sicut flos agri (Isa. XL, 6).
chunks.english: Life in the flesh is therefore a flower in grass. Hence it is well said by the Psalmist in the Psalms, chapter one hundred two, verse fifteen: A person's days are like grass, and he will flower like the flower of the field. Isaiah also says in chapter forty, verse six: All flesh is grass, and all its glory is like the flower of the field.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Homo etenim more floris procedit ex occulto, et subito apparet in publico: qui statim ex publico per mortem retrahitur ad occultum. Carnis nos viriditas ostendit, sed ariditas pulveris ab aspectibus retrahit.
chunks.english: For a human being proceeds from the hidden like a flower and suddenly appears in public—but is immediately withdrawn from public through death back to the hidden. The greenness of flesh displays us, but the dryness of dust withdraws us from sight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quasi flos apparuimus qui non eramus, quasi flos arescimus qui temporaliter apparebamus. Et quia per momenta homo quotidie compellitur ad mortem, recte in libro beati Job dicitur: Et fugit velut umbra, et nunquam in eodem statu permanet (Job XIV, 2).
chunks.english: We appeared like a flower when we were not, we wither like a flower when we appeared temporarily. And because moment by moment a human being is daily driven toward death, it is rightly said in the book of blessed Job, chapter fourteen, verse two: And he flees like a shadow, and never remains in the same state.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Homo hic vivendi vires ad modicum accepit, ut in perpetuum transeat, ubi ejus vitam terminus non concludat; sed in hac brevitate ubi roboratus est, colligit unde in perpetuitate inveniat, vel ut semper gaudeat, vel ut suscepta supplicia non evadat.
chunks.english: A human being here has received strength for living only briefly, in order to pass into perpetuity where no limit closes off his life. But in this brevity where he is strengthened, he gathers what he will find in perpetuity—either to rejoice forever or to not escape the punishments he has received.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XII, 19, n. 24

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omne quod transit breve est, etiam si tardius terminari videatur. In mortis autem semita, per quam non revertimur, ambulamus; non quod ad vitam carnis minime resurgendo reducimur, sed quod ad labores hujus vitae mortalis, vel ad conquirenda laboris praemia iterum non venimus.
chunks.english: Everything that passes is brief, even if it seems to end more slowly. We walk on the path of death through which we do not return—not that we will not be brought back to life in the flesh by rising again, but that we will not come again to the labors of this mortal life or to acquire the rewards of labor.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XIII, 27, n. 31

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui considerat qualis erit in morte, semper fit timidus in operatione, atque unde in oculis suis jam quasi non vivit, inde veraciter in oculis sui Conditoris vivat.
chunks.english: The person who considers what he will be like in death always becomes timid in action—and precisely because in his own eyes he already lives as if he were not living, in the eyes of his Creator he truly lives.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XIII, 29, n. 33

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nil quod transeat appetit, cunctis praesentis vitae desideriis contradicit; et pene mortuum se considerat, quia moriturum minime ignorat. Perfecta vita est mortis imitatio, quam dum justi sollicite peragunt, culparum laqueos evadunt.
chunks.english: He desires nothing that passes away, he contradicts all desires of present life. And he considers himself almost dead, because he by no means forgets that he is going to die. Perfect life is the imitation of death, which when the just carefully perform, they escape the snares of faults.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde scriptum est: In omnibus operibus tuis memorare novissima tua, et in aeternum non peccabis (Eccli. VII, 40).
chunks.english: Hence it is written in the book of Sirach, chapter seven, verse forty: In all your works remember your last things, and you will never sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quamlibet longum fuerit tempus vitae praesentis, eo ipso breve est quo permanens non est. Neque enim dignum est ut diutinum judicetur quidquid fine circumscribitur.
chunks.english: However long the time of present life may be, it is brief precisely because it is not lasting. For it is not worthy to be judged long-lasting when it is limited by an end.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Beatus Job, humanae conditionis miseriam considerans, ait: Homo natus de muliere, brevi vivens tempore, repletur multis miseriis (Job XIV, 1).
chunks.english: Blessed Job, considering the misery of the human condition, says in chapter fourteen, verse one: A human being born of woman, living a brief time, is filled with many miseries.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XI, 49, n. 66

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce sancti viri vocibus poena hominibus breviter est expressa, quia et angustatur ad vitam et dilatatur ad miseriam. Si enim subtiliter consideretur omne quod hic agitur, poena et miseria est.
chunks.english: Behold, by the voices of holy men the suffering of human beings is briefly expressed: because it is narrowed toward life and expanded toward misery. For if we consider carefully, everything that is done here is suffering and misery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ipsi etenim corruptioni carnis servire ad necesaria atque concessa, miseria est: ut contra frigus vestimenta, contra famem alimenta, contra aestum frigora requirantur.
chunks.english: For to serve the very corruption of the flesh for necessities and what is allowed is misery: that against cold garments must be sought, against hunger food, against heat coolness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Multa cautela custoditur salus corporis, sed plerumque etiam custodita amittitur, amissa cum gravi labore reparatur; et tamen reparata semper in dubio permanet: quid hoc aliud quam mortalis vitae miseria est?
chunks.english: The health of the body is guarded with much caution, but often even when guarded it is lost; when lost it is restored with heavy labor; and yet when restored it always remains in doubt. What is this other than the misery of mortal life?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Amamus amicos, qui suspectione offendi valeant; formidamus inimicos, atque securi de eis non sumus utique quos formidamus.
chunks.english: We love friends who can be offended by suspicion. We fear enemies, and we are certainly not secure about those we fear.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque inimicis sic fidenter quasi amicis loquimur; et nonnunquam pura verba proximorum, et multum nos fortasse diligentium, quasi verba suscipimus inimicorum; et qui falli nunquam vel fallere volumus, ex cautela nostra gravius erramus.
chunks.english: Often we speak confidently to enemies as if to friends. And sometimes we receive the pure words of neighbors, and perhaps those who love us much, as if they were the words of enemies. And we who never want to be deceived or to deceive, through our caution we err more gravely.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Humanae vitae miseria est, quod amissa coelesti patria repulsus homo delectatur exsilio, gravatur curis, et tamen cogitare dissimulat quam grave sit quia multa cogitantur; quod privatus est interno lumine, et tamen in hac vita diu vult perpeti caecitatem suam; quid hoc aliud quam de poena nostra nata miseria est?
chunks.english: The misery of human life is this: that having lost the heavenly homeland, the rejected human being delights in exile, is weighed down by cares, and yet pretends not to think how heavy it is because many things are being thought. That he is deprived of internal light, and yet wants to endure his blindness for a long time in this life—what is this other than misery born from our punishment?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed quamvis diu hic stare desideret, ipso tamen cursu vitae mortalis impellitur ut egrediatur.
chunks.english: But although he desires to remain here for a long time, he is nevertheless driven by the very course of mortal life to depart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pauca sunt bona omnia praesentis vitae, quamlibet multa videantur; sed tunc fidelis servus super multa constituitur, quando, devicta omni corruptionis molestia, de aeternis gaudiis in illa coelesti sede gloriatur.
chunks.english: All the goods of present life are few, however many they may seem. But then the faithful servant is set over many things, when, having conquered all the troubles of corruption, he glories in eternal joys in that heavenly dwelling.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, In Evangelium I, Homily 9, n. 2

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc servus ad Domini sui gaudium perfecte intromittitur, quando in aeterna illa patria assumptus, atque angelorum coetibus admixtus, sic interius gaudet de munere, ut non sit jam quod exterius doleat de corruptione.
chunks.english: Then the servant is perfectly brought into his Lord's joy when, taken up into that eternal homeland and mingled with the companies of angels, he rejoices so inwardly from the gift that there is no longer anything outwardly to grieve him about corruption.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Iniquorum gloria, cum plerumque in annorum multitudinem tenditur, ab infirmorum mentibus esse longa et quasi stabilis aestimatur. Sed cum repentinus hanc finis intercipit, brevem procul dubio fuisse redarguit; quoniam determinans innotescit quia quod praeterire potuit modicum fuit.
chunks.english: The glory of the wicked, when it often extends over a multitude of years, is estimated by weak minds to be long and almost stable. But when sudden death interrupts it, it is proven without doubt to have been brief—because when it ends it becomes known that what could pass away was small.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XVII, 7, n. 10

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT VI. De juventute ac senectute.
chunks.english: Chapter Six: Youth and Old Age.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In juventute hominis viget corpus, forte et incolume manet pectus, torosa cervix, plena sunt brachia; inanis autem senilibus statura curvatur, cervix exsiccata deponitur, frequentibus suspiriis pectus urgetur, virtus deficit, loquentis verba anhelitus intercidit; nam si languor desit, plerumque senibus ipsa sua salus aegritudo est.
chunks.english: In the youth of a human being the body flourishes, the chest remains strong and uninjured, the neck is muscular, the arms are full. But in the elderly the empty frame is bent, the dried neck droops, the chest is pressed with frequent sighs, strength fails, breathing interrupts the words of the speaker. For if illness is absent, often for the elderly their very health is sickness.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, In Evangelium I, Homily 1, n. 5

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mundus in annis prioribus velut in juventute viguit, ad propagandam humani generis prolem robustus fuit, salute corporum viridis, opulentia rerum pinguis; at nunc ipsa sua senectute deprimitur, et quasi ad vicinam mortem molestiis crescentibus urgetur.
chunks.english: The world in earlier years flourished as if in youth, was robust for propagating the offspring of the human race, green with the health of bodies, fat with the abundance of things. But now it is pressed down by its own old age, and as if toward approaching death it is pressed by growing troubles.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nolite diligere mundum senescentem, quem videtis diu stare non posse. Praecepta apostolica in animo ponite, quibus nos admonet, dicens: Nolite diligere mundum, neque ea quae in mundo sunt: quia si quis diligit mundum, non est charitas Patris in eo (I Joan. II, 15)
chunks.english: Do not love the aging world, which you see cannot stand for long. Place the apostolic precepts in your mind, by which he warns us, saying in the first letter of John, chapter two, verse fifteen: Do not love the world, nor the things that are in the world—for if anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT VII. De conjugatis.
chunks.english: Chapter Seven: The Married.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In usu mortalitatis quaedam ex semetipsis sunt noxia, quaedam vero ex his quae circa ipsa versantur. Ex semetipsis quaedam sunt noxia, sicut peccata atque flagitia; quaedam vero nonnunquam nobis ex his quae circa ipsa sunt nocent, sicut temporalis potentia, vel copula conjugalis.
chunks.english: In the practice of mortality, some things are harmful in themselves, but others are harmful from the things that revolve around them. Some things are harmful in themselves, such as sins and crimes. But some things sometimes harm us from what surrounds them, such as temporal power or the marriage bond.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XXVI, 26, n. 44

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bonum est conjugium, sed mala sunt quae circa illud ex hujus mundi cura succrescunt. Unde Paulus apostolus ait: Qui autem cum uxore est, cogitat quae sunt mundi, quomodo placeat uxori (I Cor. VII, 33).
chunks.english: Marriage is good, but the things that grow around it from the care of this world are bad. Hence the apostle Paul says in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter seven, verse thirty-three: But the one who is with a wife thinks about the things of the world, how to please his wife.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quibusdam meliora persuadens, idem Paulus apostolus eos a conjugio revocat, dicens: Hoc autem dico, non ut laqueum injiciam vobis, sed ad id quod honestum est, et quod facultatem praebeat sine impedimento Dominum obsecrandi (Ibid., 35).
chunks.english: Persuading certain ones to better things, the same apostle Paul calls them back from marriage, saying in verse thirty-five: But I say this not to cast a snare upon you, but for what is honorable and what provides the ability to beseech the Lord without impediment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum ergo tenetur quod non nocet, ex rebus juxta positis committitur plerumque quod nocet. Saepe rectum mundumque iter pergimus, et tamen ortis juxta viam vepribus per vestimenta retinemur. In via quidem munda non offendimus, sed a latere nascitur quo pungamur.
chunks.english: Therefore while what does not harm is held, from things placed nearby what does harm is often committed. Often we proceed on a straight and clean path, yet we are held back by our garments on thorns that have grown beside the road. We do not stumble on the clean road itself, but from the side there arises what pricks us.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quasi in monte sunt constituti qui carnali quidem copulae inhaerent, sed tamen extra suscipiendae prolis admixtionem debitam nulla carnis voluptate solvuntur.
chunks.english: Those who cling to carnal union are, as it were, stationed on a mountain, but who apart from the due mingling for receiving offspring are not released by any pleasure of the flesh.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Pastoral Rule III, 27

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In monte quippe stare est fructum propaginis in carne non quaerere. In monte stare est carni carnaliter non adhaerere. Multi sunt qui scelera quidem carnis deserunt, nec tamen in conjugio positi, usus solummodo debiti jura conservant.
chunks.english: To stand on a mountain is not to seek the fruit of offspring in the flesh. To stand on a mountain is not to adhere to flesh carnally. Many are those who abandon the crimes of flesh, and yet being placed in marriage, they preserve only the rights of what is owed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Exiit quidem Sodomam Loth, sed tamen mox ad montana non pervenit, quia jam damnabilis vita relinquitur, sed adhuc celsitudo conjugalis continentiae subtiliter non tenetur.
chunks.english: Lot indeed went out from Sodom, but nevertheless did not immediately arrive at the mountains—because now the damnable life is abandoned, but the height of conjugal continence is not yet subtly maintained.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Est in medio Segor civitas, quae fugientem salvet infirmum; quia videlicet cum sibi per incontinentiam miscentur conjuges, et lapsus scelerum fugiunt, et tamen venia salvantur.
chunks.english: There is in the middle the city of Segor, which saves the weak one fleeing—because indeed when spouses mingle with each other through incontinence, they both flee the falls of crimes and yet are saved by pardon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quasi parvam quippe civitatem inveniunt, in qua ab ignibus defendantur; quia conjugalis haec vita, non quidem in virtutibus mira est, sed tamen a suppliciis secura.
chunks.english: They find, as it were, a small city in which they are defended from fires—because this conjugal life is not indeed marvelous in virtues, but nevertheless secure from punishments.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Conjugalis vitae continentiam Loth praefigurans, dum de Sodomis fugeret, ait ad angelum: Est civitas Segor, ad quam possum fugere, parva, et salvabor in ea. Nunquid non modica est, et vivet in ea anima mea (Gen. XIX, 20)?
chunks.english: Lot, prefiguring the continence of conjugal life, as he fled from Sodom, said to the angel as recorded in Genesis, chapter nineteen, verse twenty: There is the city of Segor, to which I can flee; it is small, and I will be saved in it. Is it not modest, and will not my soul live in it?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Juxta igitur dicitur, et tamen ad salutem tuta perhibetur, quia conjugalis vita nec a mundo longe divisa est, nec tamen a gaudio salutis aliena.
chunks.english: It is therefore called small, and yet it is attested as secure for salvation, because conjugal life is neither far divided from the world nor yet alien from the joy of salvation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc in actione carnalis copulae vitam suam conjuges quasi in parva civitate Segor custodiunt, quando pro se assiduis deprecationibus intercedunt.
chunks.english: Then in the action of carnal union, spouses guard their life as in the small city of Segor when they intercede for themselves with constant prayers.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Recte per angelum ad eumdem Loth dicitur: Ecce etiam in hoc suscepi preces tuas, ut non subvertam urbem pro qua locutus es (Ibid., 21); quia videlicet cum Deo deprecatio funditur, nequaquam talis conjugii vita damnatur.
chunks.english: Rightly it is said by the angel to the same Lot in verse twenty-one: Behold, even in this I have received your prayers, that I will not overthrow the city about which you have spoken—because indeed when prayer is poured out to God, such a life of marriage is by no means condemned.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De qua deprecatione Paulus admonet, dicens: Nolite fraudare invicem, nisi forte ex consensu ad tempus, ut vacetis orationi (I Cor. VII, 5).
chunks.english: Concerning this prayer Paul warns, saying in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter seven, verse five: Do not defraud one another, except perhaps by consent for a time, that you may be free for prayer.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Paulus apostolus, cum quosdam incontinenter vivere cognovisset, admonuit, dicens: Propter fornicationem autem unusquisque suam uxorem habeat, et unaquaeque suum virum habeat (Ibid., 2).
chunks.english: The apostle Paul, when he learned that some were living incontinently, admonished them saying in verse two: But because of fornication, let each man have his own wife, and let each woman have her own husband.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum uxor habenda non sit nisi liberorum procreandorum gratia, ne quis in fornicationis culpa laberetur, concessit conjugibus aliquid unde adhuc surgere ad meliora potuissent.
chunks.english: Since a wife should not be had except for the sake of begetting children, lest anyone slide into the fault of fornication, he granted to spouses something from which they could still rise to better things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem Paulus praedicator egregius de conjugibus dicit: Tribulationem carnis habebunt hujusmodi (I Cor. VII, 28).
chunks.english: The same Paul, outstanding preacher, says about spouses in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter seven, verse twenty-eight: Such will have tribulation in the flesh.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed tribulationem carnis hic pati possunt etiam qui spiritaliter vivunt: cur ergo inesse conjugibus carnis tribulatio quasi specialiter dicitur, quae etiam a vita spiritualium longe non est, nisi quod hi frequenter majores tribulationes ex carne suscipiunt qui carnis voluptatibus delectantur?
chunks.english: But those who live spiritually can also suffer tribulation of the flesh here. Why then is tribulation of the flesh said to be in spouses almost specially, when it is not far even from the life of spiritual people, except that those who delight in pleasures of the flesh more frequently receive greater tribulations from the flesh?
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia XII, 22, n. 27

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nos qui pastoralis officii curam gerimus, cum vagantem quempiam et lubricum videmus, admonendus est ut conjugio frenare studeat iniquitatem suam, quatenus per hoc quod liceat discat superare quod non licet.
chunks.english: We who carry the care of pastoral office, when we see someone wandering and slippery, he should be admonished to strive to bridle his iniquity by marriage, so that through what is permitted he may learn to overcome what is not permitted.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum vero conjugatum videmus, admonendus est ut sic exerceat curam saeculi ne postponat amorem Dei; sic placeat voluptati conjugis, ut non displiceat Conditori.
chunks.english: But when we see one who is married, he should be admonished to exercise care for the world in such a way that he does not postpone the love of God—to please the pleasure of his spouse in such a way that he does not displease his Creator.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT VIII. De virginibus et continentibus.
chunks.english: Chapter Eight: Virgins and the Continent.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Peccata carnis ignorantes tanto sollicitius praecipitem ruinam metuant, quanto altius stant. Noverint itaque quia quo magis loco prominenti consistunt, eo crebrioribus sagittis insidiatoris impetuntur, qui tanto ardentius solet erigi, quanto se robustius conspicit vinci; tantoque intolerabilius dedignatur vinci, quanto contra se videt per integra infirmae carnis castra pugnari.
chunks.english: Those ignorant of the sins of the flesh should fear headlong ruin all the more carefully the higher they stand. Let them know therefore that the more they stand in a prominent place, the more they are assailed by the more frequent arrows of the insidiator, who is usually aroused all the more ardently the more robustly he sees himself conquered; and he disdains to be conquered all the more intolerably because he sees the intact camps of weak flesh fighting against him.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Pastoral Rule III, 28

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt castimoniam servantes ut incessanter praemia suspiciant, et libenter procul dubio tentationum quas tolerant labores calcent. Si enim attendatur felicitas, quae sine transitu attingitur, leve fit quod transeundo laboratur.
chunks.english: Those preserving chastity should be admonished to look unceasingly upon the rewards, and gladly without doubt to trample the labors of the temptations they endure. For if the happiness that is attained without passing away is considered, what is labored in passing becomes light.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Audiant peccata carnis ignorantes, quod per prophetam dicitur: Haec dicit Dominus eunuchis: Qui custodierint sabbata mea, et elegerint quae volui, et tenuerint foedus meum, dabo eis in domo mea, et in muris meis locum, et nomen melius a filiis et a filiabus (Isa. LVI, 4, 5).
chunks.english: Let those ignorant of the sins of the flesh hear what is said through the prophet in Isaiah, chapter fifty-six, verses four and five: Thus says the Lord to eunuchs: Who keep my sabbaths, and choose what I will, and hold my covenant, I will give them in my house and within my walls a place and a name better than sons and daughters.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Eunuchi quippe sunt qui, compressis motibus carnis, affectum in se pravi operis abscidunt. Quo autem apud Patrem loco habeantur ostenditur, quia in domo Patris, videlicet aeterna mansione, etiam filiis praeferuntur.
chunks.english: For eunuchs are those who, having compressed the movements of the flesh, cut off in themselves the affection for evil work. What place they hold with the Father is shown, because in the Father's house, that is, in the eternal dwelling, they are even preferred to sons.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Joannem in Apocalypsi dicitur: Hi sunt, qui cum mulieribus non sunt coinquinati: virgines enim sunt, qui sequuntur Agnum, quocunque ierit (Apoc. XIV, 4), et quod canticum cantant quod nemo possit dicere nisi illa centum quadraginta quatuor millia.
chunks.english: Through John in the book of Revelation, chapter fourteen, verse four, it is said: These are they who have not been defiled with women, for they are virgins, who follow the Lamb wherever he goes—and that they sing a song that no one can sing except those one hundred forty-four thousand.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Singulariter quippe canticum Agno cantare, est cum eo in perpetuum prae cunctis fidelibus etiam de carnis incorruptione gaudere; quod tamen electi caeteri canticum audire possunt, licet dicere nequeant; quia per charitatem quidem in illorum celsitudine laeti sunt, quamvis ad eorum praemia non assurgant.
chunks.english: For to sing a song singularly to the Lamb is to rejoice with him forever before all the faithful even in the incorruption of the flesh. Yet the other elect can hear this song, though they cannot sing it—because through charity they indeed rejoice in their height, although they do not rise to their rewards.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Audiant peccatorum carnis ignari, quod per semetipsam de hac integritate Veritas dicit: Non omnes capiunt verbum hoc (Matth. XIX, 11).
chunks.english: Let those ignorant of the sins of the flesh hear what Truth himself says about this integrity in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter nineteen, verse eleven: Not all receive this word.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod eo innotuit summum, quo denegavit omnium; et dum praedicit quia difficile capitur, audientibus innuit coeptum cum qua cautela teneatur.
chunks.english: This was made known to be supreme precisely by denying it to all; and while he predicts that it is received with difficulty, he hints to hearers with what caution what has been begun should be held.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt peccata carnis ignorantes, ut et praeeminere virginitatem conjugio sciant, et tamen se super conjuges non extollant; quatenus dum et virginitatem praeferunt, et se postponunt, et illud non deserant quod esse melius aestimant, et se custodiant quo se inaniter non exaltant.
chunks.english: Those ignorant of the sins of the flesh should be admonished both to know that virginity is preeminent over marriage, and yet not to exalt themselves above married people—so that while they prefer virginity and postpone themselves, they both do not abandon what they judge to be better, and they guard themselves so that they do not vainly exalt themselves.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt ut considerent quod plerumque actione saecularium vita confunditur continentium, cum et illi ultra habitum assumunt opera, et isti juxta ordinem proprium non excitant corda.
chunks.english: They should be admonished to consider that often the life of the continent is put to shame by the action of secular people, when both those assume works beyond their habit, and these do not stir up their hearts according to their proper order.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde bene per prophetam dicitur: Erubesce, Sidon, ait mare (Isa. XXIII, 4).
chunks.english: Hence it is well said through the prophet in Isaiah, chapter twenty-three, verse four: Be ashamed, Sidon, says the sea.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quasi enim per vocem maris ad verecundiam Sidon adducitur, quando per comparationem vitae saecularium atque in hoc mundo fluctuantium, ejus qui munitus et quasi stabilis cernitur vita reprobatur.
chunks.english: For Sidon is brought to shame, as it were, by the voice of the sea, when by comparison with the life of secular people and those fluctuating in this world, the life of one who is fortified and seems almost stable is reproved.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe nonnulli ad Dominum post carnis peccata redeuntes, tanto se ardentius in bonis operibus exhibent, quanto damnabiliores se de malis vident; et saepe quidam in carnis integritate perdurantes, cum minus se respiciunt habere quod defleant, plene sibi sufficere vitae suae innocentiam putant, atque ad fervorem spiritus nullis se ardoris stimulis inflammant.
chunks.english: Often some returning to the Lord after sins of the flesh show themselves all the more ardently in good works the more condemnable they see themselves in evil things. And often certain ones persevering in integrity of flesh, when they see themselves having less to weep over, think their life's innocence fully suffices for them, and they inflame themselves with no goads of ardor toward fervor of spirit.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fit plerumque Deo gratior amore ardens vita post culpam, quam securitate torpens innocentia.
chunks.english: Often a life burning with love after fault becomes more pleasing to God than an innocence torpid with security.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde et voce Judicis dicitur: Remittuntur ei peccata multa, quia dilexit multum (Luc. VII, 47), et: Gaudium erit in coelo super uno peccatore poenitente, quam super nonaginta et novem justis, quibus non opus est poenitentia (Luc. XV, 10).
chunks.english: Hence also by the voice of the Judge it is said in the Gospel of Luke, chapter seven, verse forty-seven: Her many sins are forgiven because she loved much. And in chapter fifteen, verse ten: There will be joy in heaven over one sinner doing penance more than over ninety-nine just persons who need no penance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod citius ex ipsa re colligimus, si nostrae mentis judicia pensemus. Plus namque terram diligimus quae post spinas exarata fructus uberes producit, quam quae nullas spinas habuit, sed tamen culta sterilem segetem gignit.
chunks.english: We gather this more quickly from the thing itself, if we weigh the judgments of our mind. For we love more the land that, plowed after thorns, produces abundant fruits, than that which had no thorns but nevertheless when cultivated produces a sterile harvest.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt peccata carnis ignorantes, ne superioris ordinis celsitudine se caeteris praeferant, cum ab inferioribus quanta se melius agantur, ignorant.
chunks.english: Those ignorant of the sins of the flesh should be admonished not to prefer themselves to others by the height of superior order, since they do not know how much better they are conducted by those who are inferior.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In examine namque recti Judicis mutat merita ordinum qualitas actionum. Quis consideratis ipsis rerum imaginibus, nesciat quod in natura gemmarum carbunculus praefertur hyacintho?
chunks.english: For in the examination of the righteous Judge, the quality of actions changes the merits of orders. Who, considering the very images of things, does not know that in the nature of gems the carbuncle is preferred to the hyacinth?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed tamen caerulei coloris hyacinthus praefertur pallenti carbunculo, quia et illi quod ordo naturae subtrahit species decoris adjungit, et hunc, quem naturalis ordo praetulerat, coloris qualitas foedat.
chunks.english: But nevertheless the hyacinth of blue color is preferred to the pale carbuncle, because both what the order of nature withdraws, the appearance of beauty adds to that one, and this one, whom the natural order had preferred, the quality of color defiles.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic ergo in humano genere quidam in meliori ordine deteriores sunt, quidam in deteriori meliores; quia et isti sortem extremi habitus bene vivendo transcendunt, et illi superioris loci meritum moribus non exsequendo diminuunt.
chunks.english: So therefore in the human race some are worse in a better order, some are better in a worse order—because both these transcend the lot of the lowest habit by living well, and those diminish the merit of a higher place by not following through in morals.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Candida vita carnis est sicut lilium de incorruptione virginitatis suave redolens, quasi flos lilii de fructu verae honestatis.
chunks.english: The white life of the flesh is like a lily giving off sweet fragrance from the incorruption of virginity, like the flower of a lily from the fruit of true honesty.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, In Ezechielem I, Homily 6, n. 4

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sunt plerique continentes qui ab exteriori se appetitu custodiunt, et spe ad interiora rapiuntur, carnem macerant et toto desiderio ad supernam patriam anhelant, aeterna praemia expetunt, pro laboribus suis recipere laudes humanas nolunt.
chunks.english: There are many continent persons who guard themselves from external appetite, and are seized by hope toward interior things, they macerate the flesh and pant with whole desire toward the heavenly homeland, they seek eternal rewards, they do not wish to receive human praises for their labors.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, In Evangelium I, Homily 12, n. 1

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hi nimirum gloriam suam non in honore hominum ponunt, sed intra conscientiam contegunt.
chunks.english: These assuredly do not place their glory in the honor of people, but they conceal it within their conscience.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per humilitatis custodiam servanda est munditia castitatis. Si enim pie spiritus sub Deo premitur, caro illicite super spiritu non levatur.
chunks.english: The purity of chastity must be preserved through the custody of humility. For if the spirit is piously pressed under God, the flesh is not unlawfully raised above the spirit.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Habet quippe spiritus commissum sibi dominium carnis, si tamen sub Domino recognoscit jura legitimae servitutis.
chunks.english: For the spirit has dominion over the flesh committed to it, if indeed under the Lord it recognizes the rights of legitimate servitude.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Those Recently Converted from the World
chunks.latin: CAPUT IX. De nuper conversis e saeculo.
chunks.english: Chapter Nine: Those Recently Converted from the World.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sunt nonnulli, qui mundi quidem actiones fugiunt, sed nullis virtutibus exercentur. Hi nimirum torpore non studio dormiunt, et idcirco interna non conspiciunt quia caput non in lapide, sed in terra posuerunt.
chunks.english: There are some who flee the actions of the world but are exercised in no virtues. These indeed sleep in torpor, not in zeal, and therefore they do not perceive internal things because they have placed their head not on a stone but on the earth.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia V, 31, n. 55

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quibus plerumque contingit, ut quanto securius ab externis actionibus cessant, tanto latius immundae in se cogitationis strepitum per otium congerant.
chunks.english: To whom it often happens that the more securely they cease from external actions, the more widely they heap up in themselves the noise of unclean thought through idleness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Deuteronomio scriptum est: Non arabis in primogenito bovis, et non tondebis primogenita ovium (Deut. XV, 19).
chunks.english: In Deuteronomy, chapter fifteen, verse nineteen, it is written: You shall not plow with the firstborn of the ox, and you shall not shear the firstborn of the sheep.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid per hujus prohibitionis exemplum, nisi vitam bene inchoantium Moyses humanis occupationibus exerceri prohibuit? In primogenito quippe bovis arare est bonae conversationis primordia in exercitio publicae actionis ostendere.
chunks.english: What did Moses prohibit by the example of this prohibition, except that the life of those beginning well should be exercised in human occupations? For to plow with the firstborn of the ox is to show the beginnings of good conversion in the exercise of public action.
chunks.notes: Source: Gregory the Great, Moralia VIII, 47, n. 78-79

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ovium primogenita tondere est ab occultationis suae tegmine humanis oculis inchoantia bona nostra denudare. In primogenito bovis operari prohibemur, atque a primogenitis ovium detondendis compescimur; quia et si quid robustum incipimus, exercere hoc in aperto citius non debemus. Et cum vita nostra simplex quid atque innocuum inchoat, dignum est ut secreti sui velamina non relinquat, ne nudum hoc humanis oculis quasi subducto vellere ostendat.
chunks.english: To shear the firstborn of the sheep is to denude our beginning goods from the covering of their concealment to human eyes. We are forbidden to work with the firstborn of the ox, and we are restrained from shearing the firstborn of the sheep—because even if we begin something robust, we should not exercise it openly too quickly. And when our life begins something simple and innocent, it is fitting that it not abandon the veils of its secrecy, lest it show this naked to human eyes as if the fleece were removed.
chunks.notes: Translation note: Complex passage on staged spiritual development

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Contemplative Life of Just Men
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XIV.
De sanctis viris activae et contemplativae vitae deditis.
chunks.english: Chapter fourteen: Holy Men Devoted to Active and Contemplative Life

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Justi viri, quanto in coelestibus per contemplationis radium inhaerere desiderant, tanto in terra aedificari refugiunt, ubi se peregrini et hospites noverunt, Paulo attestante, qui ait: Nostra autem conversatio in coelis est (Philip. III, 20). Et iterum: Vos autem aedificamini domum non manufactam aeternam in coelis (II Cor. V, 1).
chunks.english: The more just men desire to cling to heavenly things through the ray of contemplation, the more they refuse to build on earth, where they know themselves to be pilgrims and strangers. Paul attests to this when he says in his letter to the Philippians, chapter three, verse twenty: Our citizenship is in heaven. And again, in his second letter to the Corinthians, chapter five, verse one: You are being built into a house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum sancti viri in propriis gaudere desiderant, esse in alieno felices recusant. Injusti autem quanto longius ab aeternae patriae haereditate divisi sunt, tanto in terra altius fundamenta cogitationis figunt.
chunks.english: When holy men desire to rejoice in what is truly their own, they refuse to be happy in what belongs to another. But the unjust, the further they are divided from the inheritance of their eternal homeland, the deeper they plant the foundations of their thoughts in earthly soil.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Electorum populus per quosdam etiam in contemplationem surgit, per quosdam vero in activae vitae solummodo opera pinguescit. Unde recte per Isaac dicitur: Det tibi Dominus de rore coeli et de pinguedine terrae (Gen. XXVII, 28). Ros enim desuper subtiliter cadit; et toties de rore coelis accipimus, quoties per infusionem contemplationis intimae de supernis aliquid tenuiter videmus.
chunks.english: The people of the elect rise through some into contemplation, but through others grow strong only in the works of the active life. Therefore it is rightly said through Isaac in the book of Genesis, chapter twenty-seven, verse twenty-eight: May the Lord give you of the dew of heaven and of the fatness of the earth. For dew falls subtly from above, and we receive from the dew of heaven as often as we see something thinly of the things above through the infusion of intimate contemplation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum vero bona opera etiam per corpus agimus, de terrae pinguedine ditamur. Jacob patriarcha in itinere dormiens, a terra usque in coelum scalam porrectam vidit, angelos quoque ascendentes et descendentes per eam, et Dominum innixum scalae (Gen. XXVIII, 11).
chunks.english: But when we do good works even through the body, we are enriched by the fatness of the earth. The patriarch Jacob, sleeping on his journey, saw a ladder extended from earth to heaven, and angels ascending and descending on it, and the Lord leaning upon the ladder, as recorded in Genesis chapter twenty-eight, verse eleven.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In itinere dormire est in hoc praesentis vitae transitu a rerum temporalium amore quiescere, in dierum labentium cursu ab appetitu visibilium mentis oculos claudere. Angelos vero ascendentes et descendentes cernere est cives supernae patriae contemplari, vel quanto amore auctori suo super semetipsos inhaereant, vel quanta compassione charitatis nostris infirmitatibus condescendant.
chunks.english: To sleep on a journey is to rest from the love of temporal things in this passage of present life, to close the eyes of the mind from desire for visible things in the course of fleeting days. To see angels ascending and descending is to contemplate the citizens of the heavenly homeland, both with what love they cling to their creator above themselves, and with what compassion of charity they condescend to our weaknesses.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Notandum valde est quod ille dormiens angelos conspicit qui in lapide caput ponit: quia nimirum ipse ab exterioribus operibus cessans, interna penetrat qui intenta mente, quae principale est hominis, imitationem sui Redemptoris observat. Caput in lapide ponere est mente Christo inhaerere.
chunks.english: It should be especially noted that he who places his head on a stone while sleeping beholds angels, because he who ceases from external works penetrates inward things when, with focused mind, which is the principal part of man, he observes the imitation of his Redeemer. To place one's head on the stone is to cling to Christ with the mind.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui enim a praesentis vitae actione remoti sunt, sed ad superna nullo amore rapiuntur, dormire possunt, sed videre angelos nequeunt; quia caput in lapidem tenere contemnunt.
chunks.english: For those who are removed from the action of present life but are drawn to heavenly things by no love can sleep, but cannot see angels, because they scorn to hold their head upon the stone.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sancti viri ab importunitate desideriorum temporalium, a tumultu inutilium curarum, a clamore perstrepentium perturbationum, semetipsos sacri verbi gladio mortificare non desinunt, atque intus se ante Dei faciem in sinu mentis abscondunt. Unde recte per Psalmistam dicitur: Abscondes eos in abdito vultus tui a perturbatione hominum (Ps. XXX, 21).
chunks.english: Holy men do not cease to mortify themselves with the sword of the sacred word from the importunity of temporal desires, from the tumult of useless cares, from the clamor of resounding disturbances, and they hide themselves within, before the face of God, in the bosom of the mind. Therefore it is rightly said through the Psalmist in Psalm thirty, verse twenty-one: You will hide them in the secret of your face from the disturbance of men.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod quamvis perfecte postmodum fiat, etiam nunc ex magna parte agitur, cum a temporalium desideriorum tumultibus delectatione in interiora rapiuntur; ut mens eorum dum in amorem Dei tota tenditur, nulla inutili perturbatione laceretur.
chunks.english: Though this is done perfectly later, even now it is accomplished in great part when they are caught up by delight from the tumults of temporal desires into interior things, so that while their mind is entirely directed toward the love of God, it is torn by no useless disturbance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Paulus apostolus per contemplationem mentis mortuos, et quasi in sepulcro absconditos discipulos viderat, quibus dicebat: Mortui enim estis, et vita vestra abscondita est cum Christo in Deo (Coloss. III, 3, 4). Qui enim mortificare se appetit, valde ad inventam requiem contemplationis hilarescit; ut exstinctus mundo lateat, et a cunctis exteriorum rerum perturbationibus intra sinum se intimi amoris abscondat.
chunks.english: The apostle Paul through contemplation of mind had seen disciples dead and hidden as if in a tomb, to whom he said in his letter to the Colossians, chapter three, verses three and four: For you are dead, and your life is hidden with Christ in God. For he who desires to mortify himself rejoices greatly at the rest of contemplation he has found, so that, dead to the world, he may lie hidden and hide himself from all disturbances of external things within the bosom of intimate love.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: David sanctus aeternam patriam contemplatus, et haec mala pensans in quibus erat, atque illa bona considerans in quibus adhuc non erat, ait: Ego dixi in pavore meo: Projectus sum a vultu oculorum tuorum (Ps. XXX, 23). Sublevatus in exstasi, quod nostri interpretes pavorem non proprie vocaverunt, a vultu oculorum Dei vidit se esse projectum; quia omnis electus post interni luminis visionem, quae in ejus anima per contemplationis gratiam radio claritatis emicuit, ad semetipsum redit; et cognitione percepta, vel quibus illic bonis deesset, vel quibus malis hic adesset, invenit.
chunks.english: Holy David, contemplating the eternal homeland and weighing these evils in which he was, and considering those goods in which he was not yet, said in Psalm thirty, verse twenty-three: I said in my fear, I am cast away from the sight of your eyes. Lifted up in ecstasy, what our translators did not properly call fear, he saw himself cast away from the sight of God's eyes, because every elect person, after the vision of interior light which flashed in his soul through the grace of contemplation with a ray of brightness, returns to himself and, with knowledge received, discovers what goods he lacked there or what evils he had here.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Perfectam animam ista compunctio afficere familiarius solet, quia omnes imaginationes corporeas insolenter sibi obviantes decutit, et cordis oculum figere in ipso radio incircumscriptae lucis intendit.
chunks.english: This compunction usually affects the perfect soul more intimately, because it shakes off all bodily imaginations insolently opposing it, and strives to fix the eye of the heart on the very ray of unbounded light.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Has quippe figurarum corporalium species ad se intus ex infirmitate corporis traxit; sed perfecte compuncta hoc summopere invigilat, ne cum veritatem quaerit, eam imaginatio circumscriptae visionis illudat.
chunks.english: For it has drawn these forms of bodily figures inward to itself from the infirmity of the body, but when perfectly contrite, it watches most carefully lest, when it seeks truth, imagination of limited vision deceive it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquando mens justi ad quamdam inusitatam dulcedinem interni saporis admittitur, et raptim aliquo modo ardenti spiritu afflata renovatur: tantoque magis amat, quanto magis quod amet, degustat. Atque hoc intra se appetit quod sibi dulce sapere intrinsecus sensit: quia videlicet ejus amore dulcedinis sibi coram se viluit; et postquam hanc utcunque percipere potuit, quid sine illa dudum fuisset, invenit.
chunks.english: Sometimes the mind of the just is admitted to a certain unusual sweetness of interior savor, and suddenly in some way, inspired by an ardent spirit, it is renewed. And it loves all the more, the more it tastes what it loves. And it desires within itself what it has inwardly felt to taste sweet, because by love of this sweetness it has become worthless to itself before itself. And after it has been able to perceive this somehow, it discovers what it would have been long ago without it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unusquisque vir sanctus incircumscriptae veritati jam inhaerere conatur; sed ab ejus fortitudine sua adhuc infirmitate repellitur: et quia ejus munditiae contemperari non valet, flere dulce habet, sibique ad se cadenti infirmitatis suae lacrymas sternere.
chunks.english: Every holy man already strives to cling to unbounded truth, but he is still repelled from its strength by his own weakness. And because he cannot be conformed to its purity, he finds it sweet to weep and to lay down the tears of his weakness for himself as he falls back to himself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Neque enim potest mentis oculum in id quod intra se raptim conspexerat figere, quia ipso vetustatis suae usu deorsum ire compellitur. Inter haec anhelat, aestuat, super se ire conatur: sed ad familiares tenebras victa fatigatione relabitur.
chunks.english: For he cannot fix the eye of his mind on what he glimpsed suddenly within himself, because by the very habit of his old nature he is compelled to go downward. Amid these things he gasps, he burns, he strives to rise above himself, but he slides back to familiar darkness, overcome by weariness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene per Psalmistam dicitur: Ego dixi in pavore meo: Projectus sum a vultu oculorum tuorum (Psalm. XXX, 23). Contemplatus quippe interna gaudia visionis Dei, et socialem frequentiam angelorum persistentium, reduxit oculos ad ima: vidit quo jaceret, quia ad hoc conditus fuerat ut in coelestibus stare potuisset: et quod esset ingemuit.
chunks.english: Well is it said through the Psalmist in Psalm thirty, verse twenty-three: I said in my fear, I am cast away from the sight of your eyes. For having contemplated the interior joys of the vision of God and the social company of the abiding angels, he brought his eyes back to the lowest things. He saw where he lay, because he had been created for this, that he might stand in heavenly things. And he groaned at what he was.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Projectumque se a vultu oculorum Dei doluit, quia ima comparatione lucis intimae graviores sensit exsilii sui tenebras quas tolerabat.
