chunks.english: And he grieved that he was cast away from the sight of God's eyes, because by comparison with the lowest things to the intimate light, he felt more heavily the darkness of his exile which he endured.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XV.
De electis inter tumultus reproborum bene viventibus.
chunks.english: Chapter fifteen: The Elect Living Well Amid the Tumults of the Reprobate

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Querimur plerique cur non omnes boni sunt qui nobiscum vivunt. Mala proximorum ferre nolumus, omnes sanctos jam debere esse decernimus, dum esse nolumus quod ex proximis portemus.
chunks.english: Many of us complain why not all who live with us are good. We do not want to bear the evils of our neighbors, we decree that all should already be saints, while we do not want to be what we should bear from our neighbors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed hac in re luce clarius patet, quam multum adhuc ipsi de bono minus habeamus. Neque enim perfecte bonus est, nisi qui fuerit et cum malis bonus.
chunks.english: But in this matter it is clearer than light how much we ourselves still lack of goodness. For no one is perfectly good unless he has been good even among the wicked.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Beatus Job de semetipso asserit, dicens: Frater fui draconum, et socius struthionum (Job. XXX, 29). Hinc Paulus apostolus discipulis dicit: In medio nationis pravae et perversae, inter quos lucetis sicut luminaria in mundo (Phil. II, 15).
chunks.english: Blessed Job asserts of himself, saying in the book of Job, chapter thirty, verse twenty-nine: I was a brother of dragons and a companion of ostriches. Hence Paul the apostle says to his disciples in his letter to the Philippians, chapter two, verse fifteen: In the midst of a crooked and perverse generation, among whom you shine as lights in the world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc Petrus, pastor gregis dominici, ait: Justum Loth oppressum a nefandorum injuria conversatione eripuit. Aspectu enim et auditu justus erat habitans apud eos, qui de die in diem animam justi iniquis operibus cruciabant (II Petr. II, 7).
chunks.english: Hence Peter, shepherd of the Lord's flock, says in his second letter, chapter two, verse seven: He delivered righteous Lot, oppressed by the injury of the wicked in their conduct. For by what he saw and heard, that righteous man dwelling among them was tormented in his righteous soul day by day by their lawless deeds.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum de vita proximorum saepe querimur, mutare locum cupimus, conamur secretum vitae remotioris eligere, videlicet ignorantes quia si desit spiritus, non adjuvat locus. Loth denique Sodomis sanctus exstitit, sed in monte peccavit (Gen. XIX, 2 seq.).
chunks.english: When we often complain about the lives of our neighbors, we desire to change location, we strive to choose the secrecy of a more remote life, clearly ignorant that if the spirit is lacking, place does not help. For Lot was indeed holy in Sodom, but sinned on the mountain, as recorded in Genesis chapter nineteen, verse two and following.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quia autem loca mentem non muniunt, ipse humani generis primus testatur parens, qui et in paradiso graviter cecidit (Gen. III, 7). Sed minus sunt omnia quae loquimur ex terra. Nam si locus salvare potuisset, Satan de coelo non caderet.
chunks.english: But that places do not fortify the mind, the first parent of the human race himself testifies, who even in paradise fell gravely, as Genesis chapter three, verse seven records. But all that we speak from earth is less. For if place could have saved, Satan would not have fallen from heaven.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Psalmista David ubique in hoc mundo tentationes esse conspiciens, quaesivit locum quo fugeret, sed sine Deo non potuit munitum invenire. Ex qua re et ipsum sibi locum fieri petiit, propter quem locum quaesivit, dicens: Esto mihi in Deum protectorem et in locum munitum, ut salvum me faciat (Ps. XXX, 3).
chunks.english: The Psalmist David, seeing that temptations are everywhere in this world, sought a place to which he might flee, but without God he could not find a fortified one. For which reason he asked that God himself become a place for him, on account of which place he sought, saying in Psalm thirty, verse three: Be to me a God who protects and a fortified place, that you may make me safe.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Canticis canticorum scriptum est: Sicut lilium inter spinas, sic amica mea inter filias (Cat. II, 2). Ac si patenter dicat: Hi veraciter boni sunt qui in bonitate persistere etiam inter malos possunt.
chunks.english: In the Song of Songs chapter two, verse two, it is written: As a lily among thorns, so is my beloved among daughters. As if openly saying: These are truly good who can persist in goodness even among the wicked.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Non est valde laudabile bonum esse cum bonis, sed bonum esse cum malis. Sicut gravioris culpae est inter bonos bonum non esse, ita immensi est praeconii bonum etiam inter malos exstitisse.
chunks.english: It is not greatly praiseworthy to be good with the good, but to be good with the wicked. Just as it is a graver fault not to be good among the good, so it is of immense praise to have been good even among the wicked.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XVI.
De pressuris electorum.
chunks.english: Chapter sixteen: The Pressures of the Elect

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene viventi arcta censura non est ampla via, sed semita, in qua electus quisque studiose constringitur; quia sub praeceptorum custodia sollicite coangustatur.
chunks.english: For one living well, the strict standard is not a wide road but a narrow path, in which each elect person is carefully constrained, because he is anxiously narrowed under the custody of precepts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quasi quaedam angustia est itineris, in hoc quidem mundo vivere, sed de hujus mundi concupiscentia nil habere, aliena non appetere, propria non tenere, laudes mundi despicere, et pro Deo opprobria amare, gloriam fugere, despectumque appetere, adulantes despicere, despicientes honorare, mala nocentium ex corde dimittere, et erga eos dilectionis gratiam immobilem in corde retinere.
chunks.english: It is like a certain narrowness of journey to live indeed in this world but to have nothing of this world's desire, not to seek what belongs to others, not to hold what is one's own, to despise the praises of the world and to love reproaches for God's sake, to flee glory and seek contempt, to despise flatterers, to honor those who despise, to dismiss from the heart the evils of those who harm, and to retain toward them an immovable grace of love in the heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quae videlicet omnia semitae sunt, sed magnae, quia quanto in praesenti vita pro ipsa vivendi custodia angustae sunt, tanto amplius in aeterna retributione dilatantur.
chunks.english: All these are indeed narrow paths, but great ones, because the more narrow they are in present life for the very custody of living, the more widely they expand in eternal reward.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Reproborum nequitia triturae more electorum vitam, quasi grana a paleis separans premit et purgat. Mali enim bonos magis ab hujus mundi desideriis expediunt, dum affligunt; quia dum multa eis hic violenta ingerunt, festinare illos ad superna compellunt.
chunks.english: The wickedness of the reprobate, in the manner of threshing, presses and purifies the life of the elect, as if separating grain from chaff. For the wicked free the good more from the desires of this world while afflicting them, because while they inflict many violent things on them here, they compel them to hasten to heavenly things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene Israelitico populo Moyse vocante, et Pharaone rege saeviente signatum est (Exod. III, 7). Tunc namque Moyses ad vocandum eumdem populum missus est, cum jam Pharao duris operibus ad opprimendum fuerat excitatus; ut Israelitarum mentes Aegypto deformiter inhaerentes, alius dum vocaret quasi traheret, alius quasi impelleret dum saeviret: et plebs in servitio turpiter fixa, vel provocata bonis, vel malis impulsa moveretur.
chunks.english: It was well prefigured by the Israelite people, with Moses calling and Pharaoh the king raging, as recorded in Exodus chapter three, verse seven. For then Moses was sent to call that same people when Pharaoh had already been aroused to oppress with harsh works, so that the minds of the Israelites, clinging deformedly to Egypt, one might draw as he called, the other might push as he raged. And the people, fixed shamefully in servitude, would be moved either provoked by goods or impelled by evils.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Id. in hoc quotidie saeculo agitur, dum praedicatis coelestibus praemiis saevire in electos reprobi permittuntur: ut si ad promissionis terram vocati exire negligimus, pressuris saltem saevientibus impellamur: atque haec Aegyptus, videlicet vita praesens, quae nos oppressit blandiens, adjuvet premens; et quae dum fovet, servitutis jugo conterit, libertatis viam dum cruciat, ostentat.
chunks.english: This is done daily in this world, while the reprobate are permitted to rage against the elect when heavenly rewards are preached, so that if we, called to the land of promise, neglect to depart, we may at least be impelled by raging pressures. And this Egypt, namely present life, which oppressed us by flattering, helps by pressing. And what, while it cherishes, crushes with the yoke of servitude, while it torments, reveals the way of liberty.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nec sine causa est quod ab injustis justi sinantur affligi: ut scilicet dum futura audiunt bona, quae cupiant, patiantur etiam mala praesentia, quae perhorrescant, atque ad faciliorem exitum dum amor provocat, cruciatus impellat.
chunks.english: Nor is it without cause that the just are permitted to be afflicted by the unjust, so that while they hear of future goods which they may desire, they may also suffer present evils which they may dread, and toward an easier departure, while love provokes, torment may impel.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: David Psalmista ait: Tu mihi es refugium a pressura quae circumdedit me, exsultatio mea, redime me a circumdantibus me (Ps. XXXI, 7). Ecce quasi in nocte pressuram nominat, et tamen liberatorem suum inter angustias exsultationem vocat. Foris quidem nox erat in circumdatione pressurae, sed intus carmina resonabant de consolatione laetitiae.
chunks.english: The Psalmist David says in Psalm thirty-one, verse seven: You are my refuge from the pressure that surrounded me, my exultation, redeem me from those surrounding me. Behold, he names pressure as if in night, and yet calls his deliverer exultation amid distresses. Outside indeed was night in the encirclement of pressure, but within songs resounded from the consolation of joy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Electus quisque dum circumdari se pressuris narrat, et tamen Deum sibi esse exsultationem nominat, procul dubio quasi carmen in nocte cantat, ut ad subsequentis vitae diem felix perveniat.
chunks.english: Every elect person, while he narrates that he is surrounded by pressures and yet names God to be his exultation, without doubt sings as if a song in the night, that he may arrive happy at the day of the life to come.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XVII.
De electorum miraculis.
chunks.english: Chapter seventeen: The Miracles of the Elect

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sancta Ecclesia quotidie miracula spiritaliter facit, quod prisco tempore per sanctos apostolos corporaliter faciebat: sicut scriptum est: Signa autem eos qui credituri sunt, haec sequentur: in nomine meo daemonia ejicient, linguis loquentur novis, serpentes tollent; et si mortiferum quid biberint, non eis nocebit: super aegros manus imponent, et bene habebunt (Marc. XVI, 17).
chunks.english: The holy Church daily performs miracles spiritually which in ancient times it performed bodily through the holy apostles, as it is written in the Gospel of Mark, chapter sixteen, verse seventeen: These signs will follow those who believe: in my name they will cast out demons, they will speak in new tongues, they will take up serpents; and if they drink anything deadly, it will not harm them; they will lay hands on the sick, and they will recover.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sacerdotes Ecclesiae cum per exorcismi gratiam manum credentibus imponunt, et habitare malignos spiritus in eorum mente contradicunt, quid aliud faciunt nisi daemonia ejiciunt?
chunks.english: When the priests of the Church impose hands on believers through the grace of exorcism and forbid evil spirits to dwell in their minds, what else do they do but cast out demons?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fideles quique qui jam vitae veteris saecularia verba derelinquunt, sancta autem mysteria insonant, Conditoris sui laudes, et potentiam, quantum praevalent, narrant, quid aliud faciunt nisi novis linguis loquuntur?
chunks.english: All the faithful who now abandon the worldly words of the old life and resound holy mysteries, narrating the praises and power of their Creator as much as they prevail, what else do they do but speak in new tongues?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Electi igitur, dum bonis suis exhortationibus malitiam de alienis cordibus auferunt, serpentes tollunt. Plerique fidelium dum pestiferas suasiones audiunt, sed tamen ad operationem pravam minime pertrahuntur, mortiferum est quod bibunt, sed non eis nocebit.
chunks.english: Therefore the elect, when they remove malice from the hearts of others through their good exhortations, take up serpents. Many of the faithful, when they hear pestilent persuasions but are not at all drawn to evil action, drink what is deadly, but it will not harm them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fideles sanctae Ecclesiae quoties proximos suos in bono opere infirmari conspiciunt, et exemplo suae operationis illorum vitam roborant, quae in propria actione titubabat, quid aliud faciunt nisi super aegrotos manus imponunt, ut bene habeant?
chunks.english: As often as the faithful of the holy Church see their neighbors weakening in good work and strengthen their life by the example of their own action, which was faltering in its own action, what else do they do but lay hands on the sick so they may recover?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Miracula igitur quae operantur electi tanto majora sunt, quanto spiritalia; tanto mirabiliora sunt, quanto per haec non corpora sed animae suscitantur.
chunks.english: Therefore the miracles which the elect work are all the greater because they are spiritual; they are all the more wonderful because through these not bodies but souls are raised up.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Auctore Deo crebro fieri signa in Ecclesia cernimus; ex illis enim exterioribus signis obtineri sancta vita ab haec operanti non valet. Nam corporalia illa miracula ostendunt aliquando sanctitatem, non faciunt: spiritalia miracula, quae aguntur in mente, virtutem vitae non ostendunt, sed faciunt.
chunks.english: By God's authorship we see signs frequently occurring in the Church, yet from those exterior signs a holy life cannot be obtained by the one working them. For those bodily miracles sometimes show holiness, they do not make it. Spiritual miracles, which are done in the mind, do not show the virtue of life, but make it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illa habere et mali possunt, istis autem perfrui nisi boni non possunt. Exteriora miracula quae per apostolos fiebant, necessaria in exordio Ecclesiae fuerunt. Ut enim ad fidem cresceret miraculis fuerat nutrienda: quia et nos cum arbusta plantamus, tandiu eis aquam infundimus, quousque ea in terra jam convaluisse videamus.
chunks.english: Even the wicked can have those, but these can be enjoyed only by the good. The exterior miracles which were done through the apostles were necessary in the beginning of the Church. For so that faith might grow, it needed to be nourished by miracles, because even we, when we plant shrubs, pour water on them so long, until we see that they have already grown strong in the earth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: At si semel radicem fixerint, irrigatio cessabit. Hinc est enim quod Paulus dicit: Linguae in signum sunt non fidelibus, sed infidelibus (I Cor. XIV, 22). Dumque novas in illis virtutes aspiciunt, eorum quorum prius contempsere vitam postmodum obstupuere miracula.
chunks.english: But if they have once fixed their root, watering ceases. Hence it is that Paul says in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter fourteen, verse twenty-two: Tongues are a sign not for believers but for unbelievers. And when they see new powers in them, they later marvel at the miracles of those whose life they previously despised.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Infideles quique, visis apostolorum miraculis, mox pavidi ad sua corda redeuntes, extimuerunt sanctitatem in miraculis quam dexpexerant in praeceptis. Per infirma ergo confusa sunt fortia. Quia dum in veneratione vita surgit humilium, elatio cecidit superborum.
chunks.english: All unbelievers, seeing the miracles of the apostles, soon returning fearfully to their hearts, feared the holiness in miracles which they had despised in precepts. Therefore through weak things the strong were confounded. Because while the life of the humble rises in veneration, the pride of the arrogant fell.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In omne quod faciunt electi, idcirco semper ad laudem Creatoris redeunt, ut in ea virtute quam accipiunt vera stabilitate persistant. Nam si quid sibi tribuerent, in hoc quod acceperant stare non possent.
chunks.english: In everything the elect do, they therefore always return to the praise of the Creator, so that they may persist with true stability in that virtue which they receive. For if they attributed anything to themselves, they could not stand in what they had received.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene per quemdam sapientem dicitur: Ad locum de quo exeunt flumina revertuntur, ut iterum fluant (Eccle. I, 7). Sive enim in virtutibus, seu in doctrina quid aliud sunt sancti viri, quam flumina, quae terram cordis carnalium aridam rigant?
chunks.english: Well is it said by a certain wise man in Ecclesiastes, chapter one, verse seven: To the place from which rivers go out, they return, that they may flow again. For whether in virtues or in teaching, what else are holy men but rivers which water the dry earth of the carnal heart?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed sive in operatione quam faciunt, seu in doctrinae verbis citius siccarentur, nisi per intentionem cordis semper sollicite ad locum de quo exeunt redirent. Plerique electorum qui miraculis coruscant, si introrsus ad cor non redeant ac sese in amore Conditoris desideriorum vinculis non astringant; et manus ab eo quod agebat deficit, et lingua ab eo quod loquebatur arescit.
chunks.english: But whether in the work they do or in the words of teaching, they would more quickly dry up unless through the intention of heart they always carefully returned to the place from which they go out. Many of the elect who shine with miracles, if they do not return inward to the heart and bind themselves in the love of the Creator with the chains of desires, both the hand fails from what it was doing and the tongue dries up from what it was speaking.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad cordis sui intima semper electi per amorem Conditoris redeunt, et hoc quod in publico operantes atque loquentes fundunt, in secreto suo de fonte amoris hauriunt. Amando enim discunt quod docendo proferunt. Quasi ad locum ergo de quo exeunt flumina revertuntur, ut iterum fluant, quia aquam sapientiae inde semper hauriunt unde oritur, ne cum cucurrerit, exsiccetur.
chunks.english: The elect always return to the depths of their heart through love of the Creator, and what they pour out working and speaking in public, they draw in their secret place from the fountain of love. For by loving they learn what they bring forth by teaching. As if to the place from which rivers go out, they return that they may flow again, because they always draw the water of wisdom from where it springs, lest when it has run, it be dried up.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XVIII.
De reproborum miraculis.
chunks.english: Chapter eighteen: The Miracles of the Reprobate

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Exteriora signa vel miracula non solum electi, sed etiam reprobi facere possunt. Unde quibusdam Veritas dicit: Multi dicent mihi in illa die: Domine, Domine, nonne in tuo nomine prophetavimus, et in tuo nomine virtutes multas fecimus? Et tunc confitebor illis: quia nunquam novi vos, discedite a me qui operamini iniquitatem (Matth. VII, 22, 23).
chunks.english: Not only the elect but also the reprobate can perform exterior signs or miracles. Therefore the Truth says to some in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter seven, verses twenty-two and twenty-three: Many will say to me on that day, Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and do many mighty works in your name? And then I will declare to them, I never knew you; depart from me, you workers of iniquity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Perversus quisque, etiamsi rectam fidem in sinu universalis Ecclesiae tenere ac miraculis coruscare videatur, recta quidem sunt quae per fidem de Conditore intelligit, sed tamen quia fidei opera tenere contemnit, recte incredulitatis redarguitur, quia ab eo quod se ostendit credere, vivendo reprobatur.
chunks.english: Any perverse person, even if he seems to hold right faith in the bosom of the universal Church and to shine with miracles, right indeed are the things he understands about the Creator through faith, but nevertheless because he scorns to hold the works of faith, he is rightly convicted of unbelief, because he is rejected by living from what he shows himself to believe.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe haeretici mira signorum opera faciunt, ab obsessis corporibus spiritus pellunt, et per prophetiae donum ventura quaeque sciendo praeveniunt; sed tamen a largitore tot munerum cognitionis intentione divisi sunt, quia per ejus dona non ejus gloriam, sed proprios favores quaerunt.
chunks.english: Often heretics do wonderful works of signs, cast out spirits from possessed bodies, and by the gift of prophecy anticipate whatever is to come by knowing, but nevertheless they are divided from the giver of so many gifts by the intention of knowledge, because through his gifts they seek not his glory but their own favors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum reprobi quique per accepta bona in sua laude se elevant, ipsis muneribus contra largitorem pugnant. Inde quippe contra dantem superbiunt unde ei amplius humiles esse debuerunt. Sed eo postmodum eos districtior sententia percutit, quo nunc superna bonitas et ingratos largius infundit.
chunks.english: When any reprobate elevate themselves in their own praise through goods received, they fight with those very gifts against the giver. For they grow proud against the giver from that by which they should have been more humble to him. But a stricter sentence later strikes them by as much as heavenly goodness now more abundantly pours even on the ungrateful.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque fit reprobis amplitudo muneris incrementum damnationis, quia irrigati fructum non fecerunt, sed sub viriditatis colore vacui in altum crescunt.
chunks.english: Often for the reprobate the abundance of the gift becomes an increase of damnation, because though watered they have not made fruit, but under the color of greenness grow empty on high.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam haeretici signa ac miracula faciunt, sed ut hic praemia afflictionis suae abstinentiaeque recipiant, videlicet laudes humanas quaerant. Sed quia voce Domini reprobantur, dicentis: Discedite a me qui operamini iniquitatem (Matth. VII, 23), hac nimirum sententia datur intelligi ut in hominibus charitatis humilitas, non autem virtutum signa debeant venerari.
chunks.english: Sometimes heretics perform signs and miracles, but so that here they may receive rewards for their affliction and abstinence, namely they seek human praises. But because they are rejected by the voice of the Lord saying in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter seven, verse twenty-three: Depart from me, you workers of iniquity, by this sentence it is indeed given to be understood that in men the humility of charity, not the signs of powers, should be venerated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sancta Ecclesia etiam si qua fiant haereticorum miracula, despicit; quia haec sanctitatis speciem non esse cognoscit. Probatio quippe sanctitatis non est signa facere, sed unumquemque ut se diligere, de Deo autem vera, de proximo vero meliora quam de semetipso sentire.
chunks.english: The holy Church, even if certain miracles of heretics occur, despises them, because it knows these are not the appearance of holiness. For the proof of holiness is not to do signs, but for each person to love himself, to think truly about God, and to think better things about one's neighbor than about oneself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Non sunt amanda signa vel miracula, quae possunt cum reprobis haberi communia: sed charitatis atque pietatis miracula, quae tanto securiora sunt, quanto et occulta; et de quibus apud Deum eo major sit retributio, quo apud homines minor est gloria.
chunks.english: Signs or miracles are not to be loved which can be held in common with the reprobate, but the miracles of charity and piety, which are all the more secure as they are hidden, and about which there may be all the greater recompense before God, as the glory is less before men.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XIX.
De divinis charismatibus.
chunks.english: Chapter nineteen: Divine Gifts

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnipotens Deus interni judicii secreto moderamine alii sermonem sapientiae, alii plenam fidem, alii gratiam sanitatum, alii operationem virtutum, alii prophetiam, alii discretionem spirituum, alii genera linguarum, alii interpretationem sermonum tribuit; quatenus in uno eodemque spiritu iste verbo sapientiae polleat, nec tamen sermone scientiae, id est doctrinae, fulciatur; quia sentire atque invenire sufficit, etiam quod per discendi studium non apprehendit.
chunks.english: Almighty God, by the secret governance of his internal judgment, gives to one the word of wisdom, to another full faith, to another the grace of healings, to another the working of miracles, to another prophecy, to another discernment of spirits, to another kinds of tongues, to another interpretation of words, as recorded in Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter twelve, verses eight through ten. In such a way that in one and the same spirit this one excels in the word of wisdom, yet is not supported by the word of knowledge, that is of teaching, because it is enough to sense and find even what he does not grasp through the study of learning.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unusquisque dono gratiae praeventus sermone scientiae fulget, nec tamen in verbo sapientiae convalescit; quia et sufficit explere quantum didicit, et tamen ad sentiendum ex semetipso subtile aliquid non assurgit.
chunks.english: Each one anticipated by the gift of grace shines in the word of knowledge, yet does not grow strong in the word of wisdom, because it is enough to fulfill what he has learned, and yet he does not rise from himself to sense anything subtle.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Electus quisque per fidem elementis imperat; nec tamen per sanitatum gratiam infirmitates corporum curat. Ille vero orationis ope morbos subtrahit, nec tamen arenti terrae verbo pluvias reddit.
chunks.english: Each elect person commands the elements through faith, yet does not cure infirmities of bodies through the grace of healings. But that one takes away diseases by the help of prayer, yet does not restore rains by word to the parched earth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque vir sanctus operatione virtutum ad praesentem vitam etiam mortuos revocat; et tamen prophetiae gratiam non habens quae ventura sunt ignorat. Ille ventura quaeque velut praesentia attendit, et tamen in nulla signorum operatione se exerit.
chunks.english: Often a holy man by the working of miracles recalls even the dead to present life, and yet not having the grace of prophecy, he is ignorant of what is to come. That one observes all things to come as if present, and yet does not exert himself in any working of signs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam quisque electus per discretionem spirituum in factis subtiliter mentes conspicit; sed tamen diversi generis linguas nescit. Ille diversi generis linguas examinat, sed tamen in rebus similibus dissimilia corda non pensat.
chunks.english: Sometimes each elect person through discernment of spirits sees minds subtly in deeds, but nevertheless does not know tongues of diverse kinds. That one examines tongues of diverse kinds, but nevertheless in similar matters does not weigh dissimilar hearts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alius vero in una lingua, quam novit, sermonum pondera interpretando prudenter discutit; et tamen reliquis bonis, quae non habet, patienter caret.
chunks.english: But another prudently judges the weights of words by interpreting in one tongue which he knows, and yet patiently lacks the remaining goods which he does not have.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Creator noster ac dispositor sic cuncta moderatur, ut qui extolli poterat ex dono quod habet, humilietur ex virtute quam non habet; atque ita fit ut cum per impensam gratiam unumquemque sublevat, etiam per disparem alteri alterum subdat, et meliorem quisque dono alio eum qui sibi subjicitur attendat.
chunks.english: Our Creator and disposer so moderates all things that he who could be exalted by the gift he has is humbled by the virtue he does not have. And so it happens that when he lifts up each one through bestowed grace, he also subjects one to another through a different gift, and each one observes him who is subjected to himself as better by another gift.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ac licet se praeire ex aliis sentiat, eidem tamen quem superat se in aliis postponat. Omnipotens Deus sic cuncta moderatur ut dum singula quaeque sunt omnium, interposita quadam charitatis necessitudine, fiant omnia singulorum: et unusquisque sic quod non accepit in altero possideat, ut ipse alteri possidendum quod accepit humiliter impendat.
chunks.english: And though he feels himself to surpass by some things, he nevertheless places himself after the same one whom he surpasses in other things. Almighty God so moderates all things that while individual things belong to all, by a certain intervening necessity of charity, all things become the property of individuals. And each one so possesses in another what he did not receive, that he himself may humbly bestow on another for possessing what he received.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alia sunt dona quae nos muniunt, alia quae ornant: prophetia quippe, doctrina, genera linguarum, curationum virtus, quasi quaedam moenia mentis sunt; quae etsi quisque non habeat, stare munitus per fidem et justitiam potest.
chunks.english: There are some gifts which fortify us, others which adorn us. For prophecy, teaching, kinds of tongues, the power of healings are like certain walls of the mind. Even if anyone does not have these, he can stand fortified through faith and justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sancta Ecclesia superni sui capitis corpus est, in qua alius alta videndo oculus, alius recta operando manus, alius ad injuncta discurrendo pes, alius praeceptorum vocem intelligendo auris, alius malorum fetorem bonorumque fragrantiam discernendo naris est.
chunks.english: The holy Church is the body of its heavenly head, in which one is an eye by seeing high things, another a hand by doing right things, another a foot by running to assigned tasks, another an ear by understanding the voice of precepts, another a nose by discerning the stench of evils and the fragrance of goods.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui enim corporalium membrorum more dum vicissim sibi accepta officia impendunt, unum de semetipsis omnibus corpus reddunt; et cum diversa in charitate peragunt, diversum esse prohibent ubi continentur.
chunks.english: For in the manner of bodily members, while they mutually render to each other received offices, they make one body from all themselves. And when they perform diverse things in charity, they prevent being diverse where they are contained.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si autem unum quid cuncti agerent, corpus utique, quod ex multis continetur, non essent: quia videlicet multipliciter compactum, collectum non existeret, si hoc concors membrorum diversitas non teneret.
chunks.english: But if all were doing one thing, they would not be a body which is held together from many, because obviously it would not exist compactly joined together if this harmonious diversity of members did not hold it together.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum sanctis membris Ecclesiae virtutum dona Dominus dividit, quasi terrae mensuras ponit; sicut Paulus apostolus dicit: Unicuique sicut divisit Deus mensuram fidei (Rom. XII, 5). Et rursum: Ex quo totum corpus compactum et connexum per omnem juncturam subministrationis secundum operationem in mensuram uniuscujusque membri, augmentum corporis facit in aedificationem sui in charitate (Ephes. IV, 16).
chunks.english: When the Lord divides gifts of virtues to the holy members of the Church, he sets as it were measures to the earth, as the apostle Paul says in his letter to the Romans, chapter twelve, verse five: To each as God has divided a measure of faith. And again in his letter to the Ephesians, chapter four, verse sixteen: From whom the whole body, joined and held together by every supporting ligament, according to the working in measure of each individual member, makes the growth of the body for building itself up in love.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Miro consilio auctor ac dispositor noster huic largitur quae alii denegat, alii haec denegat quae isti largitur. Mensuras itaque sibi positas egredi nititur quisquis posse plusquam acceperit conatur.
chunks.english: By wonderful counsel our author and disposer bestows on this one what he denies to another, denies to another what he bestows on this one. Therefore whoever strives to be able to do more than he has received tries to go beyond the measures set for himself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unusquisque fidelium cui fortasse tantummodo datum est praeceptorum occulta disserere, si tentet etiam miraculis coruscare; aut quem supernae virtutis donum ad sola miracula roborat, etiam divinae legis pandere occulta contendat: in praecipiti pedem porrigit, qui mensurarum suarum limitem non attendit.
chunks.english: Each one of the faithful to whom perhaps it has been given only to expound the hidden things of precepts, if he attempts also to shine with miracles, or whom the gift of heavenly power strengthens only for miracles, if he also strives to unfold the hidden things of divine law, stretches out his foot into a precipice, he who does not heed the limit of his measures.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et plerumque amittit quod poterat, quia audacter ea ad quae pertingere non valet arripere festinat. Membrorum nostrorum tunc bene ministeriis utimur, cum sua eis officia distincte servamus. Lucem quippe oculis cernimus, vocem vero auribus audimus.
chunks.english: And often he loses what he could do, because he boldly hastens to seize those things to which he cannot attain. We use the ministries of our members well when we distinctly preserve their proper offices for them. For we discern light with eyes, but we hear voice with ears.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si quis autem, mutato ordine, voci oculos, luci aures accommodet, huic utraque incassum patent. Si quis odores velit ore discernere, sapores nare gustare, utriusque sensus sibi ministerium, quia pervertit, interimit: dum enim propriis haec usibus non aptantur, et sua officia deserunt, et ad extranea non assurgunt.
chunks.english: But if anyone, with the order changed, applies eyes to voice and ears to light, both lie open to him in vain. If anyone wishes to discern odors with the mouth, to taste flavors with the nose, he destroys for himself the ministry of both senses because he perverts it. For when these are not fitted to their proper uses, they both abandon their own offices and do not rise to foreign ones.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Egregius praedicator ait: Vos estis corpus Christi, et membra de membro (I Cor. XII, 27). Aliud quippe est membrum corporis, aliud membrum membri. Membrum quippe corporis pars ad totum; membrum vero membri est particula ad partem.
chunks.english: The outstanding preacher says in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter twelve, verse twenty-seven: You are the body of Christ and members of member. For it is one thing to be a member of the body, another to be a member of a member. A member of the body is a part to the whole, but a member of a member is a particle to the part.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Membrum namque membri est digitus ad manum, manus ad brachium; membrum vero est corporis totum hoc simul ad corpus universum: sicut et in spiritali dominico corpore membra de membro dicimus eos qui in ejus Ecclesia ab aliis reguntur.
chunks.english: For a member of a member is a finger to the hand, a hand to the arm. But a member of the body is all this together to the whole body, just as also in the spiritual body of the Lord we call members of member those who in his Church are governed by others.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XX.
De quatuor virtutibus, id est, prudentia, temperantia, fortitudine atque justitia.
chunks.english: Chapter twenty: The Four Virtues, That Is, Prudence, Temperance, Fortitude and Justice

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Solidum mentis nostrae aedificium prudentia, temperantia, fortitudo, atque justitia sustinet; quia in his quatuor virtutibus tota boni operis structura consurgit. Unde et quatuor paradisi flumina terram irrigant, quia dum his quatuor virtutibus cor infunditur, ab omnium desideriorum carnalium aestu temperatur.
chunks.english: The solid edifice of our mind is sustained by prudence, temperance, fortitude and justice, because in these four virtues the entire structure of good work rises up. Therefore the four rivers of paradise water the earth, because when the heart is infused with these four virtues, it is tempered from the heat of all carnal desires.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam dum menti ignavia subripit, prudentia frigescit; nam cum fessa torpet, ventura non providet. Nonnunquam dum nonnulla menti delectatio subripit, temperantia nostra marcescit: quantum enim ad delectationem praesentium ducimur, minus ab illicitis temperamus.
chunks.english: Sometimes when laziness creeps into the mind, prudence grows cold, for when weary it grows torpid, it does not foresee what is coming. Sometimes when some delight creeps into the mind, our temperance withers. For the more we are led to delight in present things, the less we restrain ourselves from illicit things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquando se timor cordi insinuat, et vires nostrae fortitudinis turbat: et eo minores contra adversa existimus, quo quaedam perdere immoderatius dilecta formidamus. Nonnunquam vero amor suus se menti ingerit, eamque latenti motu a rectitudine justitiae divertit; et quo se totam auctori reddere negligit, eo in se justitiae juri contradicit.
chunks.english: Sometimes fear insinuates itself into the heart and disturbs the strength of our fortitude. And we become all the less strong against adversities, the more we fear to lose certain things loved too immoderately. Sometimes, however, self-love thrusts itself into the mind and turns it by hidden movement from the rectitude of justice. And the more it neglects to render itself wholly to its author, the more it contradicts within itself the right of justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quos spiritus gratiae septiformis repleverit, perficit; eisque non solum Trinitatis notitiam, sed etiam virtutum quatuor, id est, prudentiae, temperantiae, fortitudinis atque justitiae, operationem praebet. Per Trinitatis notitiam quatuor virtutum actio accipitur, et per actionem virtutum quatuor usque ad manifestam Trinitatis speciem pervenitur.
chunks.english: Those whom the sevenfold spirit of grace has filled, he perfects, and to them he provides not only knowledge of the Trinity but also the operation of the four virtues, that is, prudence, temperance, fortitude and justice. Through knowledge of the Trinity the action of the four virtues is received, and through the action of the four virtues one arrives even to the manifest vision of the Trinity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Possumus per quatuor mundi partes principales quatuor virtutes accipere, ex quibus reliquae virtutes oriuntur: videlicet, prudentiam, fortitudinem, justitiam atque temperantiam. Quas nimirum virtutes tunc veraciter accipimus, cum earum ordinem custodimus.
chunks.english: We can understand by the four principal parts of the world the four virtues from which the remaining virtues arise: namely, prudence, fortitude, justice and temperance. These virtues we truly receive when we preserve their order.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Prima ex quatuor virtutibus prudentia, secunda fortitudo, tertia justitia, quarta temperantia est. Quid enim prodesse potest prudentia, si fortitudo desit? Scire etenim cuiquam quod non potest facere, poena magis quam virtus est.
chunks.english: First of the four virtues is prudence, second is fortitude, third is justice, fourth is temperance. For what can prudence profit if fortitude is lacking? For anyone to know what he cannot do is more punishment than virtue.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed qui prudenter intelligit quod agat, et fortiter agit quod intellexerit, jam procul dubio justus est; sed ejus justitiam temperantia sequi debet, quia plerumque justitia si modum non habet, in crudelitatem cadit.
chunks.english: But he who prudently understands what he does and fortly does what he has understood is without doubt already just. But temperance must follow his justice, because often justice, if it does not have moderation, falls into cruelty.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ipsa vero justitia vere justitia est quae se temperantiae freno moderatur, ut in zelo quo quisque fervet sit etiam temperatus; ne si plus ferveat perdat justitiam, cujus servare moderamina ignorat.
chunks.english: Justice itself is truly justice which moderates itself with the bridle of temperance, so that in the zeal in which anyone burns he may also be temperate, lest if he burns too much he lose justice, whose measures he does not know how to preserve.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XXI.
De vita activa et contemplativa.
chunks.english: Chapter twenty-one: Active and Contemplative Life

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Duae sunt sanctorum virorum vitae, activa scilicet et contemplativa; sed activa prior est tempore quam contemplativa, quia ex bono opere tenditur ad contemplationem. Contemplativa autem major est merito quam activa, quia haec in usu praesentis operis laborat, illa vero sapore intimo venturam jam requiem degustat.
chunks.english: There are two lives of holy men, namely active and contemplative. But active is prior in time to contemplative, because from good work one tends toward contemplation. Contemplative, however, is greater in merit than active, because this one labors in the use of present work, but that one already tastes the coming rest with interior savor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ezechiel propheta ait: Et manus hominis sub pennis eorum in quatuor partes (Ezech. I, 8). Quid itaque per manus nisi activa, et quid per pennas nisi contemplativa vita signatur? Manus ergo hominis sub pennis eorum est, id est, virtus operis sub volatu contemplationis.
chunks.english: The prophet Ezekiel says in chapter one, verse eight: And the hands of a man were under their wings in four directions. What therefore is signified by hands except active life, and what by wings except contemplative life? Therefore the hands of a man are under their wings, that is, the virtue of work under the flight of contemplation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Duae mulieres in Evangelio, Martha scilicet et Maria, activam et contemplativam designant. Martha etenim satagebat circa frequens ministerium, Maria autem sedebat ad pedes Domini, et verba audiebat ejus. Erat ergo una intenta operi, altera contemplationi. Una activae serviebat per exterius ministerium, altera contemplativae per suspensionem cordis in verbum.
chunks.english: Two women in the Gospel, namely Martha and Mary, designate active and contemplative life. For Martha was busy about much serving, but Mary sat at the Lord's feet and heard his words. Therefore one was intent on work, the other on contemplation. One served the active through exterior ministry, the other the contemplative through suspension of heart in the word.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quamvis activa vita bona sit, melior tamen est contemplativa; quia illa cum mortali vita deficit, ista vero in immortali vita plenius excrescit. Unde dicitur: Maria optimam partem elegit, quae non auferetur ab ea (Luc. X, 42).
chunks.english: Although the active life is good, the contemplative is better, because that one ceases with mortal life, but this one grows more fully in immortal life. Therefore it is said in the Gospel of Luke, chapter ten, verse forty-two: Mary has chosen the better part, which will not be taken away from her.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si per activam vitam boni aliquid agimus, ad coeleste tamen desiderium per contemplativam volamus. Unde et apud Moysen activa servitus, contemplativa autem libertas vocatur. Et cum utraeque vitae ex dono sint gratiae, quandiu tamen inter proximos vivimus una nobis in necessitate est, altera in voluntate.
chunks.english: If through the active life we do something good, yet we fly to heavenly desire through the contemplative. Therefore in Moses the active is called servitude, but the contemplative liberty. And though both lives are from the gift of grace, as long as we live among neighbors, one is for us in necessity, the other in will.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nemo mortalium cognoscens Deum, ad ejus regnum ingreditur, nisi bene prius operetur. Sine contemplativa ergo vita intrare possunt ad coelestem patriam, qui bona quae possunt operari non negligunt. Sine activa autem intrare non possunt, si negligunt operari bona quae possunt.
chunks.english: No mortal knowing God enters into his kingdom unless he first works well. Therefore without the contemplative life they can enter into the heavenly homeland who do not neglect to work the good things they can. But without the active they cannot enter if they neglect to work the good things they can.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illa ergo in necessitate, haec in voluntate est: illa in servitute, ista in libertate. Electorum populus per quosdam in contemplationem surgit, per quosdam vero in activae vitae solummodo opera pinguescit, sicut scriptum est: Det tibi Dominus de rore coeli, et de pinguedine terrae (Gen. XXVII, 28).
chunks.english: That one therefore is in necessity, this one in will. That one in servitude, this one in liberty. The people of the elect rise through some into contemplation, but through others grow strong only in the works of the active life, as it is written in Genesis chapter twenty-seven, verse twenty-eight: May the Lord give you of the dew of heaven and of the fatness of the earth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ros enim desuper subtiliter cadit; et toties de rore coeli accipimus, quoties per infusionem contemplationis intimae de supernis aliquid tenuiter videmus. Cum vero bona opera etiam per corpus agimus, terrae pinguedine ditamur.
chunks.english: For dew falls subtly from above, and we receive from the dew of heaven as often as we see something thinly of the things above through the infusion of intimate contemplation. But when we do good works even through the body, we are enriched by the fatness of the earth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Duae uxores Jacob, Lia videlicet et Rachel, activam et contemplativam vitam significant. Lia quippe interpretatur laboriosa, Rachel vero, ovis vel visum principium. Activa autem vita laboriosa est, quia desudat in opere; contemplativa vero simplex ad solum videndum principium anhelat, videlicet ipsum qui ait: Ego sum principium, propter quod et loquor vobis (Joan. VIII, 25).
chunks.english: The two wives of Jacob, namely Leah and Rachel, signify active and contemplative life. For Leah is interpreted as laborious, but Rachel as sheep or vision of the beginning. The active life is laborious because it sweats in work. But the contemplative, simple, longs only to see the beginning, namely he himself who says in the Gospel of John, chapter eight, verse twenty-five: I am the beginning, therefore I speak to you.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Beatus Jacob Rachel quidem concupierat, sed in nocte accepit Liam (Gen. XXIX, 16); quia videlicet omnis qui ad Deum convertitur contemplativam vitam desiderat, quietem aeternae patriae appetit; sed prius necesse est ut in nocte vitae praesentis operetur bona quae potest, desudet in labore, id est, Liam accipiat, ut post ad videndum principium in Rachel amplexibus requiescat.
chunks.english: Blessed Jacob indeed desired Rachel, but received Leah in the night, as Genesis chapter twenty-nine, verse sixteen records, because clearly everyone who is converted to God desires the contemplative life, seeks the rest of the eternal homeland. But first it is necessary that in the night of present life he work the good things he can, sweat in labor, that is, receive Leah, so that afterward he may rest in the embraces of Rachel to see the beginning.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Erat Rachel videns, sed sterilis, Lia autem lippis, sed fecunda. Rachel pulchra et infecunda, quia nimirum mens quae contemplandi otia appetit, plus videt, sed minus Deo filios generat. Cum vero se ad laborem praedicationis dirigit, minus videt, sed amplius parit.
chunks.english: Rachel was seeing but sterile, Leah however had weak eyes but was fruitful. Rachel beautiful and unfruitful, because certainly the mind that seeks the leisure of contemplating sees more, but generates fewer sons to God. But when it directs itself to the labor of preaching, it sees less but gives birth more.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Contemplativa vita valde speciosa est in animo, sed dum quiescere in silentio appetit, filios non generat ex praedicatione: videt, et non parit, quia quietis suae studio intenta minus se in aliorum collectione succendit.
chunks.english: The contemplative life is very beautiful in the soul, but while it seeks to rest in silence, it does not generate sons from preaching. It sees and does not give birth, because intent on the pursuit of its own quiet, it is less inflamed in the gathering of others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quantum introrsum conspicit, aperire aliis praedicando non sufficit. Lia uxor Jacob lippis et fecunda est, quia activa vita dum occupatur in opere, minus videt. Sed dum modo per verbum, modo per exemplum, ad imitationem suam proximos accendit, multos in bono opere filios generat; et si in contemplationem mentem tendere non valet, ex eo tamen quod agit exterius, gignere sequaces valet.
chunks.english: And as much as it sees inwardly, it is not sufficient to open to others by preaching. Leah, Jacob's wife, has weak eyes and is fruitful, because the active life, while occupied in work, sees less. But while sometimes by word, sometimes by example, it inflames neighbors to its own imitation, it generates many sons in good work. And if it cannot direct the mind into contemplation, nevertheless from what it does outwardly, it can beget followers.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Post Liae complexus ad uxorem Rachelem Jacob pervenit, quia perfectus quisque ante activae vitae ad fecunditatem jungitur, et postmodum contemplativae ad requiem copulatur.
chunks.english: After the embraces of Leah, Jacob comes to his wife Rachel, because every perfect person is first joined to the fruitfulness of the active life, and afterward united to the rest of the contemplative.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum est: Si emeris servum Hebraeum, sex annis serviet tibi (Exod. XXI, 2). Hebraeus enim transiens interpretatur. Et servus Hebraeus emitur, et sex annis servire praecipitur, quando unusquisque qui jam ab hoc saeculo mente transit, servitio omnipotentis Domini subditur.
chunks.english: It is written in Exodus chapter twenty-one, verse two: If you buy a Hebrew slave, he shall serve you six years. For Hebrew is interpreted as passing over. And a Hebrew slave is bought and commanded to serve six years when each one who already passes in mind from this world is subjected to the service of the almighty Lord.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ille enim vere servire Deo appetit qui ab hoc saeculo mente transire didicerit. Servus namque Hebraeus emitur, et sex annis servire praecipitur, ita ut in septimo liber exeat gratis. Quid enim per senarium numerum nisi activae vitae perfectio designatur? Quid per septenarium nisi contemplativa exprimitur?
chunks.english: For he truly desires to serve God who has learned to pass in mind from this world. For a Hebrew slave is bought and commanded to serve six years, so that in the seventh he may go out free without payment. For what is designated by the number six except the perfection of the active life? What is expressed by the number seven except the contemplative?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sex ergo annis servit, ex septimo egreditur liber, qui per activam, quam perfecte exhibuerit, ad contemplativae vitae libertatem transit. Servus Hebraeus emptus post sex annos gratis liber egreditur, quia hi qui postquam omnia fecerint dicunt se inutiles servos, eis procul dubio sicut ipsa activa fuit ex munere, ita erit ex gratia etiam contemplativa.
chunks.english: Therefore he serves six years and goes out free in the seventh, who through the active life, which he has perfectly shown, passes to the liberty of the contemplative life. A Hebrew slave bought goes out free without payment after six years, because those who after they have done all things say they are unprofitable servants, to them without doubt, just as the active itself was from gift, so also the contemplative will be from grace.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Isdem servus cum quali veste intraverit, cum tali exeat; quia omnino necesse est ut unusquisque nostrum in hoc quod incipit perseveret, atque usque ad finem operis in ea quam inchoavit intentione perduret. Ille quippe bene ad contemplativam transit, qui in activa vita intentionis suae vestem ad deteriora non mutaverit.
chunks.english: The same slave goes out with such garment as he entered with, because it is altogether necessary that each one of us persevere in what he begins, and endure to the end of the work in that intention which he began. For he passes well to the contemplative who has not changed the garment of his intention to worse things in the active life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum est in Apocalypsi: Factum est silentium in coelo quasi media hora (Apoc. VIII, 1). Coelum quippe est anima justi, sicut per prophetam Dominus dicit: Coelum mihi sedes est (Isa. LXVI, 1). Et: Coeli enarrant gloriam Dei (Ps. XVIII, 1).
chunks.english: It is written in the book of Revelation, chapter eight, verse one: There was silence in heaven for about half an hour. For heaven is the soul of the just, as the Lord says through the prophet in Isaiah chapter sixty-six, verse one: Heaven is my throne. And in Psalm eighteen, verse one: The heavens declare the glory of God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum ergo quies contemplativae vitae agitur in mente, silentium fit in coelo; quia terrenorum actuum strepitus quiescit a cogitatione, ut ad secretum intimum aurem animus apponat.
chunks.english: Therefore when the rest of the contemplative life is enacted in the mind, silence is made in heaven, because the noise of earthly actions rests from thought, so that the soul may place its ear to the intimate secret.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum quies mentis esse in hac vita perfecta non potest, nequaquam hora integra factum in coelo silentium dicitur, sed quasi media; ut neque ipsa media plene sentiatur, cum praemittitur quasi, quia mox, ut se animus sublevare coeperit, et quietis intimae lumine perfundi, redeunte citius cogitationum strepitu, de semetipso confunditur, et confusus caecatur.
chunks.english: Since rest of mind cannot be perfect in this life, silence in heaven is by no means said to be made for a whole hour, but as if for half. So that not even that half is fully felt, since as if is placed before it, because as soon as the soul begins to lift itself up and to be flooded with the light of intimate quiet, with the noise of thoughts returning more quickly, it is confused from itself, and confused, it is blinded.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magna est in contemplativa vita mentis contentio, cum sese ad coelestia erigit, cum in rebus spiritalibus animum tendit, cum transgredi nititur omne quod corporaliter videtur, cum sese angustat ut dilatetur.
chunks.english: Great is the striving of the mind in the contemplative life when it raises itself to heavenly things, when it directs the soul in spiritual matters, when it strives to pass beyond everything that is seen bodily, when it narrows itself that it may be widened.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et aliquando quidem vincit, et reluctantes tenebras suae caecitatis exsuperat; et de incircumscripto lumine quiddam furtim subtiliter attingit; sed statim ad semetipsam protinus reverberatur, atque ab ea luce, ad quam respirando transit, ad suae caecitatis tenebras suspirando redit.
chunks.english: And sometimes indeed it conquers and overcomes the resisting darkness of its blindness, and touches something subtly by stealth from the unbounded light. But immediately it is struck back to itself, and from that light to which it passes by breathing, it returns sighing to the darkness of its blindness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In libro Genesis scriptum est: Sepelivit Abraham conjugem suam in sepulcro duplici (Gen. XXIII, 19). Activa vita quasi sepulcrum est, quia a pravis operibus mortuos tegit; sed contemplativa perfectius sepelit, quia cunctis mundi actionibus funditus dividit.
chunks.english: In the book of Genesis, chapter twenty-three, verse nineteen, it is written: Abraham buried his wife in a double tomb. The active life is like a tomb because it covers the dead from evil works. But the contemplative buries more perfectly, because it utterly divides from all actions of the world.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XXII.
De oratione.
chunks.english: Chapter twenty-two: Prayer

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum Deus in oratione non quaeritur, citius animus in oratione lassatur. Quia cum illa quisque postulat quae fortasse juxta occultum judicium Deus tribuere recusat, ipse quoque venit in fastidium, qui non vult dare quod rogatur.
chunks.english: When God is not sought in prayer, the soul more quickly grows weary in prayer. Because when anyone asks for those things which perhaps according to hidden judgment God refuses to grant, he also comes into disgust who does not want to give what is asked.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Conditor universitatis Dominus se magis quam ea quae condidit vult amari, et aeterna potius quam terrena postulari, cum eadem Veritas dicat: Quaerite primum regnum Dei, et haec omnia adjicientur vobis (Matth. VI, 33).
chunks.english: The Lord Creator of the universe wants himself to be loved more than what he created, and eternal things to be sought rather than earthly ones, since the same Truth says in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter six, verse thirty-three: Seek first the kingdom of God, and all these things will be added to you.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui enim non ait, dabuntur, sed adjicientur, profecto indicat aliud esse quod principaliter datur, aliud quod superadditur. Quia enim nobis in intentione aeternitas, in usu vero temporalitas esse debet, et illud datur, et hoc nimirum ex abundanti superadditur.
chunks.english: For he who does not say they will be given but they will be added, certainly indicates that one thing is what is principally given, another what is added over. Because for us eternity should be in intention, but temporality in use, and that is given, and this is certainly added over from abundance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe homines dum in oratione bona temporalia postulant, aeterna vero praemia non requirunt, petunt quod adjicitur, et illud non desiderant ubi adjiciatur. Nec lucrum suae esse petitionis deputant, si hic sint temporaliter pauperes, et illic beatitudine divites in aeternum vivant: sed solis, ut dictum est, visibilibus intenti, labore postulationis renuunt invisibilia mercari.
chunks.english: Often people, while asking for temporal goods in prayer but not seeking eternal rewards, seek what is added and do not desire that to which it is added. Nor do they count it as gain of their petition if they are here temporally poor and there rich in blessedness, living forever. But intent only, as was said, on visible things, they refuse to trade labor of petition for invisible things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui si superna quaererent, jam cum fructu laborem exhiberent. Cum mens uniuscujusque electi in precibus ad Auctoris sui speciem anhelat, divinis desideriis inflammata, supernis conjungitur, ab inferioribus separatur, amore fervoris sui se aperit ut capiat, et capiens inflammat; et superiora amare, jam sursum ire est: dumque magno desiderio ad coelestia inhiat, miro modo hoc ipsum quod accipere quaerit degustat.
chunks.english: If they sought heavenly things, they would already be showing labor with fruit. When the mind of each elect person longs in prayers for the vision of its Author, inflamed with divine desires, it is joined to heavenly things, separated from lower things, opens itself by the love of its fervor that it may receive, and receiving, inflames. And to love higher things is already to go upward. And while with great desire it opens wide to heavenly things, in a wonderful way it tastes that very thing which it seeks to receive.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Libentius sacrificium oblationis accipitur quod in conspectu misericordis Judicis proximi dilectione conditur. Quod tunc veraciter quisque cumulat, si hoc etiam pro adversariis impendat.
chunks.english: The sacrifice of offering is more willingly received which is seasoned in the sight of the merciful Judge with love of neighbor. This anyone truly heaps up if he bestows it even for adversaries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Isaias propheta ait: Intra in cubiculum tuum, et claude ostium tuum (Isai. XXVI, 20). Et Veritas dicit: Ora Patrem tuum in absconso, et Pater tuus, qui videt in abscondito, reddet tibi (Matth. VI, 6).
chunks.english: The prophet Isaiah says in chapter twenty-six, verse twenty: Enter into your chamber and close your door. And the Truth says in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter six, verse six: Pray to your Father in secret, and your Father who sees in secret will reward you.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Clauso quippe ostio petit in cubiculo, qui tacente ore in conspectu supernae pietatis infundit affectum mentis. Vox vero auditur in abscondito, cum per sancta desideria silenter clamatur.
chunks.english: For with door closed he asks in the chamber who, with mouth silent, pours out the affection of mind in the sight of heavenly mercy. And the voice is heard in secret when one cries silently through holy desires.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde recte quoque per Psalmistam dicitur: Desiderium pauperum exaudivit Dominus: desideria cordis eorum exaudivit auris tua (Ps. XIX, 17). Item Veritas dicit: Scit Pater vester quid opus sit vobis, antequam petatis eum (Matth. VI, 8).
chunks.english: Therefore it is also rightly said through the Psalmist in Psalm nineteen, verse seventeen: The Lord has heard the desire of the poor; your ear has heard the desires of their heart. Likewise the Truth says in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter six, verse eight: Your Father knows what you need before you ask him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Peti vult hoc quod nos petere et se concedere praenoscit. Importune igitur orare nos admonet, et hoc inspirat ut petatur; hoc autem requirit ut cor audientium excitet ad orationem.
chunks.english: He wants to be asked for what he foreknows that we will ask and he will grant. Therefore he urges us to pray importunately, and inspires that it be asked. But this he requires, that he may stir the heart of hearers to prayer.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum ad orationis studium discipulos Veritas instrueret, ait Patri: Fiat voluntas tua sicut in coelo et in terra (Matth. VI, 10). Possunt per coelum hi qui in coelestibus sunt conditi angelici spiritus designari, ut nimirum voluntas Dei sicut a superiori creatura agitur, ita in omnibus etiam ab humana infirmitate servetur.
chunks.english: When the Truth instructed disciples in the pursuit of prayer, he said to the Father in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter six, verse ten: Your will be done on earth as it is in heaven. Through heaven those angelic spirits who were created in heavenly things can be designated, so that clearly the will of God, as it is done by the higher creature, may thus also be kept in all things even by human weakness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Panem nostrum quotidianum da nobis hodie (Matth. VI, 11). Ecce et nostrum dicimus, et tamen ut nobis detur Dominum exoramus. Noster quippe fit cum accipitur, qui tamen Dei est quia ab illo datur. Et Dei ergo est ex munere, et noster fit veraciter per acceptionem.
chunks.english: Give us today our daily bread, as Matthew chapter six, verse eleven records. Behold, we both call it ours, and yet we entreat the Lord that it be given to us. For it becomes ours when it is received, which nevertheless is God's because it is given by him. And therefore it is God's from gift, and it truly becomes ours through reception.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dimitte nobis debita nostra, sicut et nos dimittimus debitoribus nostris (Matth. VI, 12). Ut profecto bonum, quod a Deo compuncti petimus, hoc primum cum proximo conversi faciamus.
chunks.english: Forgive us our debts as we also forgive our debtors, as Matthew chapter six, verse twelve states. So that clearly the good which we, compunct, ask from God, this we first do having turned to our neighbor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc ergo vere sine macula in oratione faciem levamus, cum nec nos prohibita mala committimus, nec ea quae in nos commissa ex proprio zelo sunt retinemus. Gravi confusione mens nostra orationis suae tempore deprimitur, si hanc aut sua operatio adhuc inquinat, aut alienae nequitiae servatus dolor accusat; quae duo quisque dum terserit, ad ea quae subnixa sunt protinus libens exsurgit.
chunks.english: Then truly we raise our face without blemish in prayer when we neither commit forbidden evils, nor retain from our own zeal those things which have been committed against us. Our mind is weighed down with grave confusion at the time of its prayer if either its own action still stains it or preserved pain from another's wickedness accuses it. When anyone has wiped away these two things, he immediately rises freely to those things which are supported beneath.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et ne nos inducas in tentationem (Matth. VI, 13). Neque enim in tentationem Veritas inducit, qui semper a tentatione subditos misericorditer protegit; sed tamen in tentationem quasi ejus inducere est a tentationis nos illecebra non munire. Tunc quippe nos omnipotens Deus in tentationis laqueum non inducit, cum tentari ultra quam possumus non permittit.
chunks.english: And lead us not into temptation, as Matthew chapter six, verse thirteen says. For the Truth does not lead into temptation, who always mercifully protects his subjects from temptation. But nevertheless to lead into temptation, as it were, of his doing, is not to fortify us from the allurement of temptation. For then almighty God does not lead us into the snare of temptation when he does not permit us to be tempted beyond what we can bear.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum turbas phantasmatum in oratione patimur, Jesum aliquatenus transeuntem sentimus; cum vero orationi vehementer insistimus, stat Jesus ut lucem restituat, quia Deus in corde figitur, et lux amissa reparatur.
chunks.english: While we suffer crowds of phantasms in prayer, we feel Jesus passing by to some extent. But when we press upon prayer vehemently, Jesus stands to restore light, because God is fixed in the heart, and the lost light is restored.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Valde namque apud Dominum utraque haec sibi necessario congruunt, ut et oratione operatio, et operatione fulciatur oratio. Jeremias propheta ait: Scrutemur vias nostras, et quaeramus, et revertamur ad Dominum. Levemus corda nostra cum manibus ad Deum in coelum (Thren. III, 40).
chunks.english: For both these things necessarily agree with each other before the Lord, so that both action be supported by prayer and prayer by action. The prophet Jeremiah says in Lamentations chapter three, verse forty: Let us search and examine our ways, and return to the Lord. Let us lift up our hearts with our hands to God in heaven.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vias etenim nostras scrutari est cogitationum interna discutere; corda vero cum manibus levat qui orationem suam operibus roborat. Nam quisquis orat, sed operari dissimulat, cor levat et manus non levat; quisquis vero operatur et non orat, manus levat et cor non levat.
chunks.english: For to search our ways is to examine the interior of thoughts. But he lifts hearts with hands who strengthens his prayer with works. For whoever prays but dissimulates working lifts heart and does not lift hands. But whoever works and does not pray lifts hands and does not lift heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Joannes apostolus ait: Si cor nostrum non reprehenderit nos, fiduciam habemus apud Deum (I Joan. III, 21). Tunc ergo fiduciam cor in oratione accipit, cum sibi vitae pravitas nulla contradicit.
chunks.english: The apostle John says in his first letter, chapter three, verse twenty-one: If our heart does not condemn us, we have confidence before God. Therefore the heart then receives confidence in prayer when no depravity of life contradicts it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cor quippe nos in petitione reprehendit, cum resistere praeceptis ejus quem postulat meminit; et oratio fit exsecrabilis, cum a censura avertitur legis; quia dignum profecto est ut ab ejus beneficiis sit quisque extraneus, cujus nimirum jussionibus non vult esse subjectus.
chunks.english: For the heart reproves us in petition when it remembers resisting the precepts of him whom it petitions. And prayer becomes execrable when it is turned away from the judgment of the law, because it is clearly fitting that anyone be a stranger from his benefits whose commands he clearly does not want to be subject to.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In oratione hoc est salubre remedium, ut cum mens se ex memoria culpae reprehendit, hoc prius defleat quod erravit; quatenus dum ab erroris macula fletibus tergitur, in petitione sua cordis facies ab auctore munda videatur.
chunks.english: In prayer this is the wholesome remedy, that when the mind reproves itself from memory of fault, it first bewail what it erred, so that while it is wiped from the stain of error by weeping, the face of the heart in its petition may be seen clean by the author.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Sevenfold Spirit and Ascent to Wisdom
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XXIII.
De distributione spiritus septiformis.
chunks.english: Chapter twenty-three: The Distribution of the Sevenfold Spirit

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In mente fidelium primus ascensionis gradus est timor Domini; secundus, pietas; tertius, scientia; quartus, fortitudo; quintus, consilium; sextus, intellectus; septimus, sapientia.
chunks.english: In the mind of the faithful, the first step of ascent is fear of the Lord, the second is piety, the third is knowledge, the fourth is fortitude, the fifth is counsel, the sixth is understanding, the seventh is wisdom.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Isaias propheta in Christo gradus septiformis gratiae enumerans ait: Requiescit super eum spiritus Domini: spiritus sapientiae et intellectus, spiritus consilii et fortitudinis, spiritus scientiae et pietatis, et replebit eum spiritus timoris Domini (Isa. XI, 2, 3).
chunks.english: The prophet Isaiah, enumerating in Christ the steps of sevenfold grace, says in chapter eleven, verses two and three: The Spirit of the Lord shall rest upon him, the Spirit of wisdom and understanding, the Spirit of counsel and might, the Spirit of knowledge and piety, and the Spirit of the fear of the Lord shall fill him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quos scilicet gradus de coelestibus loquens, descendendo magis quam ascendendo numeravit, videlicet sapientiam, intellectum, consilium, fortitudinem, scientiam, pietatem, timorem. Et cum scriptum sit: Initium sapientiae timor Domini (Prov. IX, 10), constat procul dubio quia a timore ad sapientiam ascenditur, non autem a sapientia ad timorem reditur; perfectam procul dubio habet sapientia charitatem, sicut scriptum est: Perfecta charitas foris mittit timorem (Joan. IV, 18).
chunks.english: Which steps, speaking about heavenly things, he numbered descending rather than ascending, namely wisdom, understanding, counsel, fortitude, knowledge, piety, fear. And since it is written in Proverbs chapter nine, verse ten: The beginning of wisdom is fear of the Lord, it is clear without doubt that one ascends from fear to wisdom, but does not return from wisdom to fear. For wisdom without doubt has perfect charity, as it is written in the Gospel of John, chapter four, verse eighteen: Perfect love casts out fear.
chunks.notes: The John citation appears to be an error; likely intended as 1 John 4:18

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Isaias ergo quia de coelestibus ad ima loquebatur, coepit magis a sapientia, et descendit ad timorem: sed nos quia a terrenis ad coelestia tendimus, eosdem gradus ascendendo numeremus, ut a timore ad sapientiam pervenire valeamus.
chunks.english: Therefore Isaiah, because he was speaking from heavenly things to the lowest, began rather from wisdom and descended to fear. But we, because we tend from earthly things to heavenly, let us number the same steps ascending, so that we may be able to arrive from fear to wisdom.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Est timor Domini in mente fidelium; sed qualis iste timor est, si cum eo pietas non est? Qui enim misereri proximo ignorat, qui compati ejus tribulationi dissimulat, hujus timor ante omnipotentis Dei oculos nullus est: qui non sublevatur ad pietatem; sed saepe pietas per inordinatam misericordiam errare solet, si fortasse pepercerit quae parcenda non sunt.
chunks.english: There is fear of the Lord in the mind of the faithful, but what kind of fear is this if piety is not with it? For he who does not know how to have mercy on his neighbor, who dissembles compassion for his tribulation, his fear is nothing before the eyes of almighty God, because it is not lifted up to piety. But often piety is accustomed to err through disordered mercy, if perhaps it has spared what should not be spared.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Peccata enim, quae feriri gehennae ignibus possunt, disciplinae sunt verbere corrigenda. Sed inordinata pietas cum temporaliter parcit, ad aeternum supplicium pertrahit. Ut vera et ordinata sit pietas, ad gradum est alium sublevanda, id est ad scientiam, ut sciat vel quid ex misericordia puniat, vel quid ex misericordia dimittat.
chunks.english: For sins which can be struck by the fires of gehenna must be corrected by the lash of discipline. But disordered piety, when it spares temporally, drags to eternal punishment. That piety may be true and ordered, it must be lifted to another step, that is to knowledge, so that it may know either what to punish from mercy or what to forgive from mercy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed quid si sciat quid agere quisque debeat, virtutem vero agendi non habeat? Scientia ergo nostra crescat ad fortitudinem, ut cum videt quid agendum sit, hoc agere per mentis fortitudinem possit: ne timore trepidet, et, pavore collapsa, non valeat bona defendere quae sentit.
chunks.english: But what if anyone knows what he should do but does not have the power of doing? Therefore let our knowledge grow to fortitude, so that when it sees what should be done, it may be able to do this through fortitude of mind, lest it tremble with fear and, collapsed with dread, not be able to defend the good things it perceives.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe fortitudo si improvida fuerit, et minus contra vitia circumspecta, ipsa sui praesumptione in casum ruit. Ascendat ergo ad consilium ut providendo praemuniat omne quod agere fortiter potest.
chunks.english: Often fortitude, if it is improvident and less circumspect against vices, falls into ruin by its own presumption. Therefore let it ascend to counsel so that by foreseeing it may fortify in advance everything it can do bravely.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Esse consilium non potest, si intellectus deest; quia qui non intelligit malum quod agentem gravat, quomodo potest bonum solidare quod adjuvat? Itaque a consilio ascendamus ad intellectum. Sed quid si intellectus magno quidem acumine vigilet, et moderari se nesciat per maturitatem?
chunks.english: Counsel cannot exist if understanding is lacking, because he who does not understand the evil which weighs down the doer, how can he solidify the good which helps? Therefore from counsel let us ascend to understanding. But what if understanding keeps watch with great keenness and does not know how to moderate itself through maturity?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ab intellectu ergo ascendamus ad sapientiam, ut hoc quod acute intellectus invenit, sapientiae maturitate disponat.
