Quia igitur per timorem surgimus ad pietatem, per pietatem ad scientiam ducimur, per scientiam ad fortitudinem roboramur, per fortitudinem ad consilium tendimus, per consilium ad intellectum proficimus, per intellectum ad maturitatem sapientiae venimus: septem gradibus ad portam ascendimus, per quam nobis aditus vitae spiritualis aperitur.
chunks.english: From understanding therefore let us ascend to wisdom, so that what understanding keenly finds, it may arrange with the maturity of wisdom. Because therefore through fear we rise to piety, through piety we are led to knowledge, through knowledge we are strengthened to fortitude, through fortitude we tend to counsel, through counsel we advance to understanding, through understanding we come to the maturity of wisdom. By seven steps we ascend to the gate through which entrance of spiritual life is opened to us.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XXIV.
De non reddendo malum pro malo.
chunks.english: Chapter twenty-four: Not Returning Evil for Evil

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In utroque scriptum est Testamento: Quod tibi non vis fieri, alii ne feceris (Tob. IV, 16; Matth. VII, 12): quibus utriusque Testamenti mandatis per unum malitia compescitur, per aliud benignitas prorogatur; ut quod non vult malum pati quisque non faciens, cesset a nocendi opere: et rursum, bonum quod sibi fieri appetit impendens, erga utilitatem se proximi exerceat ex benignitate.
chunks.english: In both Testaments it is written, as in Tobit chapter four, verse sixteen and the Gospel of Matthew chapter seven, verse twelve: What you do not want done to yourself, do not do to another. By which commands of both Testaments, through one malice is restrained, through the other kindness is extended, so that anyone, by not doing the evil he does not want to suffer, may cease from the work of harming. And again, by bestowing the good which he desires to be done to himself, he may exercise himself from kindness toward the benefit of his neighbor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed haec nimirum duo dum sollicita intentione cogitantur, cor ad innumera virtutum ministeria tenditur: ne vel ad inferenda quae non debet desideriis inquieta mens ferveat, vel erga exhibenda quae debet otio resoluta torpescat.
chunks.english: But these two things, when thought about with careful intention, direct the heart to innumerable ministries of virtues, lest either the restless mind burn with desires to inflict what it should not, or grow torpid, relaxed by idleness, toward showing what it should.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum quisque cavet alteri facere quod nequaquam vult ab altero ipse tolerare, sollicita se intentione circumspicit ne superbia elevet, et usque ad despectum proximi animum dejiciens exaltet; ne ambitio cogitationem laniet; cumque hanc ad appetenda aliena dilatat, angustet.
chunks.english: When anyone guards against doing to another what he by no means wants to endure from another, he looks around himself with careful intention lest pride lift him up and exalt his spirit by casting it down even to contempt of neighbor, lest ambition tear his thought apart and, while it widens this to seeking what belongs to others, narrow it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cavendum summopere est ne cujusquam cor luxuria polluat, et subjectum desideriis per illicita corrumpat. Ne ira exasperet, et usque ad proferendam contumeliam inflammet. Ne invidia mordeat, et alienis felicitatibus aemula sua se face consumat.
chunks.english: It must be most carefully guarded lest lust pollute anyone's heart and corrupt it, subjected to desires through illicit things. Lest anger exasperate and inflame even to bringing forth insult. Lest envy bite and, envious of others' happiness, consume itself with its own torch.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sollicita se quisque circumspectione custodiat, ne immoderate linguam loquacitas pertrahat, eamque ad lasciviam obtrectationis extendat. Ne odium malitia excitet, et os usque ad jaculum maledictionis irritet.
chunks.english: Let anyone guard himself with careful watchfulness lest talkativeness drag the tongue immoderately and extend it to the wantonness of detraction. Lest hatred stir up malice and provoke the mouth even to the spear of cursing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum quisque cogitat ut ea alteri faciat, quae ipse sibi fieri ab altero exspectat, pensat nimirum ut malis bona et bonis meliora respondeat: ut erga procaces mansuetudinem longanimitatis exhibeat, et malitiae peste languentibus gratiam benignitatis ostendat.
chunks.english: When anyone thinks to do to another those things which he himself expects to be done to himself from another, he weighs indeed that he respond to evils with goods and to goods with better things, that toward the insolent he show the gentleness of long-suffering, and to those languishing with the plague of malice he display the grace of kindness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Convenit fidelium unicuique ut discordes pace uniat, et concordes ad concupiscentiam verae pacis accingat; ut indigentibus necessaria tribuat, et errantibus viam rectitudinis ostendat. Ut afflictos verbo et compassione mulceat, ut accensos in hujus mundi desideriis increpatione restringat.
chunks.english: It is fitting for each of the faithful to unite the discordant with peace and gird the harmonious for desire of true peace, to grant necessities to the needy and show the way of rectitude to those erring. To soothe the afflicted with word and compassion, to restrain by rebuke those inflamed with the desires of this world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unusquisque fidelium sic ea quae tacet toleret, ne tamen in animo virus doloris occultet. Sic malevolis munus benignitatis exhibeat, ne tamen per gratiam a jure rectitudinis excedat. Ut cuncta proximis quae praevalet impendat, sed haec impendendo non tumeat.
chunks.english: Let each of the faithful so endure those things which he passes over in silence, yet not hide the poison of grief in the soul. So let him show the gift of kindness to the malevolent, yet not depart through favor from the right of rectitude. That he bestow on neighbors all things which he prevails to do, but in bestowing these may he not swell up.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicque in bonis quae exhibet tumoris praecipitium paveat, ne tamen a boni exercitio torpescat.
chunks.english: And so in the goods which he shows, let him fear the precipice of swelling, yet not grow torpid from the exercise of good.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXV. De pace et concordia.
chunks.english: Chapter 25: On Peace and Concord

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Reg. Past., part. III, c. 22.) Admonendi sunt pacati, ne dum plus quam necesse est pacem quam possident amant, ad perpetuam pervenire non appetant. Plerumque enim gravius intentionem mentium rerum tranquillitas tentat, ut quo non sunt molesta quae tenent, eo minus amabilia fiant quae vocant: et quo delectant praesentia, eo non inquirantur aeterna.
chunks.english: Those who are peaceful must be warned not to love the peace they possess more than necessary, lest they fail to desire the eternal peace to come. For often the tranquility of circumstances attacks the mind's focus more seriously, so that the things they hold, being free from trouble, make what calls to them less lovable; and the more present things delight them, the less they seek eternal things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per semetipsam Veritas loquens, cum terrenam pacem a superna distingueret, atque ad venturam discipulos ex praesenti provocaret, ait: Pacem meam relinquo vobis, pacem meam do vobis (Joan. XIV, 27). Relinquo scilicet transitoriam, do mansuram.
chunks.english: Truth speaking for itself, when distinguishing earthly peace from heavenly and calling disciples from the present to what is to come, says in the Gospel of John, chapter fourteen, verse twenty-seven: I leave you my peace, I give you my peace. I leave, that is, the transitory peace; I give the enduring peace.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si ergo in ea cor quae relicta est figitur, nunquam ad illam quae danda est pervenitur. Pax praesens ita tenenda est ut et diligi debeat et contemni, ne si immoderate diligitur, diligentis animus in culpa capiatur.
chunks.english: If therefore the heart is fixed on the peace that is left behind, it will never reach the peace that is to be given. Present peace should be held in such a way that it must both be loved and despised, lest if it is loved immoderately, the soul of the lover be captured in sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt pacati ne dum nimis humanam pacem desiderant, pravos hominum mores nequaquam redarguant, et consentiendo perversis, ab auctoris sui se pace disjungant; ne dum humana foras jurgia metuunt, interni foederis discussione feriantur.
chunks.english: The peaceful must be warned lest, while they desire human peace too much, they utterly fail to rebuke the wicked conduct of people, and by consenting to the perverse, they separate themselves from peace with their Creator; lest while they fear human quarrels outwardly, they be struck by the breaking of their internal covenant.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid est pax transitoria, nisi quoddam vestigium pacis aeternae? Quid ergo esse dementius potest quam vestigia in pulvere impressa diligere, sed ipsum a quo impressa sunt non amare?
chunks.english: What is transitory peace except a certain trace of eternal peace? What therefore can be more insane than to love footprints pressed in the dust, but not to love the One from whom they were impressed?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Certissime sciendum est quia quantislibet homines virtutibus polleant, spiritales fieri nullatenus possunt, si uniri per concordiam proximis negligunt. Scriptum quippe est: Fructus spiritus est charitas, gaudium, pax (Gal. V, 22); qui ergo servare pacem non curat, ferre fructum spiritus recusat.
chunks.english: We must know with absolute certainty that no matter how many virtues people possess, they can in no way become spiritual if they neglect to be united in harmony with their neighbors. For it is written in Paul's letter to the Galatians, chapter five, verse twenty-two: The fruit of the Spirit is charity, joy, and peace. Therefore whoever does not care to preserve peace refuses to bear the fruit of the Spirit.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc idem Paulus ait: Cum sit inter vos zelus, et contentio, nonne carnales estis (I Cor. III, 3)? Hinc iterum dicit: Pacem sequimini cum omnibus, et sanctimoniam, sine qua Deum nemo videbit (Hebr. XII, 11).
chunks.english: From this the same Paul says in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter three, verse three: When there is among you envy and contention, are you not carnal? And again he says in the letter to the Hebrews, chapter twelve, verse eleven: Follow after peace with all, and holiness, without which no one will see God.
chunks.notes: The biblical reference appears to be Hebrews 12:14, not 12:11 in modern numbering.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Paulus apostolus admonens ait: Solliciti servare unitatem spiritus in vinculo pacis; unum corpus, et unus spiritus, sicut vocati estis in una spe vocationis vestrae (Eph. IV, 3, 4). Ad unam igitur vocationis spem nequaquam pertingitur, si non ad eam unita cum proximis mente curratur.
chunks.english: The apostle Paul, giving warning, says in his letter to the Ephesians, chapter four, verses three and four: Be eager to preserve the unity of the spirit in the bond of peace. One body and one Spirit, just as you are called in one hope of your calling. Therefore we in no way reach the one hope of our calling if we do not run toward it with a mind united with our neighbors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe nonnulli, dum quaedam specialiter dona percipiunt, superbiendo donum concordiae, quod majus est, amittunt: ut si fortasse carnem prae caeteris gulae refrenatione quis edomat, concordare eis quos superat abstinendo contemnat.
chunks.english: Often some people, while receiving certain special gifts, lose through pride the gift of harmony, which is greater; so that if someone perhaps tames the flesh by restraining gluttony more than others, he disdains to be in harmony with those whom he surpasses in abstinence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Evangelio Veritas ait: Habete sal in vobis, et pacem habete inter vos (Marc. IX, 49). Sal quippe sine pace non virtutis est donum, sed damnationis argumentum.
chunks.english: In the Gospel of Mark, chapter nine, verse forty-nine, Truth says: Have salt in yourselves, and have peace among yourselves. Indeed, salt without peace is not the gift of virtue, but a sign of condemnation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe aves unius ejusdemque generis sese socialiter volando non deserunt, congregatim animalia bruta pascuntur, quae si solerter aspicimus, concordando sibi irrationalis natura indicat, quantum malum per discordiam rationalis natura committat, quando haec a rationis intentione perdidit quod illa motu naturali custodit.
chunks.english: Often birds of one and the same kind do not abandon each other in social flight, and brute animals graze together. If we observe this carefully, irrational nature by its harmony shows how great an evil rational nature commits through discord, when rational nature has lost by its failure to follow reason what irrational nature preserves by natural instinct.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: David propheta dum totum se ad foedera pacis internae constringeret, testatur quod cum malis concordiam non teneret, dicens: Nonne qui oderunt te, Deus, oderam illos, et super inimicos tuos tabescebam? Perfecto odio oderam illos, inimici facti sunt mihi (Ps. CXXXVIII, 21, 22).
chunks.english: The prophet David, while binding himself completely to the bonds of internal peace, testifies that he did not keep harmony with evil people, saying in Psalm one hundred thirty-eight, verses twenty-one and twenty-two: Have I not hated those who hate you, O God, and wasted away over your enemies? With perfect hatred I hated them; they have become enemies to me.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Inimicos etenim Dei perfecto odio odisse est, et quod facti sunt diligere, et quod faciunt increpare.
chunks.english: To hate the enemies of God with perfect hatred is to love what they were made to be, and to rebuke what they do.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt pacati, ne si ad correptionis verba prosiliant, temporalem pacem sibi perturbare formident. Rursum admonendi sunt ut eamdem pacem dilectione integra intrinsecus teneant, quam per invectionem voci sibi extrinsecus turbant.
chunks.english: The peaceful must be warned lest, if they leap forward to words of correction, they fear to disturb their temporal peace. Again they must be warned to hold that same peace inwardly with complete love, which they outwardly disturb for themselves by verbal rebuke.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Provide David propheta utrumque se perhibet servare, cum dicit: Cum his qui oderunt pacem eram pacificus, dum loquebar illis, impugnabant me gratis (Ps. CXIX, 7). Ecce et loquens impugnatus erat pacificus, quia nec insanientes cessabat reprehendere, nec reprehensos negligebat amare.
chunks.english: The prophet David wisely declares that he observes both when he says in Psalm one hundred nineteen, verse seven: With those who hated peace I was peaceful; when I spoke to them, they attacked me without cause. Behold, even while speaking and being attacked, he was peaceful, because he did not cease to rebuke the insane, nor did he neglect to love those he rebuked.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Concordiam pacis Paulus commendans ait: Si fieri potest, quod ex vobis est cum omnibus hominibus pacem habentes (Rom. XII, 18). Hortaturus enim discipulos, ut pacem cum hominibus haberent, praemisit dicens: Si fieri potest, atque subjunxit: Quod ex vobis est: difficile quippe erat, ut si male acta corriperent, habere pacem cum omnibus possent.
chunks.english: Paul, commending the harmony of peace, says in his letter to the Romans, chapter twelve, verse eighteen: If it is possible, as far as it depends on you, be at peace with all people. For being about to urge disciples to have peace with people, he said first: If it is possible, and added: As far as it depends on you. For it was difficult that if they corrected wrongdoings, they could have peace with everyone.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum temporalis pax in pravorum cordibus ex nostra increpatione confunditur, inviolata necesse est ut in nostro corde servetur. Recte itaque Apostolus ait: Quod ex vobis est; ac si dicat: Quia pax ex duarum partium consensu subsistit, ut si ab eis qui corripiuntur expellitur, integra tamen in vestra qui corripitis mente teneatur.
chunks.english: When temporal peace is shattered in the hearts of the wicked by our rebuke, it must be preserved inviolate in our own heart. Therefore the Apostle rightly says: As far as it depends on you; as if to say: Because peace depends on the consent of two parties, so that if it is expelled by those who are corrected, it may nevertheless be held whole in the mind of you who correct.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem Paulus rursum discipulos admonet, dicens: Si quis non obedit verbo nostro, per epistolam hunc notate, et non commisceamini cum illo, ut confundatur (II Thess. III, 14). Atque illico subjunxit: Et nolite, ut inimicum existimare illum, sed corripite ut fratrem (Ibid., XV).
chunks.english: The same Paul again warns disciples, saying in his second letter to the Thessalonians, chapter three, verse fourteen: If anyone does not obey our word, mark him through the letter, and do not associate with him, so that he may be ashamed. And immediately he adds, verse fifteen: And do not regard him as an enemy, but correct him as a brother.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ac si diceret: Pacem cum eo exteriorem solvite, sed interiorem circa illum medullitus custodite; ut peccantis mentem sic vestra discordia feriat, quatenus pax a vestris cordibus nec abnegata discedat.
chunks.english: As if he were saying: Break exterior peace with him, but guard interior peace concerning him to the core, so that your discord may strike the sinner's mind in such a way that peace may not depart denied from your hearts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Reg. Past., part. III, c. 23.) In Evangilio Dominus dicit: Beati pacifici, quoniam filii Dei vocabuntur (Matth. V, 9). E diverso namque colligant homines; quia si Dei vocantur filii qui pacem faciunt, proculdubio Satanae sunt filii qui confidunt.
chunks.english: In the Gospel of Matthew, chapter five, verse nine, the Lord says: Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God. From this people should gather the opposite: because if those who make peace are called sons of God, without doubt those who sow conflict are sons of Satan.
chunks.notes: The Latin 'confidunt' seems unclear in this context; it may be a textual corruption.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi ergo sunt qui faciendae pacis studiis occupantur, ut pravorum mentibus prius amorem debeant internae pacis infundere, quatenus eis postmodum valeat exterior pax prodesse.
chunks.english: Therefore those who are occupied with efforts to make peace must be warned that they ought first to pour into wicked minds love of internal peace, so that afterward external peace may be able to benefit them.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXVI. De patientia.
chunks.english: Chapter 26: On Patience

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Lib. II in Ev., homil. 35, n. 5, et Reg. Past., part. III, c. 9.) Quanto culmine virtus patientiae polleat, Salomon indicat, dicens: Melior est patiens viro forti, et qui dominatur animo suo expugnatore urbium (Prov. XVI, 32).
chunks.english: How great the excellence of the virtue of patience is, Solomon indicates, saying in Proverbs, chapter sixteen, verse thirty-two: Better is the patient man than the strong, and he who rules his spirit than the conqueror of cities.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Minor est victoria urbes expugnare, quia extra sunt quae vincuntur; majus est autem quod per patientiam vincitur, quia ipse animus superatur, et semetipsum sibimet subjicit, quando eum patientia in humilitate tolerantiae sternit.
chunks.english: To conquer cities is a lesser victory, because what is conquered is external. But what is conquered through patience is greater, because the soul itself is overcome and subjects itself to itself, when patience lays it low in the humility of endurance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Melior est, inquit, patiens viro forti, et qui dominatur animo suo expugnatore urbium. (Lib. II in Ezech., homil. 6, n. 7.) Recte expugnatori urbium patiens praefertur, quia in illa actione victoriae homo victor est hominum, in hac autem mansuetudine patientiae animus victor est sui.
chunks.english: Better, he says, is the patient man than the strong, and he who rules his spirit than the conqueror of cities. Rightly is the patient man preferred to the conqueror of cities, because in that action of victory, man is victor over men, but in this gentleness of patience, the soul is victor over itself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Terra bona fructum per patientiam reddit, quia scilicet nulla sunt bona quae agimus, si non aequanimiter etiam proximorum mala toleramus. Scriptum est: Charitas patiens est (I Cor. XIII, 4). Igitur cum minime est patiens, charitas non est.
chunks.english: Good soil yields fruit through patience, because there are no good things that we do if we do not also bear our neighbors' evils with equanimity. It is written in Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter thirteen, verse four: Charity is patient. Therefore when it is not patient, it is not charity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Reg. Past., part. III, c. 9.) Per hoc quoque in patientia vitium ipsa virtutum nutrix doctrina dissipatur. Salomon ait: Doctrina viri per patientiam noscitur (Prov. XIX, 11). Tanto ergo quisque minus ostenditur doctus, quanto minus convincitur patiens.
chunks.english: Through this also, in impatience the very nurse of virtues, teaching itself, is destroyed. Solomon says in Proverbs, chapter nineteen, verse eleven: A man's learning is known by patience. Therefore one is shown to be less learned to the degree that he is proven less patient.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Neque enim potest veraciter bona docendo impendere, si vivendo nescit aequanimiter aliena mala tolerare. In Ecclesiaste scriptum est: Melior est patiens arrogante (Eccle. VII, 9); quia videlicet eligit patiens quaelibet mala perpeti, quam per ostentationis vitium bona sua occulta cognosci.
chunks.english: For he cannot truly impart good things by teaching if in living he does not know how to bear others' evils with equanimity. It is written in Ecclesiastes, chapter seven, verse nine: Better is the patient than the arrogant; because the patient person chooses to endure any evils rather than have his hidden good deeds be known through the vice of ostentation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum patientia relinquitur, etiam bona reliqua quae jam gesta sunt destruuntur. Unde recte per Ezechielem esse in altari Dei fossa perhibetur (Ezech. XLIII, 13) ut in ea videlicet superposita holocausta serventur. Si enim in altari fossa non esset, omne quod in eo sacrificium reperiret superveniens aura dispergeret.
chunks.english: When patience is abandoned, even the other good things that have already been done are destroyed. Hence it is rightly said through Ezekiel, chapter forty-three, verse thirteen, that there is a hollow in the altar of God, so that the burnt offerings placed upon it may be preserved. For if there were no hollow in the altar, any sacrifice found on it would be scattered by the coming breeze.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Custodem conditionis nostrae patientiam Dominus esse monstravit, qui in ipsa nos possidere nosmetipsos docuit, dicens: In patientia vestra possidebitis animas vestras (Luc. XXI, 19).
chunks.english: The Lord showed patience to be the guardian of our condition, who taught us to possess ourselves in it, saying in the Gospel of Luke, chapter twenty-one, verse nineteen: In your patience you shall possess your souls.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt patientes ne in eo quod exterius per patientiam portant interius doleant: ne tantae virtutis sacrificium, quod integrum foras immolant, intus, malitiae peste corrumpant.
chunks.english: The patient must be warned not to grieve inwardly over what they bear outwardly through patience, lest they corrupt inwardly by the plague of malice the sacrifice of so great a virtue, which they offer whole outwardly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dicendum est patientibus ut studeant diligere quod sibi necesse est tolerare, ne si patientiam dilectio non sequatur, in deteriorem culpam odii virtus ostensa vertatur.
chunks.english: It must be said to the patient that they should strive to love what they must tolerate, lest if love does not follow patience, the virtue displayed be turned into the worse fault of hatred.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Paulus apostolus cum diceret: Charitas patiens est (I Cor. XIII, 4), illico adjunxit: Benigna est, videlicet ostendens quia quos ex patientia tolerat, amare etiam ex benignitate non cessat.
chunks.english: When the apostle Paul said in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter thirteen, verse four: Charity is patient, he immediately added: It is kind, clearly showing that those whom it tolerates through patience, it also does not cease to love through kindness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem doctor egregius, cum patientiam discipulis suaderet, dicens: Omnis amaritudo, et ira, et indignatio, et clamor, et blasphemia tollatur a vobis (Ephes. IV, 31): quasi cunctis exterius jam bene compositis, ad interiora revertitur, cum subjungit: Cum omni malitia; quia nimirum frustra indignatio, clamor et blasphemia ab exterioribus tollitur, si in interioribus vitiorum mater malitia dominatur.
chunks.english: The same excellent teacher, when urging patience on disciples, saying in his letter to the Ephesians, chapter four, verse thirty-one: Let all bitterness and anger and indignation and clamor and blasphemy be taken away from you; as if with all exterior things now well arranged, he returns to interior things when he adds: With all malice. Because indignation, clamor, and blasphemy are removed from exterior things in vain if in interior things malice, the mother of vices, reigns.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et incassum foras nequitia ex ramis inciditur, si surrectura multiplicius intus in radice servatur. Plerum que evenire patientibus solet, ut eo quidem tempore quo vel adversa patiuntur, vel contumelias audiunt. nullo dolore puisentur; et sic patientiam exhibeant, ut custodire etiam cordis innocentiam non omittant.
chunks.english: And wickedness is cut from the branches outwardly in vain if it is preserved inwardly in the root to rise up more abundantly. It usually happens to patient people that at the very time when they suffer adversities or hear insults, they are not struck by any pain, and they exhibit patience so as not to neglect to guard even the innocence of the heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed cum post paululum haec ipsa quae pertulerint ad memoriam revocant, igne se doloris inflammant, argumenta ultionis inquirunt, et mansuetudinem quam tolerantes habuerunt, retractantes in malitiam vertunt.
chunks.english: But when a little later they recall to memory these very things they endured, they inflame themselves with the fire of pain, they seek grounds for revenge, and the gentleness they had while enduring, they turn to malice in reviewing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt patientes ut cor post victoriam muniant, ut hostem publico bello superatum insidiari moeniis mentis intendant, ut languorem plus reserpentem timeant, ne hostis callidus eo in deceptione postmodum majori exsultatione gaudeat, quod illa dudum contra se rigida colla victorum calcat.
chunks.english: The patient must be warned to fortify their heart after victory, to watch for the enemy conquered in open battle to ambush the walls of the mind, to fear the affliction creeping back more, lest the cunning enemy rejoice with all the greater exultation in deception afterward, that he tramples those stiff necks of the victors once unyielding against him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Lib. II in Ezech., homil. 2, n. 2.) Est in dilectione proximi nobis patientia, et benignitas conservanda; quia de eadem dilectione nunc dicitur: Charitas patiens est, benigna est (I Cor. XV, 4). Patiens scilicet, ut illata a proximis mala aequanimiter portet; benigna autem, ut sua bona proximis desiderabiliter impendat.
chunks.english: In love of neighbor we must preserve patience and kindness, because concerning that same love it is now said in Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter fifteen, verse four: Charity is patient, it is kind. Patient, that is, so that it may bear evils inflicted by neighbors with equanimity; kind, however, so that it may bestow its own goods on neighbors with desire.
chunks.notes: The biblical reference appears to be 1 Corinthians 13:4, not 15:4.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Lib. II in Evang., homil. 35, n. 4.) Patientia vero est aliena mala aequanimiter perpeti, contra eum quoque qui mala irrogat nullo dolore morderi. Nam qui sic proximi mala portat ut tamen tacitus doleat, et tempus dignae retributionis quaerat, patientiam non exhibet, sed ostendit.
chunks.english: Patience truly is to endure the evils of others with equanimity, and to be bitten by no pain even against him who inflicts the evils. For he who bears his neighbor's evils in such a way that he nevertheless grieves silently and seeks a time for worthy recompense, does not practice patience but only displays it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unusquisque miles Dei adversitatis bello deprehensus, et scutum patientiae debet anteferre ne pereat; et ad praedicationem promptus amoris inferre jacula ut vincat.
chunks.english: Every soldier of God caught in the battle of adversity must both hold forth the shield of patience lest he perish, and must hurl the spears of love's proclamation ready to conquer.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum est: Nunquid in finem oblivio erit pauperum, patientia pauperum non peribit in finem (Ps. IX, 19). Quantumlibet vel in adversis patientes vel in prosperis humiles simus, in hac vita retribui nobis bona praesentia nullo modo requiramus; nam pro labore patientiae bona speranda sunt sequentis vitae; ut tunc praemium nostri laboris incipiat quando omnis jam labor funditus cessat.
chunks.english: It is written in Psalm nine, verse nineteen: Will there be forgetfulness of the poor unto the end? The patience of the poor will not perish unto the end. However patient we may be in adversities or humble in prosperity, let us in no way seek present goods to be repaid to us in this life. For in return for the labor of patience, the good things of the next life must be hoped for, so that the reward of our labor may begin then when all labor finally ceases.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quasi patientia pauperum perisse cernitur, cum nihil pro illa in hac vita humilibus recompensatur; sed patientia pauperum in finem non peribit, quia tunc ejus gloria percipitur cum simul omnia laboriosa terminantur.
chunks.english: The patience of the poor seems to have perished, as it were, when nothing is recompensed to the humble for it in this life. But the patience of the poor will not perish unto the end, because its glory is perceived then when all laborious things are finished together.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Humility as the Foundation of Virtue
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXVII. De humilitate.
chunks.english: Chapter 27: On Humility

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Moral. lib. XXXIV, c. 23, n. 54; Reg. Past., part. III, c. 17.) Ad hoc unigenitus Dei filius formam infirmitatis nostrae suscepit, ob hoc invisibilis, vel despectus apparuit, ad hoc contumeliarum ludibria, irrisionum probra, passionum tormenta toleravit, ut superbum non esse hominem doceret humilis Deus.
chunks.english: For this purpose the only-begotten Son of God took on the form of our weakness, for this purpose he appeared invisible or despised, for this purpose he endured the mockeries of insults, the reproaches of derision, the torments of suffering, that God in humility might teach man not to be proud.
chunks.notes: The phrase 'invisibilis, vel despectus apparuit' presents a translation challenge; it may mean 'appeared though invisible' or 'appeared despised'; context suggests the latter.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quanta humilitatis virtus est, quantaque sublimis celsitudo, propter quam solam veraciter edocendam is qui sine aestimatione magnus est usque ad passionem factus est parvus?
chunks.english: How great is the virtue of humility, how sublime the height, for the sake of truly teaching which alone he who is great beyond measure became small even unto suffering?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quia originem perditionis nostrae se praebuit diaboli, instrumentum redemptionis nostrae inventa est humilitas Dei. Hostis noster inter omnia conditus, videri supra omnia voluit elatus, Redemptor autem noster magnus, manens supra omnia, fieri inter omnes dignatus est parvus.
chunks.english: Because the pride of the devil provided the origin of our perdition, the humility of God was found to be the instrument of our redemption. Our enemy, created among all things, wished to be seen exalted above all things. But our Redeemer, great and remaining above all things, deigned to become small among all.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Humilitas magistra est omnium materque virtutum, sicut Veritas ait: Discite a me quia mitis sum et humilis corde, et invenietis requiem animabus vestris (Matth. XI, 29), quia dum se humiles dejiciunt, ad Dei similitudinem ascendunt.
chunks.english: Humility is the teacher and mother of all virtues, as Truth says in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter eleven, verse twenty-nine: Learn from me because I am meek and humble of heart, and you will find rest for your souls; because while the humble abase themselves, they ascend to the likeness of God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid igitur humilitate sublimius, quae dum se in ima deprimit, Auctori suo manenti se super summa conjungit?
chunks.english: What therefore is loftier than humility, which while it presses itself down to the lowest, joins itself to its Creator who remains above the highest?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Reg. Past., part. III, c. 17.) Verae humilitatis testimonia sunt, et iniquitatem suam quemque cognoscere, et cognitam voce confessionis aperire. Insinuandum est humilibus quam sit vera excellentia quam sperando tenent, quam sint aeterna quae appetunt, vel quam transitoria quae contemnunt.
chunks.english: The testimonies of true humility are both that each person recognize his own iniquity and that he reveal the recognized iniquity by the voice of confession. It must be made known to the humble how true is the excellence they hold by hoping, how eternal are the things they desire, or how transitory are the things they despise.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXVIII. De simplicitate.
chunks.english: Chapter 28: On Simplicity

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Reg. Past., part. III, c. 11.) Admonendi sunt simplices ut studeant nunquam falsa dicere, sed ut noverint, nunquam vera reticere: sicut enim saepe dicentem falsitas laesit, ita nonnunquam quibusdam audita vera nocuerunt.
chunks.english: The simple must be warned to strive never to speak falsehoods, but also to know never to keep silent about truths. For just as falsehood has often harmed the speaker, so sometimes heard truths have harmed certain people.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt simplices ut sicut fallaciam semper utiliter vitant, ita veritatem semper utiliter proferant. Admonendi sunt ut simplicitatis bono prudentiam adjungant quatenus sic securitatem de simplicitate possideant, ut circumspectionem prudentiae non amittant.
chunks.english: The simple must be warned that just as they usefully always avoid deceit, so they always usefully speak the truth. They must be warned to join prudence to the good of simplicity, so that they possess security from simplicity in such a way that they do not lose the circumspection of prudence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc namque per doctorem gentium dicitur: Volo vos sapientes esse in bono, et simplices in malo (Rom. XVI, 19). Electos suos per semetipsam Veritas admonet, dicens: Estote prudentes sicut serpentes, et simplices sicut columbae (Matth. X, 16), quia videlicet in electorum cordibus debet et simplicitatem columbae astutia serpentis acuere, et serpentis astutiam columbae simplicitas temperare: quatenus nec seducti per prudentiam calleant, nec ab intellectus studio ex simplicitate torpescant.
chunks.english: For through the teacher of the nations it is said in Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter sixteen, verse nineteen: I wish you to be wise in good, and simple in evil. Truth itself warns its elect, saying in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter ten, verse sixteen: Be prudent as serpents and simple as doves; because in the hearts of the elect the cunning of the serpent must sharpen the simplicity of the dove, and the simplicity of the dove must temper the cunning of the serpent, so that neither deceived do they become crafty through prudence, nor from the pursuit of understanding do they grow sluggish from simplicity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Moral. lib. XII, c. 39, n. 44.) Nil simplici corde felicius quia quo innocentiam erga alios exhibet, nil est quod pati ab aliis formidet: habet enim quasi arcem quamdam fortitudinis simplicitatem suam, nec suspectus est pati quod se fecisse non meminit.
chunks.english: Nothing is more blessed than a simple heart, because to the degree that it shows innocence toward others, there is nothing it need fear to suffer from others. For it has its simplicity as a kind of citadel of strength, nor is it expected to suffer what it does not remember having done.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene per Salomonem dicitur: In timore Domini fiducia fortitudinis (Prov. XIV, 26.); qui et rursum ait: Secura mens quasi juge convivium (Prov. XV, 15). Quasi enim continuatio refectionis est ipsa tranquillitas securitatis.
chunks.english: Well it is said through Solomon in Proverbs, chapter fourteen, verse twenty-six: In the fear of the Lord is confidence of strength. And he says again in Proverbs, chapter fifteen, verse fifteen: A secure mind is like a continual feast. For the tranquility of security is like a continuation of refreshment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui ambulat simpliciter, ambulat confidenter (Prov. X, 9). [Reg. Past., part. III, c. 11.] Fiducia quippe magnae securitatis est simplicitas actionis, quoniam sanctus Spiritus disciplinae effugiet fictum, nec habitabit in corpore subdito peccatis (Sap. I, 5).
chunks.english: He who walks in simplicity walks confidently, as Proverbs, chapter ten, verse nine says. Indeed, simplicity of action is confidence of great security, because the Holy Spirit of discipline will flee deceit, and will not dwell in a body subject to sins, as the Book of Wisdom, chapter one, verse five states.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Right Intention in Heart and Deed
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXIX. De intentione cordis et corporis.
chunks.english: Chapter 29: On Intention of Heart and Body

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Moral., lib. XXVIII, c. 11, n. 30.) Lucerna corporis est oculus, quia per bonae intentionis radium merita illustrantur actionis, sicut Veritas ait: Si oculus tuus simplex fuerit, totum corpus tuum lucidum erit (Matth. VI, 22).
chunks.english: The lamp of the body is the eye, because through the ray of good intention the merits of action are illuminated, as Truth says in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter six, verse twenty-two: If your eye is sound, your whole body will be full of light.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per oculum exprimitur praeveniens opus suum cordis intentio; quae priusquam se in actione exerceat hoc jam quod appetit contemplatur. Quid denique appellatione corporis designatur, nisi unaquaeque actio, quae intentionem suam quasi intuentem oculum sequitur?
chunks.english: By the eye is expressed the heart's intention preceding its work; which before it exercises itself in action already contemplates what it desires. Finally, what is designated by the term body except each action, which follows its intention like a watching eye?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si recte intenditur per simplicitatem cogitationis, bonum opus efficitur, etiamsi minus bonum esse videatur. Nam si oculus nequam fuerit, totum corpus tenebrosum erit, quia cum perversa intentione quid vel rectum agitur, et si splendere coram hominibus cernitur, apud examen tamen interni judicis obscuratur.
chunks.english: If there is right intention through simplicity of thought, good work is produced, even if it seems less good. For if the eye is evil, the whole body will be dark, because when anything even right is done with perverse intention, even if it is seen to shine before men, nevertheless before the examination of the internal judge it is darkened.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si lumen, inquit, quod in te est tenebrae sunt, ipsae tenebrae quantae erunt (Ibid. 23)? Quia si hoc quod bene hos agere credimus ex mala intentione fuscamus, quanta ipsa mala sunt, quae mala esse etiam cum agimus, non ignoramus?
chunks.english: If, he says, the light that is in you is darkness, how great will that darkness be, verse twenty-three? Because if what we believe we do well we darken by evil intention, how great are those very evils which we know to be evil even when we do them?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si igitur ibi nil discernimus ubi quasi discretionis lumen tenemus, qua caecitate in illa offendimus quae sine discretione perpetramus? Vigilanti cura per cuncta opera intentio nobis nostra pensanda est, ut nil temporale in his quae agit appetat; totam se in soliditate aeternitatis figat: ne si extra fundamentum actionis nostrae fabrica ponitur, terra dehiscente solvatur.
chunks.english: If therefore we discern nothing there where we hold, as it were, the light of discretion, with what blindness do we stumble in those things which we perpetrate without discretion? With watchful care our intention must be weighed by us through all works, so that it may seek nothing temporal in the things it does; let it fix itself wholly in the solidity of eternity, lest if the structure of our action is placed outside the foundation, it be dissolved when the earth gapes.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXX. De mansuetudine.
chunks.english: Chapter 30: On Gentleness

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Reg. Past., p. III, c. 16.) Plerumque mansueti vicinum et quasi juxta positum torporem desidiae patiuntur, ac per nimiam resolutionem lenitatis, ultra quam necesse est, vigorem districtionis emolliunt. Et saepe mansueti dissolutionis torpescunt taedio, quia eorum virtuti vitium latenter adjungitur.
chunks.english: Often the gentle suffer a neighboring and closely situated torpor of laziness, and through excessive looseness of mildness, soften the vigor of strictness beyond what is necessary. And often the gentle grow numb with the tedium of dissolution, because vice is secretly added to their virtue.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed admonendi sunt ut fugiant quod juxta ipsos est, et amplectantur sollicitudinem, quae acuat multae benignitatis incuriam. Idcirco sanctus Spiritus in columba nobis est et in igne monstratus, quia videlicet omnes quos implet et columbae simplicitate mansuetos, et igne zeli ardentes exhibet.
chunks.english: But they must be warned to flee what is beside them and to embrace solicitude, which sharpens the carelessness of much kindness. Therefore the Holy Spirit is shown to us both in the dove and in fire, because he makes all whom he fills both gentle with the simplicity of the dove and burning with the fire of zeal.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nequaquam sancto Spiritu plenus est qui aut in tranquillitate mansuetudinis fervorem aemulationis deserit, aut rursum in aemulationis ardore virtutem mansuetudinis amittit.
chunks.english: He is in no way full of the Holy Spirit who either abandons the fervor of zeal in the tranquility of gentleness, or on the other hand loses the virtue of gentleness in the ardor of zeal.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vas electionis Paulus apostolus duobus discipulis non diversa tamen adjutoria praedicationis impendit. Timotheum namque admonens ait: Argue, obsecra, increpa in omni patientia et doctrina (II Tim. IV, 2). Titum quoque admonet dicens: Haec loquere, et exhortare, et argue cum omni imperio (Tit. II, 15).
chunks.english: Paul the apostle, the vessel of election, bestows on two disciples different aids of preaching, though not from diverse charity. For warning Timothy he says in his second letter to Timothy, chapter four, verse two: Argue, entreat, rebuke with all patience and teaching. He also warns Titus saying in his letter to Titus, chapter two, verse fifteen: Speak these things, and exhort, and rebuke with all authority.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid est quod doctrinam suam magister egregius tanta arte dispensat, ut in exhibenda hac alteri imperium, atque alteri patientiam proponat, nisi quod mansuetioris spiritus Titum, et paulo ferventioris vidit esse Timotheum?
chunks.english: What does it mean that the excellent teacher dispenses his teaching with such art, that in giving instruction he proposes authority to one and patience to the other, except that he saw Titus to be of a gentler spirit and Timothy of a somewhat more fervent one?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illum per aemulationis studium inflammat; hunc per lenitatem patientiae temperat. Illi quod deest jungit, huic quod superest subtrahit. Illum stimulo impellere nititur, hunc freno moderatur.
chunks.english: He inflames the one through the zeal of emulation; he tempers the other through the mildness of patience. To the one he adds what is lacking; from the other he subtracts what is excessive. He strives to drive the one with the spur; he restrains the other with the bridle.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magnus quisque susceptae Ecclesiae colonus alios palmites, ut crescere debeant, rigat; alios cum plus justo crescere conspicit, resecat: ne aut non crescendo fructus non ferant, aut immoderate crescendo quos protulerint amittant
chunks.english: Every great farmer of the Church he has undertaken waters some branches so they may grow as they should; others, when he sees them growing more than is right, he cuts back, lest either by not growing they bear no fruit, or by growing immoderately they lose what they have brought forth.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Obedience and the Will of God
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXI. De obedientia.
chunks.english: Chapter 31: On Obedience

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Moral. lib. XXXV, c. 14, n. 30.) Sciendum summopere quod obedientia aliquando si de suo aliquid habeat, nulla est; si de suo aliquid non habeat, minima.
chunks.english: We must know especially that obedience sometimes, if it has something of its own, is nothing; if it does not have something of its own, it is minimal.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum hujus mundi successus praecipitur, cum locus superior imperatur, is qui ad percipienda haec obedit, obedientiae sibi virtutem evacuit, si ad haec etiam ex proprio desiderio anhelat.
chunks.english: When the success of this world is commanded, when a higher position is ordered, he who obeys in order to receive these things empties for himself the virtue of obedience, if he also gasps for these things from his own desire.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Neque enim se sub obedientia dirigit, qui ad percipienda hujus vitae prospera libidini propriae ambitionis servit. Cum saeculi hujus despectus praecipitur, cum probra adipisci et contumeliae jubentur; nisi haec et ex semetipso animus appetat, obedientiae sibi meritum minuit, quia ad ea quae in hac vita despecta sunt invitus nolensque descendit.
chunks.english: For he does not direct himself under obedience who serves the lust of his own ambition in receiving the prosperous things of this life. When contempt of this world is commanded, when reproaches and insults are ordered to be received, unless the soul also desires these things from itself, it diminishes for itself the merit of obedience, because it descends unwillingly and reluctantly to those things which are despised in this life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquando ad detrimentum obedientia ducitur, cum mentem ad suscipienda probra hujus saeculi nequaquam ex parte aliqua etiam sua vota comitantur.
chunks.english: Sometimes obedience is led to detriment, when the mind is in no part accompanied even by its own wishes to receive the reproaches of this world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Debet obedientia et in adversis ex suo aliquid habere; et rursum in prosperis ex suo aliquid omnimodo non habere, quatenus et in adversis tanto sit gloriosior, quanto divino ordini etiam ex desiderio jungitur; et in prosperis tanto sit verior, quanto a praesenti ipsa, quam divinitus percipit, gloria funditus ex mente separatur.
chunks.english: Obedience ought both in adversities to have something of its own, and on the other hand in prosperity to have nothing at all of its own, so that both in adversities it may be the more glorious to the degree that it is joined also by desire to the divine order, and in prosperity it may be the more true to the degree that it is utterly separated in mind from the present glory itself which it receives from God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Num. 28.) Ab omnipotente Domino obedientia usque ad mortem servanda praecipitur, sicut Veritas ait: Non possum ego a me ipso facere quidquam, sed sicut audio judico (Joan., V, 30). Ipse si sicut audit judicat, tunc etiam obedit Patri, cum Judex hujus venerit saeculi.
chunks.english: By the almighty Lord, obedience is commanded to be kept even unto death, as Truth says in the Gospel of John, chapter five, verse thirty: I cannot of myself do anything, but as I hear I judge. If he judges as he hears, then he also obeys the Father even when he comes as Judge of this world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ne nobis usque ad praesentis vitae terminum obedientia laboriosa appareat, Redemptor noster indicat, qui hanc, etiam cum Judex venerit, servat. Quid ergo mirum si peccator homo obedientiae in praesentis vitae brevitate se subjicit, quando hanc Mediator Dei et hominum, et cum obedientes remunerat, non relinquit.
chunks.english: Lest obedience appear laborious to us up to the end of the present life, our Redeemer shows, who keeps it even when he comes as Judge. What wonder is it therefore if sinful man subjects himself to obedience in the brevity of the present life, when the Mediator of God and men, even while he rewards the obedient, does not abandon it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Num. 29.) Sciendum est nunquam per obedientiam malum fieri, aliquando autem debet per obedientiam bonum quod agitur intermitti; neque enim mala in paradiso arbor exstitit, quam Deus homini ne contingeret interdixit.
chunks.english: We must know that evil is never done through obedience, but sometimes good that is being done should be omitted through obedience. For there was not an evil tree in paradise which God forbade man to touch.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed ut melius per obedientiae meritum homo bene conditus cresceret, dignum fuerat ut hunc etiam a bono prohiberet; quatenus tanto verius hoc quod ageret virtus esset, quanto et a bono cessans Auctori suo se subditum humilius exhiberet.
chunks.english: But that man, well created, might better grow through the merit of obedience, it was fitting that God should forbid him even from good, so that what he did would be all the more truly virtue, the more humbly he showed himself subject to his Creator by ceasing even from good.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Notandum nobis summopere est quod primis hominibus a Domino dictum est: Ex omni ligno paradisi edite, de ligno autem scientiae boni et mali ne tetigeritis (Gen. II, 16). Qui enim ab uno quolibet bono subjectos vetat, necesse est ut multa concedat, ne obedientis mens funditus intereat, si a bonis omnibus penitus repulsa jejunat.
chunks.english: We must especially note that it was said by the Lord to the first humans in the book of Genesis, chapter two, verse sixteen: From every tree of paradise you may eat, but from the tree of knowledge of good and evil do not touch. For he who forbids subjects from any one good must grant many things, lest the mind of the obedient utterly perish if, completely repelled from all goods, it starves.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: (Num. 28.) Scriptum est in libro Salomonis: Melior est obedientia quam victimae (I Reg. XV, 22). Obedientia quippe victimis jure praeponitur, quia per victimas aliena caro, per obedientiam vero voluntas propria mactatur.
chunks.english: It is written in the first book of Samuel, chapter fifteen, verse twenty-two: Obedience is better than sacrifices. Indeed, obedience is rightly preferred to sacrifices, because through sacrifices the flesh of another is slain, but through obedience one's own will is slain.
chunks.notes: The text attributes this to the book of Solomon, but it is from 1 Samuel (1 Kings in some traditions).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tanto igitur quisque Deum citius placat, quanto ante ejus oculos, repressa arbitrii sui superbia, gladio praecepti se immolat. Ariolandi peccatum inobedientia dicitur, ut quanta sit virtus obedientiae demonstretur.
chunks.english: Therefore one placates God all the sooner the more he sacrifices himself before his eyes by the sword of precept, with the pride of his own judgment repressed. Disobedience is called the sin of divination, so that how great is the virtue of obedience may be demonstrated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex adverso ergo melius ostenditur quid de ejus laude sentiatur. Si enim quasi peccatum ariolandi est repugnare, et quasi scelus idololatriae nolle acquiescere, sola obedientia est quae fidei meritum possidet, qua sine quisque infidelis convincitur, etiamsi fidelis esse videatur.
chunks.english: From the opposite therefore it is better shown what is felt about its praise. For if to resist is like the sin of divination, and to refuse to comply is like the crime of idolatry, obedience alone possesses the merit of faith, without which anyone is proven unfaithful, even if he seems to be faithful.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Salomonem in ostensione obedientiae dicitur: Vir obediens loquitur victorias (Prov. XXI, 28). Vir quippe obediens victorias loquitur, quia dum alienae voci humiliter subdimur, nosmetipsos in corde superamus.
chunks.english: Through Solomon in the showing of obedience it is said in Proverbs, chapter twenty-one, verse twenty-eight: The obedient man speaks of victories. For the obedient man speaks of victories, because while we humbly submit to another's voice, we overcome ourselves in our heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Virtutem obedientiae Veritas commendans, ait: Eum, qui venit ad me, non ejiciam foras; quia de coelo descendi, non ut faciam voluntatem meam, sed voluntatem ejus qui misit me (Joan., VI, 37). Quid enim? Si suam faceret, eos qui ad se veniunt, repulisset? Quis nesciat, quod voluntas Filii a Patris voluntate non discrepet?
chunks.english: Truth commending the virtue of obedience says in the Gospel of John, chapter six, verse thirty-seven: Him who comes to me I will not cast out, because I came down from heaven, not to do my own will, but the will of him who sent me. For what? If he did his own will, would he have repelled those coming to him? Who does not know that the will of the Son does not differ from the Father's will?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed quoniam primus homo, quia suam facere voluntatem voluit, a paradisi gaudio exivit (Gen. III, 24), secundus ad redemptionem veniens hominum, dum voluntatem se Patris, et non suam, facere ostendit, permanere nos intus per obedientiae meritum docuit.
chunks.english: But because the first man, because he wished to do his own will, went out from the joy of paradise according to the book of Genesis, chapter three, verse twenty-four, the second, coming for the redemption of men, while showing that he does the Father's will and not his own, taught us to remain within through the merit of obedience.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum Christus Dominus non suam, sed Patris voluntatem facit, quia dum exemplo suo nos obedientiae subjicit, eos qui ad se veniunt foras non ejicit, et viam nobis egressionis claudit.
chunks.english: When Christ the Lord does not his own will but the Father's, because while by his example he subjects us to obedience, he does not cast out those who come to him, and he closes to us the way of departure.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXII. De verecundia.
chunks.english: Chapter 32: On Modesty

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verecundas mentes aliquando ad meliorem vitam exhortatio lenis et modesta componit, quia plerumque ad eorum conversionem sufficit quod eis doctor mala sua saltim leniter ad memoriam reducit (Reg. Past., part. III, c. 7).
chunks.english: Sometimes gentle and modest exhortation arranges modest minds toward a better life, because often for their conversion it is enough that the teacher brings their evils gently to memory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque major profectus verecundis adjicitur, si hoc quod in eis reprehenditur quasi ex latere tangatur. Verecundantem quippe plebem per prophetam Dominus refovet, dicens: Confusionis adolescentiae tuae oblivisceris, et opprobrium viduitatis tuae non recordaberis, quia dominabitur tui, qui fecit te (Isai. LIV, 4).
chunks.english: Often greater progress is given to the modest if what is reproved in them is touched on as it were from the side. For the Lord restores the modest people through the prophet, saying in Isaiah, chapter fifty-four, verse four: You will forget the shame of your youth, and will not remember the reproach of your widowhood, because he who made you will rule over you.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Paulus apostolus, egregius scilicet praedicator, culpas verecundantium quasi compatiens reprehendit, dicens: Gavisus sum in Domino vehementer, quoniam tandem aliquando refloruistis pro me sentire, sicut et sentiebatis: occupati enim eratis (Philipp. IV, 10).
chunks.english: The apostle Paul, an excellent preacher, reproves the faults of the modest as if sympathizing, saying in his letter to the Philippians, chapter four, verse ten: I rejoiced in the Lord greatly, that now at last you have revived your concern for me, as indeed you were concerned but were hindered.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Egit itaque doctor egregius ut verecundantium culpas medicinali benignitate sanaret, dum eorum negligentias sermo mollior temperando velaret.
chunks.english: The excellent teacher thus acted to heal the faults of the modest with medicinal kindness, while a gentler speech veiled their negligences by tempering them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut verecundia laudabilis est in malo, ita reprehensibilis est in bono (Lib. I, in Ezech., homil. 10, n. 17). Erubescere enim malum, sapientiae est; bonum vero erubescere, fatuitatis.
chunks.english: Just as modesty is praiseworthy in evil, so it is blameworthy in good. For to be ashamed of evil is wisdom; but to be ashamed of good is foolishness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde scriptum est: Qui me erubescit, et meos sermones, hunc Filius hominis erubescet, cum venerit in majestate sua (Luc. IX, 26). Quidam sapiens ait: Est confusio adducens gloriam (Eccli. IV, 25). Qui enim verecundans erubescit, poenitendo mala quae fecit, ad vitae libertatem pervenit; qui vero erubescit bona facere, a statu rectitudinis cadit, atque ad damnationem tendit.
chunks.english: From which it is written in the Gospel of Luke, chapter nine, verse twenty-six: Whoever is ashamed of me and my words, of him the Son of Man will be ashamed when he comes in his majesty. A certain wise man says in the book of Sirach, chapter four, verse twenty-five: There is a shame that brings glory. For he who in modesty is ashamed, repenting the evils he has done, arrives at the freedom of life. But he who is ashamed to do good falls from the state of righteousness and tends toward condemnation.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXIII. De misericordia et pietate.
chunks.english: Chapter 33: On Mercy and Piety

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ille bene agit quae pia sunt, qui scit prius servare quae justa sunt, quatenus collatus in proximos rivus misericordiae, de justitiae fonte ducatur (Moral. lib. XIX, c. 23, n. 38).
chunks.english: He acts well in what is pious who knows first to preserve what is just, so that the stream of mercy poured out on neighbors may be drawn from the fountain of justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Multi proximis quasi opera misericordiae impendunt, sed injustitiae facta non deserunt; qui si veraciter proximis misericordiam facere studerent, sibi ipsis prius debuerant juste vivendo misereri.
chunks.english: Many bestow on neighbors as it were works of mercy, but do not abandon deeds of injustice. If they truly strove to show mercy to neighbors, they ought first to show mercy to themselves by living justly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde scriptum est: Miserere animae tuae placens Deo (Eccli. XXX, 24). Qui misereri vult proximo, a se trahat necesse est originem miserendi. Scriptum namque est: Diliges proximum tuum sicut teipsum (Matth. XIX, 19).
chunks.english: From which it is written in the book of Sirach, chapter thirty, verse twenty-four: Have mercy on your own soul, pleasing God. Whoever wishes to show mercy to his neighbor must draw the origin of mercy from himself. For it is written in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter nineteen, verse nineteen: You shall love your neighbor as yourself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quomodo ergo alteri miserendo pius est, qui adhuc injuste vivendo fit impius sibimetipsi? Ad exhibendam misericordiam, ut indigentibus plene exterius valeat impendi, duo sibi necessaria congruunt, id est, homo qui praebeat, et res quae praebeatur. Sed longe incomparabiliter melior est homo quam res.
chunks.english: How therefore is he pious in showing mercy to another, who still by living unjustly becomes impious to himself? For showing mercy, so that it may be fully bestowed outwardly on the needy, two necessary things come together, that is, the man who gives and the thing that is given. But man is far and incomparably better than the thing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde per quemdam sapientem dicitur: Qui sibi nequam est, cui bonus erit (Eccli. XIV, 5)? In ipso misericordiae opere plus solet apud internum Judicem animus pensari quam factum.
chunks.english: From which a certain wise man says in the book of Sirach, chapter fourteen, verse five: He who is wicked to himself, to whom will he be good? In the very work of mercy, the disposition is usually weighed more by the internal Judge than the deed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et notandum quod beatus Job caeci oculum fuisse se asserit, pedem claudi (Job, XXIX, 15); haec enim dicens, profecto indicat quia et illi per semetipsum manum praebuerat, et hunc portando sustinebat. Ex qua re colligitur super egenos ac debiles quantum misericordiae illius viscera fundebantur.
chunks.english: And it should be noted that blessed Job asserts he was the eye of the blind, the foot of the lame, according to the book of Job, chapter twenty-nine, verse fifteen. For saying these things, he clearly indicates that he offered his hand to the one through himself, and sustained the other by carrying him. From which it is gathered how much the heart of his mercy poured out upon the needy and weak.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem beatus Job dicit: Pater eram pauperum (Job, XXIX, 16; Moral. l. XIX, c. 24, n. 41). Non ergo se patronum, vel proximum, vel adjutorem pauperum, sed patrem fuisse testatur. Quia nimirum magno charitatis officio studium misericordiae vertit in effectum naturae, ut eos quasi filios cerneret per amorem, quibus quasi pater praeerat per protectionem.
chunks.english: The same blessed Job says in the book of Job, chapter twenty-nine, verse sixteen: I was a father to the poor. He therefore testifies that he was not their patron, or neighbor, or helper of the poor, but their father. Because by the great duty of charity he turned the zeal of mercy into the effect of nature, so that he viewed them as children through love, over whom he presided as a father through protection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quia vis misericordiae beati Job naturam fuerat imitata, patrem se pauperum fuisse commemorat; ubi etiam subdidit: Et causam quam nesciebam diligentissime investigabam (Job, XXIX, 16; Moral. l. XIX, c. 25, n. 42).
chunks.english: Because the force of mercy of blessed Job had imitated nature, he recalls that he was a father to the poor. Where he also added in the book of Job, chapter twenty-nine, verse sixteen: And the cause that I did not know I investigated most diligently.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In quibus videlicet verbis pensanda sunt omnia, quam distincte narrantur, quod nulla ab eo merces praetermittitur. Justus quippe est in actionibus suis, pius in infirmitatibus proximorum, strenuous in negotiis pauperum.
chunks.english: In which words all things should be weighed, how distinctly they are narrated, that no reward is overlooked by him. For he is just in his actions, pious in the weaknesses of neighbors, vigorous in the affairs of the poor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui aeternae retributionis bona cogitat, necesse est ut misericorditer ad omnem se causam secuturae mercedis extendat. Hinc enim per Salomonem dicitur: Qui Deum timet, nihil negligit (Eccle. VII, 19). Hinc Paulus ait: Ad omne opus bonum parati (II Tim. II, 21).
chunks.english: Whoever thinks of the good things of eternal retribution must mercifully extend himself to every cause of reward to follow. For through Solomon it is said in Ecclesiastes, chapter seven, verse nineteen: He who fears God neglects nothing. From this Paul says in his second letter to Timothy, chapter two, verse twenty-one: Prepared for every good work.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXIV De eleemosynis.
chunks.english: Chapter 34: On Almsgiving

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui indigenti proximo exteriorem substantiam praebet, sed vitam suam a nequitia non custodit, rem suam Deo tribuit, et se peccato. Hoc quod minus est obtulit Auctori, et hoc quod majus est servavit iniquitati (Moral. lib. XVIIII, c. 25, n. 38).
chunks.english: He who provides exterior substance to a needy neighbor, but does not guard his own life from wickedness, offers his possessions to God and himself to sin. What is less he has offered to the Creator, and what is greater he has preserved for iniquity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam divites elati inferiores opprimunt, et aliena rapiunt; et tamen quasi quaedam aliis largiuntur; et cum multos deprimant, aliquando quibusdam opem defensionis ferunt, et pro iniquitatibus, quas nunquam deserunt, dare pretium videntur (Moral. lib. XII, c. 51, n. 57).
chunks.english: Sometimes proud rich men oppress their inferiors and seize what belongs to others, and yet as it were bestow certain things on others; and while they oppress many, they sometimes bring the help of defense to some, and they seem to pay a price for the iniquities which they never abandon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quoties post culpam eleemosynam facimus, quasi pro pravis actibus pretium damus. Unde et per Prophetam de eo qui haec non agit dicitur: Non dabit Deo propitiationem suam, nec pretium redemptionis animae suae (Psal. XLVIII, 8, 9).
chunks.english: Whenever we give alms after sin, we pay a price as it were for wicked deeds. From which also it is said through the prophet concerning him who does not do these things in Psalm forty-eight, verses eight and nine: He will not give God his propitiation, nor the price of redemption for his soul.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc eleemosynae pretium nos a culpis liberat, cum perpetrata plangimus, et abdicamus. Nam qui et semper peccare vult, et quasi semper eleemosynam largiri, frustra pretium tribuit, quia non redimit animam, quam a vitiis non compescit.
chunks.english: Then the price of almsgiving frees us from sins when we lament and renounce what we have done. For he who both always wishes to sin and as it were always to give alms, pays a price in vain, because he does not redeem a soul which he does not restrain from vices.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Eleemosynae impensio superbum divitem redimere non valet, quam perpetrata simul rapina pauperis ante Dei oculos ascendere non permittit. Unde per Eliphaz dicitur: Non credat frustra errore deceptus, quod aliquo pretio redimendus sit (Job. XV, 31).
chunks.english: The expenditure of almsgiving cannot redeem a proud rich man, whom the plunder of the poor simultaneously perpetrated does not permit to ascend before God's eyes. From which it is said through Eliphaz in the book of Job, chapter fifteen, verse thirty-one: Let him not believe, deceived by vain error, that he is to be redeemed by any price.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe superbi divites, cum eleemosynam tribuunt, non hanc pro aeternae vitae desiderio, sed pro extendenda vita temporali largiuntur. Mortem se posse differre dationibus credunt, sed nequaquam obtinere ex impenso munere valent, ut finem debitum evadant.
chunks.english: Often proud rich men, when they give alms, bestow it not for the desire of eternal life but for extending temporal life. They believe they can put off death by gifts, but they can in no way obtain from the gift expended that they may escape the due end.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Evangelio Veritas ait: Nesciat sinistra tua quid faciat dextera tua (Matth. VI, 3; Reg. Past., p. 3, c. 20); id est, piae dispensationi nequaquam se gloria vitae praesentis admisceat; sed opus rectitudinis appetitio ignoret favoris.
chunks.english: In the Gospel of Matthew, chapter six, verse three, Truth says: Let not your left hand know what your right hand does; that is, let the glory of present life in no way mingle with pious dispensation; but let the desire for favor not know the work of righteousness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque multa homines pauperibus largiuntur, non quia eosdem pauperes diligunt, sed quia si minime tribuant, iram Judicis superni formidant; qui si Deum non metuerent, quae habent dare noluissent.
chunks.english: Often men bestow many things on the poor, not because they love those same poor, but because if they give least, they fear the wrath of the supreme Judge; who, if they did not fear God, would not have wished to give what they have.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt qui sua misericorditer tribuunt, ne cogitatione tumida super eos se quibus terrena largiuntur extollant; et ne idcirco se meliores aestiment, quia contineri per se caeteros vident.
chunks.english: Those who bestow their possessions mercifully must be warned not to exalt themselves with proud thought above those to whom they give earthly things, and not to consider themselves better because they see others supported by themselves.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui possessa misericorditer tribuunt, a coelesti Domino dispensatores se positos subsidiorum temporalium agnoscant; et tanto humiliter praebeant, quanto et aliena esse intelligunt quae dispensant.
chunks.english: Those who mercifully bestow their possessions should recognize that they are placed by the heavenly Lord as stewards of temporal subsidies; and let them give all the more humbly, as they understand that what they dispense belongs to another.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ne in benefactis largitores immoderatius gaudeant, audiant quod scriptum est: Cum feceritis omnia quae praecepta sunt vobis, dicite: Servi inutiles sumus, quod debuimus facere fecimus (Luc. XVII, 10).
chunks.english: Lest givers rejoice excessively in good deeds, let them hear what is written in the Gospel of Luke, chapter seventeen, verse ten: When you have done all that is commanded you, say: We are unprofitable servants; what we ought to have done, we have done.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ac ne largitatem tristitia corrumpat, audiant quod scriptum est: Hilarem datorem diligit Deus (II Cor. IX, 7). Ne largitores eleemosynarum impensae gratiae vicissitudinem requirant, audiant quod scriptum est: Cum facis prandium, aut coenam, noli vocare amicos tuos, neque fratres tuos, neque cognatos, neque vicinos divites, ne forte et ipsi te invitent, et fiat tibi retributio; sed cum facis convivium, voca pauperes, debiles, claudos, caecos; et beatus eris, quia non habent unde retribuere tibi (Luc. XIV, 22 seq.).
chunks.english: And lest sadness corrupt generosity, let them hear what is written in Paul's second letter to the Corinthians, chapter nine, verse seven: God loves a cheerful giver. Lest givers of alms seek reciprocation of grace expended, let them hear what is written in the Gospel of Luke, chapter fourteen, verse twenty-two and following: When you make a dinner or supper, do not call your friends, nor your brothers, nor your relatives, nor rich neighbors, lest perhaps they also invite you, and recompense be made to you; but when you make a feast, call the poor, the weak, the lame, the blind; and you will be blessed, because they do not have the means to repay you.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ne quae praebenda sunt citius sero praebeant largitores, audiant quod scriptum est: Ne dicas amico tuo: Vade, et revertere, et cras dabo tibi, cum statim possis dare (Proverb. III, 28).
chunks.english: Lest givers provide too late what must be provided, let them hear what is written in Proverbs, chapter three, verse twenty-eight: Do not say to your friend: Go, and return, and tomorrow I will give to you, when you can give immediately.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ne sub obtentu largitatis ea quae possident inutiliter distributores spargant, audiant quod scriptum est: Sudet in manu tua eleemosyna. Et ne, cum multa necesse sint, pauca largiantur, audiant quod scriptum est: Qui parce seminat, parce et metet (II Cor. IX, 6).
chunks.english: Lest distributors under the pretext of generosity scatter uselessly what they possess, let them hear what is written: Let alms sweat in your hand. And lest, when many things are necessary, they give few, let them hear what is written in Paul's second letter to the Corinthians, chapter nine, verse six: He who sows sparingly will also reap sparingly.
chunks.notes: The phrase 'Sudet in manu tua eleemosyna' appears to be from Sirach/Ecclesiasticus 12:3, though not quoted verbatim.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ne, cum pauca oportet, plurima praebeant largitores, et ipsi postmodum minime inopiam tolerantes, ad impatientiam erumpant, audiant quod scriptum est: Non ut aliis sit remissio, vobis autem tribulatio, sed ex aequalitate vestra abundantia illorum inopiam suppleat, et illorum abundantia vestrae inopiae sit supplementum (II Cor. VIII, 13, 14).
chunks.english: Lest, when few things are fitting, givers provide very many, and they themselves afterward barely tolerating need break out into impatience, let them hear what is written in Paul's second letter to the Corinthians, chapter eight, verses thirteen and fourteen: Not that others may have relief and you tribulation, but by equality let your abundance supply their need, and let their abundance be a supplement to your need.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum igitur dantis mens inopiam ferre nescit, si multa sibi subtrahit, occasionem contra se impatientiae exquirit. Prius praeparandus est patientiae animus, et tunc, aut multa sunt, aut cuncta largienda, ne dum minus aequanimiter inopia irruens fertur, et praemissae largitatis merces pereat, et adhuc mentem deterius murmuratio subsequens, perdat.
chunks.english: When therefore the mind of the giver does not know how to bear need, if he takes away much from himself, he seeks an occasion of impatience against himself. First the mind must be prepared for patience, and then either many or all things are to be given, lest while the irrupting need is borne less equally, both the reward of previous generosity perish, and still worse, subsequent murmuring destroy the mind.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ne omnino distributores nihil eis praebeant quibus conferre aliquid parum debent, audiant quod scriptum est: Omni petenti te tribue (Luc. VI, 30). Et ne saltim aliquid praebeant quibus omnino conferre nihil debent, audiant quod scriptum est: Da bono, et non receperis peccatorem, bene fac humili, et non dederis impio (Eccli. XII, 4).
chunks.english: Lest distributors provide nothing at all to those to whom they ought to give something small, let them hear what is written in the Gospel of Luke, chapter six, verse thirty: Give to everyone who asks you. And lest they provide even something to those to whom they ought to give nothing at all, let them hear what is written in the book of Sirach, chapter twelve, verse four: Give to the good, and do not receive the sinner; do good to the humble, and do not give to the impious.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per quemdam dicitur sapientem: Panem tuum et vinum super sepulturam justi constitue, et noli ex eo manducare et bibere cum peccatoribus (Tob. IV, 17). Panem enim, et vinum suum peccatoribus praebet, qui iniquis subsidia, pro eo quod sunt iniqui, impendit.
chunks.english: It is said by a certain wise man in the book of Tobit, chapter four, verse seventeen: Set your bread and wine upon the burial of a just man, and do not eat and drink of it with sinners. For he gives his bread and wine to sinners who bestows subsidies on the wicked because they are wicked.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnulli hujus mundi divites, cum fame crucientur Christi pauperes, effusis largitatibus nutriunt histriones. Qui vero indigenti etiam peccatori panem suum, non quia peccator, sed quia homo est, tribuit, nimirum non peccatorem, sed justum nutrit, quia in illo non culpam, sed naturam diligit.
chunks.english: Some rich men of this world, while Christ's poor are crucified by hunger, nourish actors with lavish generosity.
