But he who gives his bread even to a needy sinner, not because he is a sinner but because he is a man, clearly nourishes not the sinner but the just, because in him he loves not the fault but the nature.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui sua misericorditer largiuntur, sollicite custodire studeant, ne cum commissa peccata eleemosynis redimunt, adhuc redimenda committant; ne venalem Dei justitiam aestiment, si cum curant pro peccatis nummos tribuere, arbitrentur se posse inulte peccare.
chunks.english: Those who mercifully bestow their possessions should carefully strive to guard, lest when they redeem committed sins with alms, they still commit sins to be redeemed; lest they consider God's justice for sale, if when they care to pay money for sins, they think they can sin with impunity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plus est anima quam esca, et corpus, scilicet, quam vestimentum (Matth. VI, 25). Qui ergo escam atque vestimentum pauperibus largitur, sed tamen animae vel corporis iniquitate polluitur, quod minus est justitiae obtulit, et quod majus est praebuit culpae. Sua enim Deo dedit, et se diabolo praebuit.
chunks.english: The soul is more than food, and the body, that is, more than clothing, as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter six, verse twenty-five says. Therefore he who gives food and clothing to the poor, but nevertheless is polluted by the iniquity of soul or body, has offered to justice what is less, and has given to fault what is greater. For he has given his possessions to God, and given himself to the devil.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sollicite perpendere studeamus, quia et eos quos nunc inopes cernimus, abundantes quandoque videbimus; et qui abundantes aspicimur, si largiri negligimus, quandoque inopes erimus (Moral. lib. XXI, c. 19, n. 30).
chunks.english: Let us carefully strive to consider, because both those whom we now see as needy, we will sometime see as abundant; and we who are seen as abundant, if we neglect to give, will sometime be needy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui nunc temporale subsidium pauperi tribuit, ab eo postmodum perpetua recepturus, ut ita dicam, quasi ad frugem terram excolit, quae quod acceperit uberius reddit. Restat ergo ut nunquam elatio surgat ex eleemosyna, quando videlicet dives ex eo quod pauperi tribuit, agit ut in perpetuum pauper non sit.
chunks.english: He who now gives temporal subsidy to the poor, to receive from him afterward perpetual things, so to speak, cultivates the land as it were for fruit, which returns more abundantly what it has received. It remains therefore that pride never arise from almsgiving, when the rich man, from what he gives to the poor, acts so that he may not be poor forever.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Honorate quos pauperes cernitis, et quos foris conspicitis despectos saeculi, intus arbitramini amicos Dei (Lib. II, in Evang., homil. 40, n. 12). Cum his participamini quod habetis, ut quandoque dignentur vobiscum comparticipari quod habent (Moral. lib. X, c. 6, n. 8).
chunks.english: Honor those whom you see as poor, and those whom you see outwardly as despised by the world, inwardly consider friends of God. Share with them what you have, so that sometime they may deign to share with you what they have.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Electus quisque sic quae possidet tribuat, quatenus quanta sit largitas remunerantis attendat. Ne cum terrena largitur, suam plusquam necesse est inopiam cogitet, et in oblatione muneris hilaritatis lumen tristitia obscuret.
chunks.english: Let each elect person bestow what he possesses in such a way that he may pay attention to how great is the generosity of the one rewarding. Lest when he bestows earthly things, he think of his own need more than necessary, and sadness obscure the light of cheerfulness in the offering of the gift.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Suffering with the Elect and Compassion for Neighbors
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXV. De passione electorum et compassione proximorum.
chunks.english: Chapter 35: On the Passion of the Elect and Compassion for Neighbors

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sancti viri tribulationum bello deprehensi, uno eodemque tempore alios ferientes, atque alios suadentes feriunt (Moral. lib. III, c. 21, n. 39). Illis opponunt scutum patientiae, istis jacula intorquent doctrinae.
chunks.english: Holy men caught in the battle of tribulations, at one and the same time strike some while persuading others. Against some they oppose the shield of patience; against others they hurl the spears of teaching.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad utrumque pugnandi modum mira virtutis arte electi viri se erigunt, quatenus et intus sapienter doceant, et foras fortiter adversa contemnant; hos docentes corrigant, illos tolerantes premant.
chunks.english: For both modes of fighting, elect men raise themselves with wondrous art of virtue, so that both inwardly they may wisely teach, and outwardly they may bravely despise adversities; correcting these by teaching, pressing down those by tolerating.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Electi viri insurgentes hostes patiendo despiciunt, infirmantes vero cives compatiendo ad salutem reducunt. Illis resistunt, ne et alios subtrahant; istis metuunt, ne vitam rectitudinis funditus perdant.
chunks.english: Elect men despise rising enemies by enduring them, but bring back failing citizens to salvation by compassion. They resist some, lest they also withdraw others; they fear for others, lest they utterly lose the life of righteousness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Videamus castrorum Dei militem Paulum apostolum contra utrumque praeliantem (Ibid., n. 40). Ait namque: Foris pugnae, intus timores (II Cor. VII, 5). Enumerat bella, quae extrinsecus tolerat, dicens: Periculis fluminum, periculis latronum, periculis ex genere, periculis ex gentibus, periculis in civitate, periculis in solitudine, periculis in mari, periculis in falsis fratribus (II Cor. XI, 26).
chunks.english: Let us see Paul the apostle, soldier of God's camp, fighting against both. For he says in his second letter to the Corinthians, chapter seven, verse five: Outward battles, inward fears. He enumerates the battles which he endures outwardly, saying in his second letter to the Corinthians, chapter eleven, verse twenty-six: In dangers of rivers, in dangers of robbers, in dangers from my own people, in dangers from the Gentiles, in dangers in the city, in dangers in the wilderness, in dangers at sea, in dangers among false brethren.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In hoc spirituali bello quae contra adversarium Paulus spicula intorqueat, adjungat: In labore, et aerumna, in vigiliis multis; in fame et siti, in jejuniis multis, in frigore et nuditate (Ibid.).
chunks.english: In this spiritual battle what spears Paul hurls against the adversary, he adds in the same passage: In labor and hardship, in many sleepless nights; in hunger and thirst, in many fastings, in cold and nakedness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Inter multimoda certamina deprehensus Vas electionis dicat, quanto vigiliarum munimine etiam castra custodiat. Ait enim. Praeter illa quae extrinsecus sunt, instantia mea quotidiana, sollicitudo omnium Ecclesiarum.
chunks.english: Caught among manifold contests, let the Vessel of Election tell with what fortification of vigils he also guards the camp. For he says in the same passage: Besides those things that are outward, my daily pressing concern, the care of all the churches.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce et bella fortiter suscipit, et tuendis se per compassionem proximis misericorditer impendit. Narrat mala quae patitur, subjungit bona quae impertitur. Pensetur cujus laboris sit Paulum apostolum uno eodemque tempore et foris adversa tolerare, et intus infirma protegere.
chunks.english: Behold, he both undertakes battles bravely and spends himself mercifully in protecting neighbors through compassion. He narrates the evils he suffers; he adds the good things he imparts. Let it be considered what labor it is for the apostle Paul at one and the same time both to tolerate adversities outwardly and to protect the weak inwardly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Foris pugnas patitur, quia verberibus scinditur, catenis ligatur; intus metum tolerat, quia passionem suam, non sibi, sed discipulis obesse formidat. Aliorum casus Apostolus in propria passione metuebat, ne dum et ipsum discipuli afflictum pro fide verberibus agnoscerent, fideles se profiteri recusarent.
chunks.english: Outwardly he suffers battles, because he is torn by blows, bound with chains; inwardly he tolerates fear, because he fears that his suffering may harm not himself but his disciples. The Apostle feared the fall of others in his own suffering, lest while disciples recognized him himself afflicted by blows for the faith, they might refuse to profess themselves faithful.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde eisdem scribit, dicens: Nemo moveatur in tribulationibus istis. Ipsi enim scitis quod in hoc positi sumus (I Thess. III, 3). O immensa charitatis viscera Pauli apostoli! despicit passiones quas ipse patitur, et curat ne quid pravae persuasionis discipuli in corde patiantur.
chunks.english: From which he writes to them, saying in his first letter to the Thessalonians, chapter three, verse three: Let no one be moved by these tribulations. For you yourselves know that we are appointed to this. O immense heart of charity of the apostle Paul! He despises the sufferings which he himself endures, and cares that disciples may not suffer anything of wicked persuasion in their heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In se contemnit vulnera corporis, et in aliis vulnera medetur cordis. Habent hoc justi proprium, ut in dolore positi tribulationis suae, curam non deserant utilitatis alienae; et cum de se adversa patientes dolent, aliis necessaria docentes praevident, et quasi percussi quidam magni medici aegrotant.
chunks.english: In himself he despises wounds of the body, and in others he heals wounds of the heart. The just have this as their own, that placed in the pain of their tribulation, they do not abandon care for others' usefulness; and while suffering adversities about themselves they grieve, they foresee by teaching what is necessary for others, and as it were certain great physicians grow sick when struck.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ipsi tolerant scissuras vulneris, et aliis proferunt medicamenta sanitatis. Valde autem minoris laboris est, aut docere, cum nihil toleras, aut tolerare, cum nihil doces. Sancti viri ad utrasque passionum se vel compassionum pugnas solerter extendunt; et cum tribulationibus fortasse feriuntur, sic exteriora bella suscipiunt, ut sollicite cogitent ne proximorum interiora lacerentur.
chunks.english: They themselves tolerate the wounds of injury, and to others they offer medicines of health. But it is of far less labor either to teach when you tolerate nothing, or to tolerate when you teach nothing. Holy men carefully extend themselves to both battles of sufferings or compassions; and when perhaps they are struck by tribulations, they undertake exterior battles in such a way that they carefully think lest the interior things of neighbors be torn.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum viri fortes in acie assistunt, illic jaculis adversantia pectora feriunt; hinc scuto postpositos debiles tuentur. Atque ita utrobique velocitate circumspectionis invigilant, quatenus et ante se audaces confodiant, et post se trepidos a vulnere defendant.
chunks.english: While brave men stand in battle line, there they strike opposing breasts with spears; here with shield they protect the weak placed behind. And thus on both sides they watch with speed of circumspection, so that both before themselves they pierce the bold, and behind themselves they defend the trembling from wounds.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXVI. Quid sit jubilum.
chunks.english: Chapter 36: What Jubilation Is

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Jubilum dicitur, quando ineffabile gaudium mente concipitur, quod nec abscondi possit, nec sermonibus aperiri, et tamen quibusdam motibus proditur, quamvis nullis proprietatibus exprimatur (Moral., lib. XXIV, c. 6, n. 10).
chunks.english: Jubilation is spoken of when ineffable joy is conceived in the mind, which can neither be hidden nor opened in words, and nevertheless is betrayed by certain movements, although it is expressed by no specific terms.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: David propheta intuens electorum animas tantum gaudium mente concipere, quantum sermone non valent aperire, ait: Beatus populus, qui scit jubilationem (Psal. LXXXVIII, 16). Non enim ait, qui loquitur, sed qui scit, quia sciri quidem jubilatio intellectu potest, sed dictu exprimi non potest.
chunks.english: The prophet David, seeing the souls of the elect conceive as much joy in mind as they cannot reveal in speech, says in Psalm eighty-eight, verse sixteen: Blessed is the people who know jubilation. For he does not say who speak it, but who know it, because jubilation can indeed be known by understanding, but cannot be expressed by speaking.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per jubilum sentitur quod ultra sensum est; et cum vix ad contemplandum sufficiat conscientia sentientis, quomodo ad exprimendum sufficit lingua dicentis?
chunks.english: Through jubilation is felt what is beyond feeling; and when the consciousness of the one feeling scarcely suffices for contemplating, how does the tongue of the one speaking suffice for expressing?

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXVII. De regni coelestis desiderio.
chunks.english: Chapter 37: On Desire for the Heavenly Kingdom

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magnus clamor sanctorum magnum est desiderium: tanto enim quisque minus clamat, quanto minus desiderat; et tanto majorem vocem in aures incircumscripti spiritus exprimit, quanto se in ejus desiderio plenius fundit (Moral. II, c. 7, n. 11).
chunks.english: The great cry of the saints is great desire, for one cries out less to the degree that he desires less; and he expresses a greater voice into the ears of the unlimited spirit to the degree that he pours himself out more fully in desire for him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum aliter moveri soleat mens quae petit, aliter quae petitur, et sanctorum animae ita in interni secreti sinu Deo inhaereant, ut inherendo quiescant, quomodo dicuntur petere quas ab interno motu constat nullatenus discrepare?
chunks.english: Since the mind that seeks is usually moved one way, and the mind that is sought another way, and the souls of the saints cling to God in the bosom of the internal secret in such a way that by clinging they rest, how are they said to seek when it is clear they in no way differ from his internal movement?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quomodo dicuntur petere quas et voluntatem Dei certum est et ea quae futura sunt non ignorare? Sed in ipso positae ab ipso aliquid petere dicuntur, non quo quidquam desiderent quod ab ejus quem cernunt voluntate discordat, sed quo mente ardentius inhaerent, eo etiam de ipso accipiunt, ut ab ipso petant quod eum facere velle noverunt.
chunks.english: How are they said to ask when it is certain they know both the will of God and the things that are to be? But placed in him they are said to ask something from him, not that they desire anything that differs from the will of him whom they see, but the more ardently they cling in mind, the more also they receive from him, so that they ask from him what they know he wishes to do.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nemo qui tardius auditur credat quod a superna cura negligitur (Moral., lib. XXVI, c. 19, n. 34). Saepe enim nostra desideria, quia celeriter non fiunt, exaudiuntur; et quod impleri concite petimus, ex ipsa melius tarditate prosperatur.
chunks.english: Let no one who is heard more slowly believe that he is neglected by heavenly care. For often our desires are heard because they are not done quickly; and what we ask to be fulfilled rapidly is made more prosperous by that very delay.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe vox nostra eo perficitur, quod differtur; et cum superficie tenus petitio negligitur, vota nostra altius in cogitationum radice complentur; sicut et semina messium gelu pressa solidantur, et quo ad superficiem tardius exeunt, eo ad frugem multipliciora consurgunt.
chunks.english: Often our voice is perfected by being deferred; and when the petition is neglected on the surface, our vows are fulfilled more deeply in the root of thoughts; just as seeds of harvests are solidified by the pressing of frost, and the more slowly they come to the surface, the more abundantly they rise to fruit.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Desideria nostra dilatione extenduntur ut proficiant, proficiunt ut ad hoc quod perceptura sunt convalescant, et exercitantur in certamine, ut majoribus cumulentur praemiis in retributione. Labor protrahitur pugnae, ut crescat corona victoriae.
chunks.english: Our desires are extended by delay so that they may profit, they profit so that they may grow strong for what they are about to receive, and they are exercised in struggle, so that they may be heaped with greater rewards in recompense. The labor of the fight is prolonged, so that the crown of victory may grow.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Suos ergo Dominus, cum velociter non exaudiat, quo repellere creditur, assidue trahit. Internus medicus peccatorum in nobis contagia, quae inesse medullitus reprobat, secat, et abscindit virus putredinis ferro tribulationis; eoque voces aegri audire dissimulat, quo aegritudinis finem procurat.
chunks.english: Therefore when the Lord does not hear his own quickly, by which he is believed to repel them, he continually draws them. The internal physician cuts away the contagions of sins in us, which he deeply disapproves to be within, and cuts off the poison of corruption with the blade of tribulation; and the more he pretends not to hear the voice of the sick, the more he procures the end of the sickness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: David propheta ait: Clamabo per diem, et non exaudies, in nocte, et non ad insipientiam mihi (Ps. XXI, 5). Ac si diceret: Nequaquam mihi ad insipientiam proficit quod die ac nocte clamantem me continue non exaudis, quia unde me in temporali tribulatione quasi deseris, et non exaudis, inde ad aeternam sapientiam plus erudis.
chunks.english: The prophet David says in Psalm twenty-one, verse five: I will cry by day, and you will not hear; in the night, and not to foolishness for me. As if he were saying: It profits me in no way toward foolishness that you do not continuously hear me crying day and night, because from where you as it were abandon me in temporal tribulation and do not hear, from there you more fully train me toward eternal wisdom.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Iterum Psalmista dicit: Adjutor in opportunitatibus in tribulatione (Psal. IX, 10). Tribulationem quippe dicturus, opportunitates praemisit, quia saepe et tribulatione conterimur, et tamen opportunum nondum est, ut ad desiderium ereptionis adjuvemur.
chunks.english: Again the Psalmist says in Psalm nine, verse ten: A helper in opportunities in tribulation. Indeed, being about to speak of tribulation, he put opportunities first, because often we are worn down by tribulation, and yet it is not yet opportune that we be helped toward the desire of deliverance.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXVIII. De bonorum concordia.
chunks.english: Chapter 38: On the Concord of the Good

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc charitas a jugo culpae liberos reddit, cum vicissim nos nostro per amorem servitio subjicit, cum et aliena bona nostra credimus, et nostra aliis quasi sua offerentes exhibemus (Reg. Past., part. III c. 22).
chunks.english: Then charity makes us free from the yoke of fault, when in turn it subjects us to our own service through love, when we both believe the goods of others to be ours, and exhibit our own to others offering them as their own.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Certissime sciant homines quia quantislibet virtutibus polleant, spiritales fieri nullatenus possunt, si uniri per concordiam proximis negligunt. Scriptum est: Fructus enim spiritus est, charitas, gaudium, pax (Galat. V, 22). Qui ergo servare pacem non curat, ferre fructum spiritus recusat. Unde idem Paulus ait: Cum sit inter vos zelus et contentio, nonne carnales estis? Hinc iterum dicit: Pacem sequimini cum omnibus, et sanctimoniam, sine qua nemo videbit Deum (I Cor. III, 3).
chunks.english: Let people know with absolute certainty that however many virtues they possess, they can in no way become spiritual if they neglect to be united in harmony with neighbors. It is written in Paul's letter to the Galatians, chapter five, verse twenty-two: For the fruit of the Spirit is charity, joy, and peace. Therefore whoever does not care to preserve peace refuses to bear the fruit of the Spirit. From this the same Paul says in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter three, verse three: When there is among you envy and contention, are you not carnal? From this again he says: Follow after peace with all, and holiness, without which no one will see God.
chunks.notes: The last citation appears to be from Hebrews 12:14, not 1 Corinthians 3:3.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXIX. De conservanda amicitia.
chunks.english: Chapter 39: On Preserving Friendship

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Amicus quasi animi custos vocatur (Moral. lib. XXVII, c. 15, n. 28). Unde non immerito quisquis custodire voluntatem Dei in praeceptis illius nititur, ejus amicus vocatur, sicut Veritas discipulis ait: Jam non dicam vos servos, sed amicos. Et iterum: Vos amici mei estis, si feceritis quae ego praecipio vobis (Joan. XV, 14, 15; Moral. lib. VII, c. 24, n. 29).
chunks.english: A friend is called as it were the guardian of the soul. From which not undeservedly whoever strives to guard the will of God in his precepts is called his friend, as Truth says to disciples in the Gospel of John, chapter fifteen, verses fourteen and fifteen: Now I will not call you servants, but friends. And again: You are my friends, if you do what I command you.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum quisque positus in prosperitate ut amicus diligitur, incertum valde est utrum prosperitas an persona diligatur. Amissio autem felicitatis interrogat vim dilectionis. Unde bene quidam sapiens dicit: Non agnoscitur in bonis amicus, et non absconditur in malis inimicus (Eccli. XII, 8).
chunks.english: When someone placed in prosperity is loved as a friend, it is very uncertain whether the prosperity or the person is loved. But the loss of happiness tests the strength of love. From which a certain wise man well says in the book of Sirach, chapter twelve, verse eight: The friend is not recognized in good things, and the enemy is not hidden in evil things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nec prosperitas amicum indicat, nec adversitas inimicum celat. Quia et ille saepe prosperitatis nostrae reverentia tegitur, et iste ex confidentia adversitatis aperitur.
chunks.english: Neither prosperity shows a friend, nor adversity hides an enemy. Because both the one is often covered by reverence for our prosperity, and the other is revealed by the confidence of adversity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qualiter amicitia sit tenenda Redemptor noster insinuat, qui in plerisque Scripturae suae sententiis, et amicos jubet diligi in se, et inimicos diligi propter se (Lib. II in Ev., hom. 27, n. 1). Ille enim veraciter charitatem habet, qui et amicum diligit in Deo, et inimicum diligit propter Deum.
chunks.english: How friendship is to be held our Redeemer makes known, who in very many opinions of his Scripture commands both that friends be loved in him and enemies be loved for his sake. For he truly has charity who both loves a friend in God and loves an enemy for God's sake.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XL. De legendi assiduitate.
chunks.english: Chapter 40: On Diligence in Reading

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptura sacra lectoris sui animum ad coelestem patriam vocat, atque a terrenis desideriis ad superna amplectenda cor legentis immutat; dictisque obscurioribus exercet fortes, et parvulis humili sermone blanditur (Moral. lib. XX, c. 1, n. 1).
chunks.english: Sacred Scripture calls the soul of its reader to the heavenly homeland, and changes the heart of the reader from earthly desires to embracing heavenly things; and it exercises the strong with more obscure sayings, and flatters the little ones with humble speech.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tanto amplius sancta Scriptura diligitur, quanto amplius meditatur, et usu legendi fastidium tollit; lectorisque animum verbis humilibus adjuvat, sublimibus levat.
chunks.english: Sacred Scripture is loved all the more, the more it is meditated on, and by the practice of reading it removes tedium; and it helps the soul of the reader with humble words, lifts it with sublime ones.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptura sacra aliquomodo cum legentibus crescit, a rudibus lectoribus quasi recognoscitur, et tamen doctis semper nova reperitur (Lib. I in Ezech., homil. 10, n. 2).
chunks.english: Sacred Scripture somehow grows with its readers; it is recognized as it were by unskilled readers, and yet is always found new by the learned.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad Ezechielem prophetam dicitur: Fili hominis quodcumque inveneris comede (Ezech. III, 1). Quidquid enim in sacra Scriptura invenitur edendum est, quia et ejus parva simplicem componunt vitam, et ejus magna subtilem aedificant intelligentiam.
chunks.english: To the prophet Ezekiel it is said in the book of Ezekiel, chapter three, verse one: Son of man, whatever you find, eat. For whatever is found in Sacred Scripture is to be eaten, because both its small things compose a simple life, and its great things build up subtle understanding.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praecepta Scripturae sacrae legendo reviviscimus, qui mortui in culpa jacebamus (Lib. I in Ezech., homil. 7, n. 16). Unde omnipotenti Domino per Psalmistam dicitur: In aeternum non obliviscar sermones tuos, quia in ipsis vivificasti me (Psal. CXVIII, 93).
chunks.english: By reading we come alive again through the precepts of Sacred Scripture, we who lay dead in guilt. From which it is said to the almighty Lord through the Psalmist in Psalm one hundred eighteen, verse ninety-three: I will never forget your words, because by them you have given me life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sacra Scriptura in tenebris vitae praesentis facta est nobis lumen itineris (Lib. I in Ezech., hom. 7, n. 17). Hinc enim Petrus ait: Cui bene facitis intendentes quasi lucernae lucenti in caliginoso loco (II Petr. I, 19).
chunks.english: Sacred Scripture has been made for us a light of the journey in the darkness of the present life. For from this Peter says in his second letter, chapter one, verse nineteen: To which you do well to attend as to a lamp shining in a dark place.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum legenti cuilibet sermo Scripturae sacrae tepidus videtur, sensus divini eloquii ejus mentem non excitat, et in cogitatione sua nullo intellectu luminis emittit (Lib. I in Ezech., hom. 7, n. 8). At vero si bene vivendi ordinem quaerat, et per gressum cordis inveniat, quemadmodum pedem boni operis ponat, tantum in sacro eloquio profectum invenit, quantum apud illud ipse profecerit.
chunks.english: When to anyone reading, the word of Sacred Scripture seems lukewarm, the sense of divine speech does not stir his mind, and sends forth no light of understanding in his thought. But truly if he seeks the order of living well, and finds through the step of the heart how to place the foot of good work, he finds as much progress in sacred speech as he himself has made progress before it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque fit, ut Scripturae sacrae verba esse mystica quisque sentiat, si accensus per contemplationis gratiam semetipsum ad coelestia suspendat. Mira enim atque ineffabilis sacri eloquii virtus agnoscitur, cum superno amore legentis animus penetratur.
chunks.english: It often happens that anyone feels the words of Sacred Scripture to be mystical if, kindled through the grace of contemplation, he suspends himself toward heavenly things. For the wondrous and ineffable power of sacred speech is recognized when the mind of the reader is penetrated by heavenly love.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum eloquiis sacris intendimus, plerumque malignorum spirituum insidias gravius toleramus, quia menti nostrae terrenarum cogitationum pulverem aspergunt, ut intentionis nostrae oculos a luce intimae visionis obscurent (Moral. lib. XVI, c. 18, n. 23).
chunks.english: When we attend to sacred speeches, we often bear more heavily the snares of evil spirits, because they sprinkle the dust of earthly thoughts on our mind, so that they may obscure the eyes of our intention from the light of intimate vision.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene Isaac patriarcha designat, cum puteos, quos pro haurienda aqua foderat, Allophyli terrae congerie replebant (Gen. XXVI, 15). Nos nimirum quasi puteos fodimus, cum in Scripturae abditis sensibus alta penetramus. Quos tamen occulte replent Allophyli, quando nobis ad alta tendentibus immundi spiritus terrenas cogitationes ingerunt, et quasi inventam divinae scientiae aquam tollunt.
chunks.english: The patriarch Isaac well represents this, when the wells which he had dug to draw water, the foreigners filled with piles of earth, according to the book of Genesis, chapter twenty-six, verse fifteen. We certainly dig wells as it were when in the hidden senses of Scripture we penetrate deep things. Which nevertheless the foreigners secretly fill, when unclean spirits thrust earthly thoughts on us tending toward high things, and as it were take away the water of divine knowledge that was found.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In mandatorum Dei praescrutatione insidias malignorum spirituum Psalmista pertulerat, cum dicebat: Declinate a me, maligni, et praescrubator mandata Dei mei (Psal. CXVIII, 115). Videlicet patenter insinuans quia mandata Dei praescrutari non poterat, cum malignorum spirituum insidias in mente tolerabat.
chunks.english: In examining the commandments of God the Psalmist had borne the snares of evil spirits, when he said in Psalm one hundred eighteen, verse one hundred fifteen: Depart from me, you evil ones, and I will scrutinize the commandments of my God. Clearly making known that he could not scrutinize the commandments of God when he was tolerating the snares of evil spirits in his mind.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Lectores sacri eloquii, dum foris ambigunt quod disponant mente, semper quasi ad tabernaculum redeunt, et velut coram testamenti arca Dominum consulunt, si de his in quibus dubitant apud semetipsos intus sacri eloquii paginas requirant (Moral. lib. XXIII, c. 20, n. 38).
chunks.english: Readers of sacred speech, while they are uncertain outwardly about what they arrange in mind, always as it were return to the tabernacle, and as if before the ark of the covenant they consult the Lord, if they seek within themselves inwardly the pages of sacred speech concerning those things about which they doubt.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XLI. De spiritalibus deliciis.
chunks.english: Chapter 41: On Spiritual Delights

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Augent spiritales deliciae desiderium in mente, dum satiant, quia quanto magis earum sapor percipitur, eo amplius cognoscitur quod avidius ametur (Lib. II in Ev., hom. 36, n. 1). Et idcirco non habitae amari non possunt, quia earum sapor ignoratur.
chunks.english: Spiritual delights increase desire in the mind while they satisfy, because the more their taste is perceived, the more it is known that they should be loved more eagerly. And therefore those not possessed cannot be loved, because their taste is unknown.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quis enim amare valeat quod ignorat? Psalmista nos admonet dicens: Gustate, et videte, quam suavis est Dominus (Psal. XXXIII, 9); ac si aperte dicat: Suavitatem ejus non cognoscitis, si hanc minime gustatis. Sed cibum vitae ex palato cordis tangite, ut probantes ejus dulcedinem amare valeatis.
chunks.english: For who can love what he does not know? The Psalmist warns us saying in Psalm thirty-three, verse nine: Taste and see how sweet the Lord is; as if he openly says: You do not know his sweetness if you do not taste it at all. But touch the food of life with the palate of the heart, so that by proving his sweetness you may be able to love.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Spiritales delicias tunc homo amisit, cum in paradiso peccavit. Extractus enim, a cibo aeternae dulcedinis os clausit. Unde nos quoque nati in hujus peregrinationis aerumna, qui fastidiosi jam venimus, nescimus quid desiderare debeamus.
chunks.english: Man lost spiritual delights when he sinned in paradise. For drawn out, he closed his mouth from the food of eternal sweetness. From which we also, born in the misery of this pilgrimage, who have now come fastidious, do not know what we ought to desire.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tantoque amplius fastidii nostri se morbus exaggerat, quanto magis ab esu illius dulcedinis animus elongat. Et eo jam internas delicias non appetit, quo se eas comedere diu longeque desuevit. Fastidio miseriae nostrae tabescimus, et longa inediae peste fatigamur.
chunks.english: And the disease of our fastidiousness is exaggerated all the more, the more the soul distances itself from eating that sweetness. And now it does not desire internal delights to the degree that it has long and far ceased to eat them. We waste away with the fastidiousness of our misery, and are wearied by the long plague of starvation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quia gustare intus nolumus dulcedinem praeparatam, amamus foras miseri famem nostram. Superna pietas nos nec deserentes se deserit. Contemptas enim illas delicias ad memoriae nostrae oculos revocat, easque nobis proponit, in promissione torporem excutit, atque ut fastidium nostrum repellere debeamus, invitat, ac dicit: Gustate, et videte, quam suavis est Dominus (Lib. II in Evang., hom. 36, n. 2)
chunks.english: And because we do not wish to taste inwardly the sweetness prepared, we wretched people love outwardly our hunger. Heavenly piety does not abandon us even when we abandon it. For it recalls those despised delights to the eyes of our memory, and proposes them to us, shakes off torpor in the promise, and that we should repel our fastidiousness, invites us and says: Taste and see how sweet the Lord is.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XLII. De discretione.
chunks.english: Chapter 42: On Discretion

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magnum est disciplinae magisterium subtilitas discretionis, quatenus quisque rector culpas delinquentium discrete noverit parcere, et pie resecare (Lib. II in Ezech., homil. 9, n. 20). Qui autem sic dimittunt peccata, ut non corrigant, aut sic quasi corrigendo feriunt, ut non dimittant, discretionis spiritum non habent.
chunks.english: Great is the teaching of discipline, the subtlety of discretion, so that each ruler may know how to spare the faults of offenders with discretion and to cut them off piously. But those who so forgive sins that they do not correct, or so strike as if correcting that they do not forgive, do not have the spirit of discretion.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum est in libro Genesis: Si recte offeras, et recte non dividas, peccasti (Gen. IV, 7, secundum LXX). Recte offertur, cum recta intentione quid agitur. Sed recte non dividitur, si non hoc, quod pie agitur, etiam subtiliter discernatur.
chunks.english: It is written in the book of Genesis, chapter four, verse seven according to the Septuagint: If you offer rightly, and do not divide rightly, you have sinned. It is offered rightly when something is done with right intention. But it is not divided rightly if what is piously done is not also subtly discerned.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Oblata recte dividere, est quaelibet bona nostra studia discernendo pensare. Quod nimirum qui agere dissimulat, etiam recte offerens peccat. Saepe quod bono studio gerimus, dum discernere caute negligimus, quo judicetur fine nescimus; et nonnunquam hoc fit reatus criminis, quod putatur causa virtutis (Moral. lib. III, c. 13, n. 23, 24).
chunks.english: To divide rightly what is offered is to weigh any of our good efforts by discerning. Which indeed whoever pretends to do sins even while offering rightly. Often what we do with good zeal, while we neglect to discern cautiously, we do not know by what end it will be judged; and sometimes that becomes the guilt of crime which is thought to be the cause of virtue.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Recte ergo offerimus, cum bono studio bonum opus agimus; sed recte non dividimus, si habere discretionem in bono opere postponamus (Lib. I in Ezech., homil. 11, n. 12). Ille namque angustam viam ingreditur, qui in cunctis quae agit, discretionis subtilitate sollicite coarctatur.
chunks.english: We therefore offer rightly when we do good work with good zeal; but we do not divide rightly if we postpone having discretion in good work. For he enters the narrow way who in all things which he does is carefully restricted by the subtlety of discretion.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam qui per voluntates proprias secura mente se dilatat, angustae sibi portae aditum damnat (Moral. lib. XXVIII, c. 11, n. 26). Sancta Ecclesia, quae ex causis singulis tentamenta prodeant, per discretionem prospicit, et ventura vitiorum bella ex alto deprehendit.
chunks.english: For he who with secure mind expands himself through his own wills condemns for himself the entrance of the narrow gate. The Holy Church, through discretion foresees from what causes individual temptations proceed, and from on high perceives the coming battles of vices.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Discipline of Silence and Restraint
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XLIII.
De taciturnitate.
chunks.english: Chapter 43: On Silence
chunks.notes: Begins extended section on discretion in speech

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Lingua discrete frenanda est, non insolubiter obliganda. Scriptum namque est: Sapiens tacebit usque ad tempus (Eccli. XX, 7; Moral. lib. VII, c. 37, n. 61). Ut nimirum cum opportunum considerat, postposita censura silentii, loquendo quae congruunt, in usum se utilitatis impendat.
chunks.english: The tongue should be restrained with discretion, not bound up irrevocably. For Scripture says: A wise person will be silent until the right time—from Ecclesiasticus, chapter twenty, verse seven. The goal is that when someone judges the moment opportune, they set aside the constraint of silence and, by speaking what is appropriate, devote themselves to usefulness.
chunks.notes: Key theme: balance between speech and silence

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De tacendi atque loquendi censura per Salomonem dicitur: Tempus loquendi, et tempus tacendi (Eccle. III, 7). Discrete quippe vicissitudinum pensanda sunt tempora, ne aut cum restringi lingua debet, per verba inutiliter defluat, aut cum loqui utiliter potest, semetipsam pigre restringat.
chunks.english: About the discipline of silence and speech, Solomon says: There is a time for speaking and a time for being silent—from Ecclesiastes, chapter three, verse seven. We must weigh carefully the changing circumstances, lest either our tongue flow uselessly in words when it ought to be restrained, or when it could speak usefully, it sluggishly restricts itself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quanta sit utilitas taciturnitatis silentium, Psalmista considerans ait: Pone, Domine, custodiam ori meo, et ostium circumstantiae labiis meis (Psal. CXLIX, 3). Non enim poni ori suo parietem, sed ostium petit, quod videlicet aperitur et clauditur.
chunks.english: The Psalmist, considering how great is the benefit of restrained silence, says: Lord, set a guard over my mouth, and a door of enclosure around my lips—from Psalm one hundred forty-nine, verse three. For he does not ask that a wall be placed over his mouth, but a door—something that obviously opens and closes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde et nobis caute dicendum est, quatenus os discretum et congruo tempore vox aperiat, et rursum congruo taciturnitas claudat. Sollicite studeant nimis taciti, non solum quales foras ostendere, sed etiam quales se debeant intus exhibere, ut plus ex cogitationibus occultum judicium, quam ex sermonibus reprehensionem metuant proximorum.
chunks.english: And so we ourselves must speak cautiously, so that discretion opens the mouth with voice at the right time, and silence closes it again at the right time. The overly silent should strive carefully not only to show what they are like outwardly, but also to exhibit what they ought to be like inwardly, so that they fear God's hidden judgment on their thoughts more than their neighbors' criticism of their words.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Insinuari nimis tacitis debet, quia dum quaedam vitia incaute fugiunt, occulte deterioribus implicantur. Nam saepe linguam quia immoderatius frenant, in corde gravius multiloquium tolerant. Ut eo plus cogitationes in mente ferveant, quo illas violenta custodia indiscreti silentii angustat.
chunks.english: The excessively silent should be instructed that while they carelessly flee certain vices, they secretly become entangled in worse ones. For often, because they restrain the tongue too immoderately, they endure more serious chattering in the heart. The result is that thoughts burn more intensely in the mind precisely because the violent custody of indiscriminate silence constricts them.
chunks.notes: Psychological insight: suppression leads to internal turbulence

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque nimis taciti tanto latius in cogitationibus diffluunt, quanto se esse securius aestimant, quia foris a reprehensoribus non videntur. Nonnunquam mens taciti in superbiam tollitur, et quos loquentes audit, quasi infirmos despicit. Cumque os corporis claudit, quantum se vitiis superbiendo aperiat, non agnoscit.
chunks.english: Usually the excessively silent flow all the more widely in their thoughts because they consider themselves more secure, since outwardly they are not seen by critics. Sometimes the silent person's mind is lifted up in pride, and when he hears others speaking, he despises them as though they were weak. And while he closes the mouth of his body, he does not recognize how much he opens himself to vices through pride.
chunks.notes: Pride as hidden vice of silence

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque contingit ut mens nimium taciti linguam premat, mentem elevet. Et cum suam nequitiam minime considerat, tanto apud se cunctos liberius, quanto et secretius accusat. Saepe nimis taciti, cum nonnulla injusta patiuntur, eo in acriorem dolorem prodeunt, quo ea quae sustinent non loquuntur.
chunks.english: It often happens that the mind of the overly silent person presses down the tongue but lifts up the mind. And when he scarcely considers his own wickedness, he accuses everyone else all the more freely in his own thoughts precisely because he does so secretly. Often the excessively silent, when they suffer certain injustices, advance into sharper pain precisely because they do not speak about what they endure.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam si illatas molestias tranquille lingua diceret, a conscientia dolor emanaret. Vulnera enim clausa plus cruciant. Nam cum putredo, quae interius fervet, ejicitur, ad salutem dolor aperitur. Scire debent qui plusquam expedit tacant ne inter molesta quae tolerant, dum linguam tenent, vim doloris exaggerent.
chunks.english: For if the tongue calmly spoke about the troubles inflicted, the pain would flow away from the conscience. Closed wounds torment more severely. For when the infection that festers inside is expelled, the pain is opened toward healing. Those who keep silent more than is expedient should know that among the troubles they endure, by holding their tongue they intensify the force of pain.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Monendi enim sunt ut si proximos sicut se diligunt, minime illis taceant, unde eos juste reprehendunt. Vocis medicamine utrorumque saluti concurritur, dum ab illo qui infert actio prava compescitur, et ab hoc qui sustinet doloris fervor vulnere aperto temperatur.
chunks.english: They should be admonished that if they love their neighbors as themselves, they should by no means keep silent when they justly reprove them. Through the medicine of voice, the salvation of both parties is served: the wrongdoer is restrained from the evil action, and the one who suffers has the fever of pain tempered when the wound is opened.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui proximorum mala respiciunt, et tamen in silentio linguam premunt, quasi conspectis vulneribus usum medicaminis subtrahunt; et eo mortis auctores fiunt, quo virus quod poterant curare noluerunt.
chunks.english: Those who observe their neighbors' evils and yet press down their tongue in silence are like those who, having seen wounds, withhold the use of medicine. They become authors of death precisely because they refused to cure the poison they were capable of curing.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XLIV.
De cavenda detractione.
chunks.english: Chapter 44: On Avoiding Detraction
chunks.notes: Shift to speaking ill of others

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui alienae vitae detractione pascuntur, alienis procul dubio carnibus satiantur, sicut scriptum est: Non comedas cum eis qui carnem ad vescendum conferunt (Prov. XXIII, 20; Moral. lib. XIV, c. 52, n. 61). Carnes quippe ad vescendum conferre est in collocutione derogationis vicissim proximorum vitia derogare.
chunks.english: Those who feed on slandering others' lives are undoubtedly filled with alien flesh, as Scripture says: Do not eat with those who bring meat to the feast—from Proverbs, chapter twenty-three, verse twenty. To bring meat to the feast is to mutually disparage the faults of neighbors in conversations of detraction.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De obtrectatoribus recte per Salomonem dicitur: Quia vacantes potibus, et dantes symbolum, consumentur, et vestietur pannis dormitio. Potibus vacant, qui de opprobrio alienae vitae se debriant. Symbolum vero dare est sicut unusquisque solet de parte sua cibos ad vescendum, ita in confabulatione detractionis verba conferre.
chunks.english: Solomon rightly says concerning slanderers: Because those who linger over drinks and contribute their share will be consumed, and sleep will be clothed with rags—from the same place. They linger over drinks who intoxicate themselves on the reproach of others' lives. To contribute one's share is like each person bringing their portion of food to eat, so in the conversation of detraction they contribute words.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vacantes, inquit, potibus, et dantes symbolum consumentur, quia sicut scriptum est: Omnis detractor eradicabitur. Vestietur autem pannis dormitio, quia despectum et inopem a cunctis bonis operibus mors sua invenit, quem hic ad alienae vitae exquirenda crimina detractionis suae languor occupavat.
chunks.english: Those who linger over drinks and contribute their share, he says, will be consumed, because as Scripture says: Every slanderer will be uprooted. Sleep will be clothed with rags because death finds despised and destitute of all good works the one whom here the sickness of his own detraction occupied in seeking out crimes in others' lives.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XLV.
De compunctione.
chunks.english: Chapter 45: On Compunction
chunks.notes: Major transition to repentance and sorrow for sin

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Disciplina exterior culpas diluit, et extensam mentem compunctio poenitentiae ultione transfigit. Sed hoc inter se utraque haec differunt, quod plagae percussionum dolent, lamenta compunctionum sapiunt. Illae affligentes cruciant, ista reficiunt, dum affligunt. Per illas in afflictione moeror est, per haec in moerore laetitia.
chunks.english: Outward discipline washes away faults, and the compunction of repentance pierces through the extended mind with vengeance. But these two differ from each other in this: the blows of beatings cause pain, while the laments of compunction bring savor. The former torment while afflicting, but the latter refresh even while afflicting. Through beatings there is sorrow in affliction; through compunction there is joy in sorrow.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omne peccatum spina est, quia dum trabit ad delectationem, quasi pungendo lacerat mentem. Unde voce justi, et poenitentis dicitur: Conversus sum in aerumna mea dum confringitur spina (Psal. XXXI, 4). Quia scilicet mens ad lamentum vertitur, ut peccati punctio poenitendo frangatur.
chunks.english: Every sin is a thorn, because while it draws toward pleasure, it tears the mind as though by pricking. Hence in the voice of the just person who repents it is said: I am turned into misery while the thorn is being broken—from Psalm thirty-one, verse four. For the mind is turned to lamentation so that the pricking of sin may be broken through repentance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Disrupisti, inquit, vincula mea, tibi sacrificabo hostiam laudis (Psal. CXV, 16). Tunc disrumpit Dominus vincula nostra, cum et mala nobis quae fecimus demonstrat, atque ad haec eadem deflenda quae cognoscimus adjuvat. Omnipotens Dominus culpas operis objicit, et pia manu gratiae vincula cordis solvit, ut ad vacationem poenitentiae mens nostra se erigat, et carnis soluta compedibus, in auctorem suum libera gressum amoris tendat.
chunks.english: You have broken my bonds, he says, and I will sacrifice to you a sacrifice of praise—from Psalm one hundred fifteen, verse sixteen. The Lord then breaks our bonds when he both shows us the evils we have done and helps us to weep for these same things we recognize. The almighty Lord exposes our guilty works, and with the merciful hand of grace he loosens the bonds of the heart, so that our mind may rise up to the leisure of repentance, and freed from the fetters of flesh, may direct its step of love freely toward its maker.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum vehemens poenitentiae spiritus mentem occupat, omnem in ea explorationem reprehensibilis gaudii perturbat, ut nihil ei jam nisi flere libeat, nihil quod se terrere possit, attendat. Electi uniuscujusque mens ponit ante oculos illinc districtionem justitiae, hinc meritum culpae: conspicit quo supplicio digna sit, si parcentis pietas desit, quae per lamenta praesentia ab aeterna eruere poena consuevit.
chunks.english: When the vehement spirit of repentance occupies the mind, it disturbs every examination of blameworthy joy within it, so that nothing pleases the mind except to weep, and it pays attention to nothing that could terrify it. Each elect person's mind places before its eyes on one side the strictness of justice, on the other side the merit of guilt: it perceives what punishment it would deserve if the mercy of the one who spares were absent—that mercy which is accustomed to rescue from eternal punishment through present laments.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum vis compunctionis valida mentes nostras huic mundo quasi mari deditas salubriter rore confundit, quasi in spiritu vehementi Deus naves Tharsis conterit. Omnipotens Dominus mentes carnalium, quae prius in hujus mundi fluctibus vagabantur, per sancti Spiritus adventum in moerore poenitentiae affligit, ut contritae a superbia salubriter in humilitate jaceant, quas prius in hoc mari saeculi ad alta vanitatis prosperitatis suae unda sublevavit.
chunks.english: When the strong force of compunction wholesomely overwhelms our minds dedicated to this world as to a sea, it is as though in a mighty spirit God shatters the ships of Tarshish. The almighty Lord, through the coming of the Holy Spirit, afflicts in the sorrow of repentance the minds of carnal people that previously wandered in the waves of this world, so that crushed from pride they may lie wholesomely in humility—they whom the wave of their prosperity previously lifted up to the heights of vanity in this sea of the world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque ad Christum mentes carnalium per poenitentiam conversae, quibus poena videbatur ab hominibus despici, postmodum grave fit ab hominibus honorari. Et qui ante mala perpetrare consueverant, et gaudebant, postmodum non timent etiam affligi pro bonis.
chunks.english: Usually the minds of carnal people converted to Christ through repentance—for whom it seemed a punishment to be despised by men—afterward find it burdensome to be honored by men. And those who previously were accustomed to perpetrate evils and rejoiced in them, afterward do not fear even to be afflicted for good things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quatuor sunt qualitates quibus justi viri anima in compunctione vehementer afficitur, cum aut malorum suorum reminiscitur, considerans ubi fuit; aut judiciorum Dei sententiam metuens, et secum quaerens, cogitat ubi erit; aut cum mala vitae praesentis solerter attendens, moerens considerat ubi est; aut cum bona supernae patriae contemplatur, quae quia necdum adipiscitur, lugens conspicit ubi non est.
chunks.english: There are four qualities by which the soul of a just person is vehemently affected in compunction: either when he remembers his evils, considering where he was; or when fearing the sentence of God's judgments and inquiring within himself, he thinks where he will be; or when carefully attending to the evils of present life, he mourns and considers where he is; or when he contemplates the goods of the heavenly homeland which he has not yet attained, he grieves and sees where he is not.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Malorum suorum Paulus meminerat, et ex eis se in quibus fuerat affligebat, cum diceret: Non sum dignus vocari apostolus, quia persecutus sum Ecclesiam Dei (I Cor. XV, 9). Divinum judicium subtiliter Paulus apostolus pensans, in futuro male esse metuebat, dicens: Castigo corpus meum, et servituti subjicio, ne forte aliis praedicans, ipse reprobus efficiar (I Cor. IX, 27).
chunks.english: Paul remembered his evils and afflicted himself for those things in which he had been, when he said: I am not worthy to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the Church of God—from Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter fifteen, verse nine. The apostle Paul, carefully weighing divine judgment, feared being in a bad state in the future, saying: I discipline my body and bring it into subjection, lest perhaps when preaching to others I myself should become disqualified—from Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter nine, verse twenty-seven.
chunks.notes: Biblical examples of first two modes

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mala praesentis vitae idem Paulus pensabat, cum diceret: Dum sumus in corpore, peregrinamur a Domino (II Cor. V, 6). Et: Video aliam legem in membris meis repugnantem legi mentis meae, et captivum me ducentem in lege peccati, quae est in membris meis. Infelix ego homo, quis me liberabit de corpore mortis hujus (Rom. VII, 22)?
chunks.english: The same Paul weighed the evils of present life when he said: While we are in the body, we are away from the Lord—from Paul's second letter to the Corinthians, chapter five, verse six. And: I see another law in my members warring against the law of my mind, and leading me captive in the law of sin which is in my members. Wretched man that I am, who will deliver me from this body of death—from Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter seven, verse twenty-two?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Rursum bona aeternae patriae idem Vas electionis considerabat, dicens: Videmus nunc per speculum in aenigmate; tunc autem facie ad faciem. Nunc cognosco ex parte, tunc autem cognoscam sicut et cognitus sum (I Cor. XIII, 12). Atque iterum: Scimus quoniam si terrestris domus nostra hujus habitationis dissolvatur, quod aedificationem ex Deo habemus, domum non manufactam aeternam in coelis (II Cor. V, 1).
chunks.english: Again the same Vessel of Election considered the goods of the eternal homeland, saying: We now see through a glass dimly, but then face to face. Now I know in part, but then I shall know just as I am known—from Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter thirteen, verse twelve. And again: We know that if our earthly house of this dwelling is dissolved, we have a building from God, a house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens—from Paul's second letter to the Corinthians, chapter five, verse one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mala vitae praesentis beatus Job considerans, ait: Tentatio est vita humana super terram (Job. VII, 1). Unde David quoque ait: Universa vanitas omnis homo vivens (Psal. XXXVIII, 6). Et quanquam in imaginem Dei ambulet homo, tamen vane conturbabitur. Aeternam patriam Psalmista contemplatus, et haec mala pensans, in quibus erat, atque illa bona considerans, in quibus adhuc non erat, ait: Heu me quod incolatus meus prolongatus est (Psal. CXIX, 5)!
chunks.english: Blessed Job, considering the evils of present life, said: Human life on earth is a trial—from Job, chapter seven, verse one. Hence David also said: Every living man is altogether vanity—from Psalm thirty-eight, verse six. And although man walks in the image of God, yet he will be troubled in vain. The Psalmist, contemplating the eternal homeland and weighing these evils in which he was, and considering those goods in which he was not yet, said: Woe is me that my sojourning is prolonged—from Psalm one hundred nineteen, verse five!
chunks.notes: OT examples of third and fourth modes

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et: Ego dixi in pavore meo: Projectus sum a vultu oculorum tuorum (Psal. XXX, 23). Sunt nonnulli qui jam in dono perceperunt libere pro justitia eloqui oppressos tueri, indigentibus possessa tribuere, ardorem fidei habere; sed adhuc gratiam lacrymarum non habent. Hi nimirum terram Australem et arentem habent, sed adhuc irrigua indigent, quia in bonis operibus positi, in quibus magni atque ferventes sunt, oportet nimis ut aut timore supplicii, aut amore regni coelorum, mala etiam quae antea perpetraverunt deplorent.
chunks.english: And: I said in my alarm, I am cut off from before your eyes—from Psalm thirty, verse twenty-three. There are some who have already received in gift the ability to speak freely for justice, to protect the oppressed, to distribute their possessions to the needy, to have the ardor of faith—but they do not yet have the grace of tears. These certainly have the southern land that is dry, but they still need irrigation, because being established in good works in which they are great and fervent, it is extremely necessary that either by fear of punishment or by love of the kingdom of heaven, they should also weep for the evils they previously committed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Licet in multis speciebus compunctio dividatur, quando singulae quaeque a poenitentibus culpae planguntur; unde ex voce quoque poenitentium Jeremias ait: Divisiones aquarum deduxit oculus meus (Thren. III, 48); principaliter tamen compunctionum genera duo sunt, quia ad Deum sitiens anima, prius timore compungitur, post amore.
chunks.english: Although compunction is divided into many forms when each individual fault is mourned by penitents—hence Jeremiah also says in the voice of penitents: My eye has sent down divisions of waters—from Lamentations, chapter three, verse forty-eight—nevertheless there are principally two kinds of compunction, because the soul thirsting for God is first pricked by fear, then by love.
chunks.notes: Two primary modes: fear and love

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Prius enim sese in lacrymis afficit, quia dum malorum suorum recolit, pro his perpeti aeterna supplicia pertimescit. Cum longa moeroris anxietudine fuerit formido consumpta, quaedam jam de praesumptione veniae securitas nascitur, et in amore coelestium gaudiorum animus inflammatur. Et qui prius flebat, ne duceretur ad supplicium, postmodum flere amarissime incipit, quia differtur a regno.
chunks.english: For first it affects itself in tears because, while recalling its evils, it fears suffering eternal punishments for them. When fear has been consumed by long anxiety of sorrow, a certain security is born from the presumption of pardon, and the soul is inflamed with love of heavenly joys. And the one who previously wept lest he be led to punishment, afterward begins to weep most bitterly because he is delayed from the kingdom.
chunks.notes: Progression from servile to filial devotion

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque contemplatur mens qui sint illi angelorum chori, quae ipsa societas sanctorum spirituum, quae majestas internae visionis Dei, et amplius plangit, quia a bonis perennibus deest, quam flebat prius, cum mala aeterna metuebat. Sicque fit ut perfecta compunctio formidinis tradat animum compunctioni dilectionis.
chunks.english: Often the mind contemplates what those angelic choirs are, what that very society of holy spirits is, what the majesty of the inner vision of God is, and it mourns more greatly because it is absent from perennial goods than it wept before when it feared eternal evils. And so it happens that perfect compunction of fear delivers the soul over to compunction of love.
chunks.notes: Transformation of motivation in spiritual growth

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In sacra veracique historia figurata narratione describitur quod Axa filia Caleph sedens super asinum suspiravit (Josue XV, 18 seq.). Cui dixit pater suus: Quid habes? atque illa respondit: Da mihi benedictionem, terram Australem et arentem dedisti mihi, junge et irriguam. Tunc dedit ei pater suus irriguum superius et irriguum inferius.
chunks.english: In sacred and true history, a figurative narrative describes how Achsah the daughter of Caleb, sitting on a donkey, sighed—from the book of Joshua, chapter fifteen, verse eighteen and following. Her father said to her: What do you have? And she responded: Give me a blessing; you have given me the southern and dry land, add also the irrigated land. Then her father gave her the upper springs and the lower springs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Axa quippe super asinum sedet, cum irrationabilibus carnis suae motibus anima praesidet; quae suspirans a patre terram irriguam petit; quia a Creatore nostro cum magno gemitu quaerendae sunt lacrymae compunctionum. Quia ergo, ut dixi, duo sunt compunctionis genera, dedit ei Pater suus irriguum superius, et irriguum inferius.
chunks.english: Achsah sits on a donkey when the soul presides over the irrational movements of its flesh; sighing, she seeks irrigated land from her father, because from our Creator the tears of compunction must be sought with great groaning. Because therefore, as I said, there are two kinds of compunction, her father gave her the upper springs and the lower springs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Irriguum quippe superius accipit anima, cum sese in lacrymis coelestis regni desiderio affligit; irriguum vero inferius accipit, cum inferni supplicia flendo pertimescit.
chunks.english: The soul receives the upper springs when it afflicts itself in tears through desire for the heavenly kingdom; it receives the lower springs when it fears the punishments of hell with weeping.
chunks.notes: Two springs = two modes of compunction

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Hope and Fear in the Elect
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XLVI.
De spe et formidine electorum.
chunks.english: Chapter 46: On the Hope and Fear of the Elect
chunks.notes: Shift to tension between confidence and trembling

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque mens justi jam quidem quod perverse egisse meminit deplorat, jam prave acta non solum deserit, sed amarissimis etiam lamentis punit: sed tamen dum eorum quae egit reminiscitur, gravi de judicio pavore terretur. Electus quisque jam se perfecte convertit; sed adhuc se perfecte in securitate non erigit, quia dum quanta sit districtio extremi examinis pensat, inter spem ac formidinem sollicitus trepidat, quia, justus Judex veniens quid de perpetratis reputet, quid relaxet, ignorat.
chunks.english: Often the mind of a just person now deplores what it remembers doing perversely, now not only abandons wrongly done deeds but also punishes them with most bitter laments—yet nevertheless, while remembering what it has done, it is terrified with grave fear concerning judgment. Each elect person now converts himself perfectly, but does not yet raise himself up perfectly in security, because while he weighs how great is the strictness of the final examination, he trembles anxiously between hope and fear, because he does not know what the just Judge coming will consider concerning deeds committed, what he will forgive.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe mens poenitentis quam prava commiserit meminit; sed haec commissa si digne fleverit nescit, ac ne culpae immanitas modum poenitentiae transeat, metuit. Plerumque culpam jam veritas relaxat, sed mens afflicta adhuc de venia, dum valde sibi est sollicita, trepidat. Sanctus quisque vir etiam hic misericordiam suscipit, sed suscepisse se nescit, quia peccatum suum homo jam corrigendo et poenitendo deserit; sed tamen adhuc districtum Judicem de ejus retributione pertimescit.
chunks.english: Often the penitent mind remembers what wrongs it committed, but does not know whether it has wept for these committed wrongs worthily, and fears lest the enormity of guilt exceed the measure of repentance. Often truth now forgives the guilt, but the afflicted mind still trembles concerning pardon, being very anxious about itself. Each holy person receives mercy even here, but does not know that he has received it, because man now abandons his sin by correcting and repenting, but nevertheless still fears the strict Judge concerning its retribution.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illic justus quilibet sine ulla formidine misericordias Domini libere in aeternitate cantat (Psal. LXXXVIII, 1), ubi jam de peccati venia dubietas non erit ubi, jam securam mentem culpae suae memoria non addicit, ubi non sub reatu animus trepidat, sed de ejus indulgentia liber exsultat.
chunks.english: There every just person freely sings the mercies of the Lord in eternity without any fear—from Psalm eighty-eight, verse one—where there will no longer be doubt about the forgiveness of sin, where the memory of his guilt no longer indicts the secure mind, where the soul does not tremble under guilt but rejoices freely in his pardon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: David propheta cum peteret, dicens: Averte oculos tuos a peccatis meis (Psal. L, 11), paulo superius intulit: Delictum meum coram me est semper (Psal. L, 5). Ac si diceret: Peccatum meum ne respicias, postulo, quia hoc respicere ipse non cesso. Unde et per alium prophetam Dominus dicit: Et peccatorum tuorum memor non ero, tu autem memor esto (Isai. XLIII, 25).
chunks.english: When the prophet David petitioned saying: Turn away your eyes from my sins—from Psalm fifty, verse eleven—a little earlier he added: My offense is before me always—from Psalm fifty, verse five. As if to say: I ask that you not look at my sin, because I myself do not cease looking at it. Hence also through another prophet the Lord says: And I will not remember your sins, but you remember them—from Isaiah, chapter forty-three, verse twenty-five.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Providendum est his qui peccata sua deflent, ut singula quaeque admissa considerent, et dum per unumquodque erroris sui inquinationem deflent, simul se ac totos lacrymis mundent. Per Jeremiam dicitur, cum Judeae singula delicta pensarentur: Divisiones aquarum deduxit oculus meus (Thren. III, 48). Divisas quippe ex oculis aquas deducimus, quando peccatis singulis dispertitas lacrymas damus. Neque enim uno eodemque tempore aeque mens de omnibus dolet; sed dum nunc hujus, nunc illius culpae memoria acrius tangitur, simul de omnibus in singulis commota purgatur.
chunks.english: It must be provided for those who weep for their sins that they consider each individual committed deed, and while they weep through each one for the defilement of their error, they simultaneously cleanse themselves entirely with tears. Through Jeremiah it is said, when the individual sins of Judea were weighed: My eye has sent down divisions of waters—from Lamentations, chapter three, verse forty-eight. We send down divided waters from our eyes when we give distributed tears to individual sins. For the mind does not grieve equally for all things at one and the same time, but while now the memory of this fault, now of that fault touches it more sharply, moved in individual cases it is purged of all simultaneously.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt timore formidinis oppressi, ut de misericordia quam postulant praesumant, ne vi immoderatae afflictionis intereant. Neque enim pie Dominus ante delinquentium oculos flenda peccata opponeret, si per semetipsum ea districte ferire voluisset. Constat enim quod suo judicio abscondere voluit, quos miserando praeveniens sibimetipsis judices fecit.
chunks.english: Those oppressed by the terror of fear should be admonished to presume upon the mercy they ask for, lest they perish by the force of immoderate affliction. For the merciful Lord would not place sins to be wept for before the eyes of offenders if he had wanted to strike them strictly by himself. For it is established that he wanted to hide from his own judgment those whom, mercifully preventing them, he made judges of themselves.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc enim scriptum est: Praeveniamus faciem ejus in confessione (Psal. XCXIV, 2). Hinc per Paulum dicitur: Si nosmetipsos dijudicaremus, non utique judicaremur (I Cor. XI, 31). Sancti viri, in eo quod se peccasse meminerunt, quia pertimescunt et deflent, quid aliud quam corpus cooperiunt? Qui facta carnalia per superducta bona opera districto examine abscondunt.
chunks.english: Hence it is written: Let us come before his face in confession—from Psalm ninety-four, verse two. Hence Paul says: If we judged ourselves, we would certainly not be judged—from Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter eleven, verse thirty-one. Holy men, in that they remember having sinned because they fear and weep, what else do they do but cover the body? They hide carnal deeds from strict examination through good works superimposed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum est: Beati quorum remissae sunt iniquitates, et quorum tecta sunt peccata (Psal. XXXI, 1). Peccata enim tegimus, cum bona facta malis actibus superponimus. Omne enim quod operitur, inferius ponitur; et hoc unde operitur, desuper ducitur. Quando ergo abdicamus mala quae fecimus, et eligimus bona quae facimus quasi tegimen illi rei superducimus, quam videri erubescimus.
chunks.english: It is written: Blessed are those whose iniquities are forgiven and whose sins are covered—from Psalm thirty-one, verse one. For we cover sins when we superimpose good deeds upon evil acts. For everything that is covered is placed below, and that with which it is covered is drawn over from above. When therefore we renounce the evils we have done and choose the goods we do, we draw over as a covering that thing which we blush to have seen.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sancti viri adhuc in hac vita constituti habent, quod ante Dei oculos operire debeant, quia omnino est impossibile ut aut in opere, aut in locutione, aut in cogitatione nunquam delinquant. Per beatum Mosen Dominus ait: Non accipies loco pignoris molam superiorem aut inferiorem (Deut. XXIV, 6.)
chunks.english: Holy men still established in this life have something they should cover before God's eyes, because it is altogether impossible that they never sin either in deed, or in speech, or in thought. Through blessed Moses the Lord says: You shall not take as a pledge the upper or lower millstone—from Deuteronomy, chapter twenty-four, verse six.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Superior autem aut inferior mola est spes et timor: spes quippe ad alta subvehit, timor autem cor inferius premit. Mola superior et inferior ita sibi necessario jungitur, ut una sine altera inutiliter habeatur. In peccatoris itaque pectore incessanter debet spes, et formido conjungi, quia incassum misericordiam sperat, si non etiam de misericordia confidit.
chunks.english: The upper or lower millstone is hope and fear: for hope lifts up to the heights, while fear presses the heart down below. The upper and lower millstone are so necessarily joined to each other that one is uselessly held without the other. In the sinner's breast therefore hope and fear must be joined incessantly, because he hopes for mercy in vain if he does not also trust in mercy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Loco pignoris mola superior aut inferior tolli prohibetur, quia qui peccatori praedicat, tanta dispensatione componere praedicationem debet, ut nec derelicta spe timorem subtrahat, nec subtracta spe in solo eum timore derelinquat. Mola superior aut inferior tollitur, si per praedicantis linguam in peccatoris pectore aut timor a spe, aut spes a timore dividatur.
chunks.english: The upper or lower millstone is forbidden to be taken as a pledge, because whoever preaches to a sinner must compose the preaching with such dispensation that he neither removes fear by abandoning hope, nor abandons him in fear alone by removing hope. The upper or lower millstone is removed if through the preacher's tongue either fear is divided from hope or hope from fear in the sinner's breast.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XLVII.
De poenitentia.
chunks.english: Chapter 47: On Repentance

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnis peccator in poenitentia duplum habere gemitum debet. Nimirum quia et bonum quod oportuit, non fecit, et malum quod non oportuit, fecit. Cum ad bona opera non assurgimus, necesse est ut nosmetipsos dupliciter defleamus, quia et recta non fecimus, et prava operati sumus.
chunks.english: Every sinner in repentance ought to have double groaning. Certainly because he both did not do the good which he ought to have done, and did the evil which he ought not to have done. When we do not rise to good works, it is necessary that we weep for ourselves doubly, because we both did not do right things and we performed wrong things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per beatum Moysen unus turtur pro peccato, alter vero offerri in holocaustum jubetur (Levit. XV, 29, 30). Holocaustum namque totum incensum dicitur. Unum ergo turturem pro peccato offerimus, cum pro culpa gemitum damus; de altero holocaustum facimus, cum pro eo bona negligimus, nosmetipsos funditus succendentes igne doloris ardemus.
chunks.english: Through blessed Moses, one turtledove is commanded to be offered for sin, but another for a burnt offering—from Leviticus, chapter fifteen, verses twenty-nine and thirty. For burnt offering means totally burned. We therefore offer one turtledove for sin when we give groaning for guilt; we make a burnt offering of the other when, for those good things we neglected, we burn ourselves completely with the fire of pain.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Beatus Job per flagella proficiens, et a semetipso in magna sui redargutione dissentiens, dicit: Idcirco ipse me reprehendo, et ago poenitentiam (Job. XLII, 6). Poenitentiam agere est, contemplata summa essentia, nihil aliud quam favillam se cineremque cognoscere.
chunks.english: Blessed Job, advancing through afflictions and dissenting from himself in great self-rebuke, says: Therefore I reprehend myself and do penance—from Job, chapter forty-two, verse six. To do penance is, having contemplated the highest essence, to recognize oneself as nothing other than dust and ashes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In cilicio asperitas et punctio peccatorum; in cinere autem pulvis ostenditur mortuorum. Et idcirco utrumque hoc adhiberi ad poenitentiam solet, ut in punctione cilicii cognoscamus quid per culpam fecimus, et in favilla cineris perpendamus quid per judicium facti sumus.
chunks.english: In sackcloth there is the roughness and pricking of sins; in ashes the dust of the dead is shown. And therefore both of these are customarily applied to repentance, so that in the pricking of sackcloth we recognize what we did through guilt, and in the embers of ashes we weigh what we have become through judgment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Considerentur ergo in cilicio pungentia vitia, consideretur in cinere per mortis sententiam subsequens justa poena vitiorum. Quia post peccatum carnis contumeliae surrexerunt, videat homo in asperitate cilicii superbiendo quid fecit, videat in cinere usquequo peccando pervenit. Quasi quodam cilicio peccator pungitur, dum in mente sua asperis redargutionum stimulis confricatur. In cinere autem agit poenitentiam, quia ex primo peccato quid per justum judicium factus sit solerter attendit.
chunks.english: Therefore let the pricking vices be considered in sackcloth, let the just punishment of vices following through the sentence of death be considered in ashes. Because the insults of the flesh arose after sin, let man see in the roughness of sackcloth what he did through pride, let him see in ashes how far he arrived through sinning. The sinner is pricked as by a certain sackcloth when he is rubbed in his mind by the harsh goads of reproofs. He does penance in ashes because from the first sin he carefully attends to what he has become through just judgment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In tenebras diem vertimus, cum, nosmetipsos districte punientes, ipsa delectationis pravae blandimenta per districta poenitentiae lamenta cruciamus, et flendo insequimur quidquid in corde taciti ex delectatione peccamus.
chunks.english: We turn day into darkness when, strictly punishing ourselves, we torment the very allurements of wicked pleasure through the strict laments of repentance, and weeping we pursue whatever we sinned from pleasure silently in the heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fidelis quisque, dum cogitationes in judicio exquiri subtiliter non ignorat, semetipsum introrsus discutiens, ante judicium vehementer examinat, ut districtus Judex eo jam tranquillus veniat, quo reum suum, quem discutere appetit, pro culpa punitum cernit.
chunks.english: Each faithful person, while not ignorant that thoughts are examined subtly in judgment, examining himself inwardly, examines himself vehemently before judgment, so that the strict Judge comes already tranquil to the extent that he sees his guilty one whom he desires to examine already punished for guilt.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnipotens Deus subtiliter acta nostra considerat, et tamen haec poenitentibus misericorditer relaxat.
