Qui et duritiam in peccantibus conspicit, sed tamen hanc, praeveniente gratia, ad poenitentiam emollit. Culpas nostras Deus enumerat, cum nos ipsos ad singula quae fecimus deflenda convertit. Quas misericorditer relaxat, quia eas dum nos punimus, ipse nequaquam in extremo examine judicat; Paulo attestante, qui ait: Si nosmetipsos dijudicaremus, non utique judicaremur (I ad Cor. XI, 31).
chunks.english: Almighty God considers our deeds subtly, and yet mercifully relaxes these for the penitent. He perceives hardness in sinners, but nevertheless, with grace going before, he softens this toward repentance. God enumerates our faults when he converts us ourselves to weeping for individual things we have done. He mercifully relaxes them because while we punish them, he by no means judges them in the final examination—as Paul attests, saying: If we judged ourselves, we would certainly not be judged—from Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter eleven, verse thirty-one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod exterius agimus, nisi poenitentia interveniente diluamus, in secreto judiciorum Dei sub quadam occultatione servatur, ut quandoque etiam de sigillo secreti exeat ad publicum judicii. Cum vero pro malis quae fecimus disciplinae flagello atterimur, et haec per poenitentiam deflemus, iniquitatem nostram signat, et curat quia nec inulta hic deserit, nec in judicio punienda reservat.
chunks.english: What we do outwardly, unless we wash it away with repentance intervening, is preserved in the secret of God's judgments under a certain concealment, so that sometimes it also comes out from the seal of secrecy to the public of judgment. But when we are worn away by the scourge of discipline for the evils we have done, and we weep for these through repentance, he seals and heals our iniquity because he neither abandons it here unavenged nor reserves it to be punished in judgment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Signat igitur Deus delicta nostra, quia hic ea subtiliter attendit, ut feriat; curat vero, quia haec per flagellum funditus relaxat. Beatus Job, humani generis assumens personam, dicit: Signasti quasi in sacculo delicta mea (Job XIV, 17). Peccata nostra signantur in sacculo, cum mala quae fecimus sollicito semper corde pensamus. Quid namque est cor hominis nisi sacculus Dei?
chunks.english: God therefore seals our sins because here he attends to them subtly in order to strike; he heals them because through the scourge he completely relaxes them. Blessed Job, assuming the person of the human race, says: You have sealed up my offenses as in a bag—from Job, chapter fourteen, verse seventeen. Our sins are sealed in a bag when we weigh with always anxious heart the evils we have done. For what is the heart of man but God's little bag?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Dei sacculo signata portamus, quia culpas quas nos intuendo et poenitendo cognoscimus pius nobis conditor relaxat. Recte post signata in sacculo delicta dicitur: Sed curasti iniquitatem meam (Job, ibid.), ac si aperte dicat: Quae modo signas, ut poenitendo videam, agis, procul dubio, ne in retributione videantur.
chunks.english: We carry sealed things in God's bag, because the merciful creator relaxes for us the faults which by observing and repenting we recognize. Rightly after sins sealed in the bag it is said: But you have healed my iniquity—from the same place in Job—as if to say openly: Those things which you now seal so that I may see them in repenting, you do without doubt so that they may not be seen in retribution.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XLVIII.
De confessione.
chunks.english: Chapter 48: On Confession

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Humilitatis testimonia sunt, et iniquitatem suam quemque cognoscere, et cognitam voce confessionis aperire. Usitatum humani generis vitium est, et labendo peccatum committere, et commissum non confitendo prodere, sed negando defendere, atque convictum defendendo multiplicare.
chunks.english: These are testimonies of humility: both to recognize one's own iniquity and to reveal what is recognized by the voice of confession. It is the customary vice of the human race both to commit sin by falling and to betray what is committed not by confessing but by denying and defending, and to multiply what is proven by defending it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex illo quippe lapsu primi hominis haec augmenta nequitiae ducimus, ex quo ipsam radicem traximus culpae. Ad hoc primus homo requisitus fuerat, ut peccatum quod transgrediendo commiserat, confitendo deleret; et interrogatur ubi esset, ut perpetratam culpam respiceret, et confitendo cognosceret, quam longe a conditoris sui facie abesset.
chunks.english: For from that fall of the first man we derive these increases of wickedness, from which we drew the very root of guilt. For this reason the first man was sought out: so that the sin he committed by transgressing he might delete by confessing; and he is asked where he is so that he might look back at the guilt perpetrated and by confessing might recognize how far he is from the face of his creator.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Indicia verae confessionis sunt, si cum quisque se peccatorem dicit, id de se dicenti etiam alteri non contradicit. Scriptum est: Justus in principio accusator est sui (Prov. XVIII, 17). Non magis peccator, sed justus videri appetit, cum peccatorem se quisquis nullo arguente confitetur.
chunks.english: These are signs of true confession: if when anyone calls himself a sinner, he does not contradict another who also says this about him. It is written: The just person is an accuser of himself in the beginning—from Proverbs, chapter eighteen, verse seventeen. One does not desire to appear more as a sinner but as just when anyone confesses himself a sinner with no one accusing him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Confessionis veritatem probat, cum alter malum quod fecimus increpat. Quod si superbe defendimus, liquet quia peccatores nos ex nobis ficte dicebamus. Curandum summopere est ut mala quae fecimus et sponte fateamur, et haec aliis arguentibus non negemus.
chunks.english: The truth of confession is proven when another rebukes the evil we have done. But if we proudly defend it, it is clear that we were calling ourselves sinners from ourselves fictitiously. It must be cared for supremely that we both spontaneously confess the evils we have done and do not deny these when others argue against us.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Superbiae quippe vitium est, ut quod se fateri quisque quasi sua sponte dignatur, hoc sibi dici ab aliis dedignetur. Pignus debitoris est confessio peccatoris; a debitore enim pignus accipitur, cum a peccatore jam peccati confessio tenetur.
chunks.english: For it is the vice of pride that what anyone deigns to confess as though of his own will, he disdains to have said of himself by others. The pledge of the debtor is the confession of the sinner; for a pledge is accepted from a debtor when the confession of sin is now held from the sinner.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT XLIX.
De gravitate consilii.
chunks.english: Chapter 49: On the Gravity of Counsel

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliud est gravem esse per consilium, aliud per peccatum; aliud est gravem esse per constantiam aliud per culpam. Ista enim gravitas pondus habet oneris, illa virtutis. Respectu supernae remunerationis hoc praemium plerumque percipiunt animae, uti ab intentione Dei non jam levi motu desiliant, sed in eo fixa constantiae gravitate consistant.
chunks.english: It is one thing to be weighty through counsel, another through sin; it is one thing to be weighty through constancy, another through guilt. For this gravity has the weight of burden, that one of virtue. In respect to heavenly reward, souls usually receive this prize: that they no longer leap away from the intention of God with light movement, but stand fixed in it with the gravity of constancy.
chunks.notes: Wordplay on gravitas: burden vs. dignity

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Judaicus populus leviter movebatur, de quo per prophetam dicitur: Abiit vagus in viam cordis sui: Viam ejus vidi, et dimisi eum (Isai. LVII, 17). Grave autem consilium cordis omnem inconstantiam vagationis expellit. Sunt animae quae levi motu nunc ista, nunc illa, desiderent. Omnipotens ergo Deus, quia ipsas leves fluctuationes mentium non leviter pensat, vagationem cordis relinquendo dijudicat.
chunks.english: The Jewish people were moved lightly, concerning whom it is said through the prophet: He went wandering in the way of his heart; I saw his way and abandoned him—from Isaiah, chapter fifty-seven, verse seventeen. But the grave counsel of the heart expels all inconstancy of wandering. There are souls that with light movement desire now these things, now those. The almighty God therefore, because he does not weigh lightly these light fluctuations of minds, judges by abandoning the wandering of the heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed cum per gratiam respicit vagam mentem, in consilii stabilitatem figit. Cum Deus omnipotens leves motus hominis misericorditer dignatur aspicere, hunc protinus ad constantiae maturitatem format, atque supernae gratiae respectu cor ejus ad gravitatem consilii repente perducit.
chunks.english: But when through grace he looks upon a wandering mind, he fixes it in the stability of counsel. When almighty God mercifully deigns to look upon the light movements of man, he immediately forms him to the maturity of constancy, and by the regard of heavenly grace suddenly leads his heart to the gravity of counsel.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT L.
De hospitalitate.
chunks.english: Chapter 50: On Hospitality

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hospitalitatem fraternitatis amare, charitatis opera diligere, nobis valde necessarium est. Unde egregius praedicator, nobis utraque commendans, ait: Charitas fraternitatis maneat in vobis, et hospitalitatem nolite oblivisci (Hebr. XIII, 1). Beatus Petrus princeps apostolorum hujus hospitalitatis gratiam cum benignitate fratribus exhibendam nobis commendat, dicens: Hospitales invicem sine murmurationibus (I Pet. IV, 9).
chunks.english: To love the hospitality of brotherhood, to cherish works of charity, is very necessary for us. Hence the distinguished preacher, commending both to us, says: Let the charity of brotherhood remain in you, and do not forget hospitality—from the letter to the Hebrews, chapter thirteen, verse one. Blessed Peter, prince of the apostles, commends to us that the grace of this hospitality be shown to brothers with kindness, saying: Be hospitable to one another without grumbling—from Peter's first letter, chapter four, verse nine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc ipsa Veritas dicit: Hospes fui, et suscepistis me (Matth. XXV, 35). Mira pietate Redemptor noster loquitur ex suorum compassione membrorum, dicens: Quodcunque uni ex minimis istis fecistis, mihi fecistis (Ibid., 40). Ipse igitur nos per hoc quod caput nostrum est adjuvat, qui per nostra bona opera in suis membris adjuvatur.
chunks.english: Hence Truth itself says: I was a stranger and you welcomed me—from the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-five, verse thirty-five. With wonderful mercy our Redeemer speaks from compassion for his members, saying: Whatever you did for one of the least of these, you did for me—from the same place, verse forty. He therefore helps us through the fact that he is our head, who is helped through our good works in his members.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pensandum nobis est quantum hospitalitas fraternae compassionis valeat, quantum nos omnipotenti Domino misericordiae viscera conjungant. Inde enim ei qui est super omnia appropinquamus, unde nos per compassionem proximi etiam sub nosmetipsos deponimus. In rebus corporalibus nemo alta tangit, nisi qui tenditur; in rebus vero spiritalibus certum est quia quanto plus per compassionem attrahimur, tanto ultis verius propinquamus.
chunks.english: We must consider how much the hospitality of fraternal compassion is worth, how much the bowels of mercy join us to the almighty Lord. For from that place we draw near to him who is above all things, from which place we place ourselves even below ourselves through compassion for our neighbor. In bodily matters no one touches high things unless he is stretched; but in spiritual matters it is certain that the more we are drawn down through compassion, the more truly we draw near to the heights.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Redemptori generis humani ad aedificationem nostram minime sufficit quod in extremo judicio dicturum se esse perhibuit: Quandiu fecistis uni ex his fratribus meis minimis, mihi fecistis (Matth. XXV, 42), nisi et ante judicium hoc in se ostenderet quod dixisset; ut videlicet demonstraret quia bona quisquis nunc opera indigentibus exhibet, ei haec specialiter impendit cujus haec amore exhibuerit.
chunks.english: It does not suffice for the Redeemer of the human race for our edification that he declared he would say in the final judgment: As long as you did it for one of these my least brothers, you did it for me—from the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-five, verse forty-two—unless he also showed in himself before judgment what he had said; namely to demonstrate that whoever now shows good works to the needy, he specially renders these to him for whose love he showed them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et tanto in se plus quisque majorem mercedem recipit, quanto nec eum despicit qui amplius despiciendus videtur.
chunks.english: And anyone receives a greater reward in proportion as he does not despise the one who seems more worthy of being despised.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT LI.
De corruptione vitae praesentis.
chunks.english: Chapter 51: On the Corruption of Present Life

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Justis in hac vita positis ipsa sarcina suae corruptionis onerosa est, quod vigiliae defatigant, somnus quaeritur, ut vigiliarum labor atque anxietas temperetur. Nonnunquam etiam sonus occidit, fames corpus atterit, atque ut ejus necessitas repellatur, cibi requiruntur. Sed saepe et cibi gravant, qui ad repellendum debilitatis gravamen quaesiti fuerant.
chunks.english: For the just established in this life, the very burden of their corruption is burdensome: vigils exhaust, sleep is sought so that the labor and anxiety of vigils may be tempered. Sometimes even sound kills, hunger wears away the body, and food is sought so that its necessity may be repelled. But often also food weighs down—food that had been sought to repel the weight of weakness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Gravis est valde sarcina corruptionis, quae nisi vehementer onerosa esset, Paulus nequaquam diceret: Vanitati creatura subjecta est non volens, sed propter eum qui subjecit in spe, quia et ipsa creatura liberabitur a servitute corruptionis in libertatem gloriae filiorum Dei. Scimus enim quod omnis creatura congemiscit et parturit usque adhuc (Rom. VIII, 20).
chunks.english: Very heavy is the burden of corruption; unless it were vehemently burdensome, Paul would by no means say: Creation was subjected to futility not willingly, but because of him who subjected it in hope, because creation itself will be freed from the slavery of corruption into the freedom of the glory of the children of God. For we know that all creation groans and labors until now—from Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter eight, verse twenty.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quousque subditi corruptioni sumus, Auctori nostro minime respondemus, quia dum corruptio ab incorruptione longe est, similitudo apta nostrae responsioni non est. Beatus Job, aeternae incorruptionis statum desiderans, dicit: Operi manuum tuarum porriges dexteram tuam (Job. XIV, 15).
chunks.english: As long as we are subjected to corruption, we scarcely correspond to our Maker, because while corruption is far from incorruption, there is no fitting likeness for our correspondence. Blessed Job, desiring the state of eternal incorruption, says: You will extend your right hand to the work of your hands—from Job, chapter fourteen, verse fifteen.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ac si patenter dicat: Idcirco creatura corruptibilis persistere ad incorruptionem potest, quia Dei omnipotentis manu erigitur, et sui respectus gratia ut persistat tenetur. Humana creatura eo ipso quo creatura est, in semetipsa habet sub se defluere; sed a conditore suo homo accipit, ut et super se contemplatione rapiatur, et in seipso in incorruptione teneatur.
chunks.english: As if to say openly: Therefore the corruptible creature can persist toward incorruption because it is raised up by the hand of almighty God, and by the grace of his regard is held so that it persists. Human creation by the very fact that it is creation has in itself to flow down beneath itself; but from his creator man receives that he be both rapt above himself in contemplation and be held in incorruption in himself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Creatura humana corruptioni subdita, ne sub se defluat, sed in incorruptione persistat, ad incommutabilitatis statum Auctoris sui dextera levatur.
chunks.english: Human creation, subjected to corruption, lest it flow down beneath itself but persist in incorruption, is lifted up to the state of immutability by the right hand of its Maker.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT LII.
De salute corporis.
chunks.english: Chapter 52: On Bodily Health

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt incolumes, ut salutem corporis exerceant ad salutem mentis; ne si acceptae incolumitatis gratiam ad usum nequitiae inclinent, dono deteriores fiant: et eo postmodum supplicia graviora mereantur, quo nunc largioribus bonis Dei male uti non metuunt.
chunks.english: The healthy are to be admonished to exercise bodily health toward the health of the mind, lest if they incline the grace of received health to the use of wickedness, they become worse by the gift, and afterward merit graver punishments to the degree that now they do not fear to use badly God's more abundant goods.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt incolumes, ne opportunitatem salutis in perpetuum promerendae despiciant. Scriptum namque est: Ecce nunc acceptabile, ecce nunc dies salutis (II Cor. VI, 2). Dicendum est eis, ne placere Deo, si cum possunt noluerint, cum voluerint sero non possint.
chunks.english: The healthy are to be admonished not to despise the opportunity of meriting salvation in perpetuity. For it is written: Behold now is the acceptable time, behold now is the day of salvation—from Paul's second letter to the Corinthians, chapter six, verse two. It must be said to them lest, if when they are able they are unwilling to please God, when they are willing they are late unable.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Salus corporis, quando ad bene operandum accepta despicitur, quanti sit muneris amissa sentitur; et infructuose ad ultimum quaeritur, quae congruo concessa tempore utiliter non habetur.
chunks.english: Bodily health, when received for doing good it is despised, is felt to be of what great gift when it is lost; and it is sought fruitlessly at the end, which when granted at the fitting time is not held usefully.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Sapientissimum Salomonem dicitur: Ne des alienis laborem tuum, et annos tuos crudeli, ne forte impleantur extranei viribus tuis, et labores tui sint in domo aliena, et gemas in novissimis, quando consumpseris carnes et corpus tuum (Prov. V, 9 seq.).
chunks.english: Through the most wise Solomon it is said: Do not give your labor to strangers, and your years to the cruel one, lest perhaps strangers be filled with your strength and your labors be in a foreign house, and you groan in the end when you have consumed your flesh and body—from Proverbs, chapter five, verse nine and following.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quinamque a nobis alieni sunt, nisi maligni spiritus, qui a coelestis sunt patriae sorte separati? Quis vero honor noster est, nisi quod etiam in luteis corporibus conditi, ad conditoris tamen nostri sumus imaginem et similitudinem creati? Vel quis alius crudelis est, nisi ille apostata angelus, qui et semetipsum poena mortis superbiendo perculit, et inferre mortem humano generi etiam perditus non pepercit?
chunks.english: Who indeed are strangers to us but evil spirits who are separated from the lot of the heavenly homeland? What truly is our honor but that even formed in clay bodies, we are nevertheless created in the image and likeness of our creator? Or who else is cruel but that apostate angel who both struck himself with the punishment of death through pride, and even though lost did not spare inflicting death on the human race?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Honorem suum alienis dat, qui, ad Dei imaginem ac similitudinem conditus, vitae suae tempora malignorum spirituum voluptatibus administrat. Annos etiam suos crudeli tradit, qui ad voluntatem male dominantis adversarii accepta vivendi spatia expendit.
chunks.english: He gives his honor to strangers who, formed in the image and likeness of God, administers the times of his life to the pleasures of evil spirits. He also delivers his years to the cruel one who expends the spaces of living received to the will of the adversary ruling badly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quisquis per acceptam valetudinem corporis, per attributam sibi sapientiam mentis non exercendis virtutibus, sed perpetrandis vitiis, elaborat, nequaquam suis viribus suam domum, sed extraneorum habitacula, id est, immundorum spirituum facta, multiplicat, nimirum vel luxuriando, vel superbiendo agens, ut etiam se addito perditorum numerus crescat.
chunks.english: Whoever through received bodily health, through wisdom of mind attributed to him, labors not in exercising virtues but in perpetrating vices, by no means with his own strength multiplies his own house, but the dwellings of strangers, that is, the deeds of unclean spirits, certainly acting either by lusting or by being proud, so that the number of the lost grows with himself added.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque accepta salus carnis per vitia expenditur; sed cum repente subtrahitur, cum molestiis caro atteritur, cum jam egredi anima urgetur, diu male habita, quasi ad bene vivendum salus amissa requiritur. Tunc recte gemunt homines, quod Deo servire noluerunt, quando damna suae negligentiae recuperare serviendo nequaquam possunt. Unde alias dicitur: Cum occideret eos, tunc quaerebant eum (Ps. LXXVII, 34).
chunks.english: Often received health of the flesh is expended through vices; but when it is suddenly withdrawn, when the flesh is worn away with troubles, when the soul is now urged to depart, long badly possessed, as though the lost health is sought for living well. Then men rightly groan that they were unwilling to serve God, when they can by no means recover the losses of their negligence by serving. Hence elsewhere it is said: When he killed them, then they sought him—from Psalm seventy-seven, verse thirty-four.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT LIII.
De aegritudine corporis.
chunks.english: Chapter 53: On Bodily Sickness

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt aegri, ut eo se Dei filios sentiant, quod illos disciplinae flagella castigant. Nisi enim correctis filiis haereditatem dare disponeret, erudire eos per molestias non curaret. Dicendum est aegris ut si coelestem patriam suam credunt, necessarium est ut in hac labores, velut in aliena patria, patiantur.
chunks.english: The sick are to be admonished to feel themselves to be God's sons by the fact that the scourges of discipline chastise them. For unless he were disposing to give an inheritance to corrected sons, he would not care to train them through troubles. It must be said to the sick that if they believe the heavenly homeland is theirs, it is necessary that they suffer labors in this one as in a foreign homeland.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Lapides sanctuarii Domini extra tunsi sunt, ut in constructione templi absque mallei sonitu ponerentur. Quia videlicet nunc foras per flagella tundimur, ut intus in templum Dei postmodum sine disciplinae percussione disponamur; quatenus quidquid in nobis est superfluum modo percussio resecet, et tunc sola nos in aedificio concordia charitatis liget.
chunks.english: The stones of the Lord's sanctuary were struck outside so that they might be placed in the construction of the temple without the sound of a hammer. Because clearly now we are struck outside through scourges, so that afterward we may be arranged inside in the temple of God without the percussion of discipline; so that whatever is superfluous in us the striking may now cut off, and then the concord of charity alone may bind us in the building.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt aegri, ut considerent pro percipiendis terrenis haereditatibus, quam dura carnis filios disciplinae flagella castigent. Cur ergo nobis divinae correctionis poena gravis est, per quam et nunquam amittenda haereditas percipitur, et semper mansura supplicia vitantur?
chunks.english: The sick are to be admonished to consider, for receiving earthly inheritances, how hard are the scourges of discipline that chastise carnal sons. Why therefore is the punishment of divine correction heavy for us, through which both an inheritance never to be lost is received and punishments always to remain are avoided?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Considerent aegri quanta salus cordis sit molestia corporalis, quae ad cognitionem sui mentem revocat, et quam plerumque salus abjicit, infirmitatis memoria reformat, ut animus, qui extra se in elatione ducitur cui sit conditioni subditus ex percussa quam sustinet carne memoretur.
chunks.english: Let the sick consider what great salvation of the heart is bodily trouble, which recalls the mind to knowledge of itself, and what health often rejects, the memory of infirmity reforms, so that the soul which is led outside itself in elation may be reminded from the struck flesh which it sustains of what condition it is subjected to.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque caro per molestias tarda flagello suo menti Deum indicat, quem mens ipsa carni praesidens non videbat, ita ut anxietatem spiritus proficere in hoc mundo cupientis, velut iter tendentis impediat, donec ei invisibilem qui sibi obviat innotescat.
chunks.english: Often the flesh, slow through troubles, indicates God to the mind by its scourge—God whom the mind itself presiding over the flesh did not see—so that it impedes the anxiety of the spirit desiring to advance in this world, as of one extending a journey, until the invisible one who meets him becomes known to him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt aegri, ut considerent quanti sit muneris molestia corporalis, quae et admissa peccata diluit, et ea quae poterant admitti compescit, quae sumpta ab exterioribus plagis concussae menti poenitentiae vulnera infligit. In Proverbiis Salomonis scriptum est: Livor vulneris abstergit mala, et plagae in secretioribus ventris (Prov. XX, 20).
chunks.english: The sick are to be admonished to consider of how great a gift is bodily trouble, which both washes away committed sins and restrains those which could be committed, which taken from exterior blows inflicts the wounds of repentance on the shaken mind. In the Proverbs of Solomon it is written: The bruise of a wound cleanses away evils, and blows in the inner parts of the belly—from Proverbs, chapter twenty, verse twenty.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mala enim livor vulneris abstergit, quia flagellorum dolor vel cogitatas vel perpetratas nequitias diluit. Cum exterius percutimur, ad peccatorum nostrorum memoriam taciti afflictique revocamur, ante oculos nostros cuncta quae a nobis sunt male gesta reducimus, et per hoc quod foras patimur magis intus quod fecimus dolemus.
chunks.english: For the bruise of a wound cleanses away evils because the pain of scourges washes away either thought or perpetrated wickednesses. When we are struck outwardly, we are recalled silent and afflicted to the memory of our sins, we bring back before our eyes all things that were done badly by us, and through this that we suffer outwardly we grieve more inwardly for what we have done.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde fit ut inter aperta vulnera corporis amplius nos abluat plaga secreta ventris, quia sanat nequitias pravi operis occultum vulnus doloris.
chunks.english: Whence it happens that amid the open wounds of the body the secret blow of the belly washes us more fully, because the hidden wound of pain heals the wickednesses of evil work.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt aegri quatenus patientiae virtutem servent, ut incessanter quanta Redemptor ab his quos creaverat pertulit mala considerent. Cur itaque asperum creditur ut a Deo homo toleret flagella pro malis, si tanta Deus ab hominibus pertulit mala pro bonis? Aut quis sana intelligentia de percussione sua ingratus exstet, si ipse hinc sine flagello non exiit, qui hic sine peccato vixit?
chunks.english: The sick are to be admonished to preserve the virtue of patience, so that they consider unceasingly how great evils the Redeemer endured from those he had created. Why therefore is it believed harsh that man endure scourges from God for evils, if God endured so great evils from men for goods? Or who would be ungrateful with sound understanding concerning his own striking, if he himself did not depart hence without scourge who lived here without sin?

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin:
CAPUT LIV.
De morte corporis.
chunks.english: Chapter 54: On Bodily Death

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praefixi dies singulis ab interna Dei praescientia nec augeri possunt, nec minui, nisi contingat ut ita praesciantur, ut aut cum optimis operibus longiores sint, aut cum pessimis breviores, sicut Ezechias augmentum dierum meruit impensione lacrymarum, et sicut de perversis scriptum est: Indisciplinatis obviat mors.
chunks.english: The days fixed for individuals by the internal foreknowledge of God can neither be increased nor diminished, unless it happens that they are so foreknown that either with the best works they are longer, or with the worst shorter, just as Hezekiah merited the increase of days by the outpouring of tears, and as it is written concerning the perverse: Death meets the undisciplined.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omne quod secundum praesens saeculum laboramus vix usque ad mortem sufficit. Mors namque interveniens fructum nostri laboris abscidit; quod vero pro aeterna vita agitur, etiam post mortem servatur; et tunc apparere incipit, cum laborum carnalium fructus coeperit non videri. Ibi ergo illa retributio inchoat, ubi ista terminatur.
chunks.english: Everything that we labor according to the present age scarcely suffices until death. For death intervening cuts off the fruit of our labor; but what is done for eternal life is preserved even after death, and then begins to appear when the fruit of carnal labors begins not to be seen. There therefore that retribution begins where this one is terminated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Horam nobis ultimam Dominus noster idcirco voluit esse incognitam, ut semper possit esse suspecta; ut dum illam praevidere non possumus, ad illam sine intermissione praeparemur. Quia venturae mortis tempus ignoramus, et post mortem operari non possumus, superest ut ante mortem tempora indulta rapiamus.
chunks.english: Our Lord wanted the last hour to be unknown to us for this reason: that it might always be able to be suspected; so that while we cannot foresee it, we may prepare for it without interruption. Because we do not know the time of coming death, and after death we cannot work, it remains that before death we seize the times granted.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic enim mors ipsa cum venerit vincetur, si priusquam veniat semper timeatur. Redemptor noster suscepit mortem, ne mori timeremus. Ostendit resurrectionem, ut nos resurgere posse confidamus. Unde et eamdem mortem non plusquam triduanam esse voluit, ne si in illo resurrectio differretur, in nobis omnimodo desperaretur.
chunks.english: For thus death itself when it comes will be conquered, if before it comes it is always feared. Our Redeemer underwent death so that we would not fear to die. He showed resurrection so that we might be confident that we can rise. Hence he wanted that same death to be no more than three days, lest if resurrection were deferred in him, it would be despaired of altogether in us.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nos per mortem carnis usque ad finem mundi remanemus in pulvere; Christus autem die tertia ab ariditate mortis viruit, et divinitatis suae nobis potentiam in ipsa innovatione suae carnis ostendit.
chunks.english: We through the death of the flesh remain in dust until the end of the world; but Christ on the third day sprouted from the dryness of death, and showed to us the power of his divinity in the very renewal of his flesh.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Exutam carne animam nequaquam jam visus hominis aspicit, quia post mortem non liberat, quem ante mortem gratia ad veniam non reformat. Beatus David ait: Exibit spiritus eorum, et revertetur in terram suam (Psal. CXLV, 4). Tunc spiritus per cognitionem reatus sui ad terram consternitur, cum caro, quam vitam suam credit, redire ad pulverem urgetur.
chunks.english: The soul stripped of flesh no longer looks upon human sight, because it does not free after death the one whom grace does not reform to pardon before death. Blessed David says: Their spirit will go out and will return to its earth—from Psalm one hundred forty-five, verse four. Then the spirit is cast down to earth through knowledge of its guilt when the flesh, which it believes is its life, is urged to return to dust.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Paululum in praesenti vita roboratus est homo, quia hic vivendi vires ad modicum accepit, ut in perpetuum transeat, ubi ejus vitam terminus non concludat. Sed in hac brevitate, ubi roboratus est, colligit unde in perpetuitate inveniat, vel ut semper gaudeat, vel ut suscepta supplicia non evadat.
chunks.english: Man is strengthened a little in the present life because here he received the strength of living for a little, so that he may pass into perpetuity where the boundary does not close his life. But in this brevity where he is strengthened, he gathers from where in perpetuity he may find either to rejoice always or not to escape received punishments.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In libro beati Job scriptum est: Immutabis faciem ejus, et emittes eum (Job XIV, 20). Facies hominis immutatur, cum ejus species morte atteritur. Emittitur vero, quia ab his quae volens tenuit, transire ad aeterna cogitur nolens. Dumque ad illa perducitur, haec quae diu cogitata tenuit, qualiter sese habitura sint relicta nescit.
chunks.english: In the book of blessed Job it is written: You will change his face and send him forth—from Job, chapter fourteen, verse twenty. The face of man is changed when his appearance is worn away by death. He is sent forth because from those things which he held willingly, he is forced unwillingly to pass to eternal things. And while he is led to those things, he does not know how these things which he held thought about for a long time will be disposed when left behind.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut hi qui adhuc viventes sunt, mortuorum animae quo loco habeantur ignorant, ita mortui vita in carne viventium post eos qualiter disponatur nesciunt, quia et vita spiritus longe est a vita carnis, et sicut corporea atque incorporea diversa sunt genere, ita etiam distincta cognitione.
chunks.english: Just as those who are still living do not know in what place the souls of the dead are held, so the dead do not know how the life in the flesh of those living after them is disposed, because the life of the spirit is far from the life of the flesh, and just as corporeal and incorporeal things are diverse in kind, so also they are distinct in knowledge.
chunks.notes: Epistemological separation of living and dead

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod tamen de animabus sanctis sentiendum non est, quia quae intus omnipotentis Dei claritatem vident, nullo modo credendum est quia sit foris aliquid quod ignorent.
chunks.english: This however is not to be thought concerning holy souls, because those which see within the brightness of almighty God, it is by no means to be believed that there is anything outside which they do not know.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnipotens Dominus ideo latere nos voluit finem nostrum, ut dum incerti sumus quando moriamur, semper ad mortem parati inveniri debeamus. Cur igitur quasi de certo extollitur, cujus vita sub poena incertitudinis tenetur?
chunks.english: The almighty Lord wanted our end to be hidden from us for this reason: so that while we are uncertain when we will die, we should always be found prepared for death. Why therefore does he lift himself up as though certain, whose life is held under the penalty of uncertainty?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Paulus egregius praedicator ait: Qui sumus in hoc habitaculo, ingemiscimus gravati, eo quod nolumus exspoliari, sed supervestiri, ut absorbeatur quod mortale est a vita (II Cor. V, 4). Ecce et mori desiderat, et tamen carne exspoliari formidat. Cur hoc? quia etsi victoria in perpetuum laetificat, ipsa nihilominus ad praesens mortis poena perturbat.
chunks.english: Paul the distinguished preacher says: We who are in this dwelling groan, being burdened, because we do not wish to be unclothed but to be clothed over, so that what is mortal may be swallowed up by life—from Paul's second letter to the Corinthians, chapter five, verse four. Behold, he desires to die, and yet fears to be stripped of flesh. Why this? Because even if victory gladdens in perpetuity, nevertheless the punishment of death at present disturbs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quamvis vincat amor subsequentis muneris, tangit tamen non sine moerore animum pulsus doloris. Sicut enim vir fortis, cum vicino jam belli certamine armis accingitur, palpitat et festinat, tremit et saevit, quasi pavere per pallorem cernitur, sed per iram vehementer urgetur: ita vir sanctus, cum passioni mortis propinquare se conspicit, a naturae suae infirmitate concutitur, et spei suae soliditate roboratur.
chunks.english: And although the love of the following gift conquers, nevertheless the impulse of pain touches the soul not without sorrow. For just as a strong man, when he girds himself with arms with the battle contest already near, palpitates and hastens, trembles and rages, is seen as though to fear through pallor, but is vehemently urged through anger: so the holy man, when he perceives himself approaching the passion of death, is shaken by the weakness of his nature and strengthened by the solidity of his hope.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De vicina quidem morte trepidat, et tamen, quod moriendo verius vivat, exsultat. Unusquisque vir sanctus ad regnum non potest nisi interposita morte transire. Et idcirco confidendo quasi ambigit, et quasi ambigendo confidit, et gaudens metuit, et metuens gaudet, quia scit quod ad bravium quietis non perveniet, nisi hoc quod interjacet cum labore transcendat.
chunks.english: He trembles indeed concerning nearby death, and yet exults that he will live more truly by dying. Each holy man cannot pass to the kingdom except through interposed death. And therefore trusting he as it were doubts, and as it were doubting he trusts, and rejoicing he fears, and fearing he rejoices, because he knows that he will not arrive at the prize of rest unless he transcends with labor this that lies between.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum morbos a corpore nostro repellere cupimus, tristes quidem amarum purgationis poculum sumimus, certi autem de subsequenti salute gaudemus. Quia enim pervenire corpus aliter ad salutem non valet, in potum libet etiam quod taedet. Cumque amaritudini inesse vitam animus conspicit, moerore turbatus hilarescit.
chunks.english: When we desire to repel diseases from our body, sad indeed we take the bitter drink of purgation, but certain we rejoice concerning subsequent health. For because the body cannot otherwise arrive at health, even what disgusts is pleasing in drink. And when the soul perceives life to be present in bitterness, troubled by sorrow it grows cheerful.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In libro Salomonis scriptum est: Si ceciderit lignum ad Austrum, aut ad Aquilonem, in quocunque loco ceciderit, ibi erit (Eccle. XI, 3). Ligni namque nomine vir justus aut etiam injustus exprimitur, sicut per prophetam Dominus dicit: Ego Dominus humiliavi lignum sublime (Ezech. XVII, 24).
chunks.english: In the book of Solomon it is written: If a tree falls to the south or to the north, in whatever place it falls, there it will be—from Ecclesiastes, chapter eleven, verse three. For by the name of tree is expressed either a just man or also an unjust one, just as the Lord says through the prophet: I the Lord have humbled the lofty tree—from Ezekiel, chapter seventeen, verse twenty-four.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In die mortis suae justus ad Austrum cadit, peccator ad Aquilonem, quia et justus per fervorem spiritus ad gaudia ducitur, et peccator cum apostata angelo, qui dixit: Sedebo in monte testamenti in lateribus Aquilonis (Isai. XIV, 13), in frigido suo corde reprobatur.
chunks.english: On the day of his death the just person falls to the south, the sinner to the north, because both the just person is led through fervor of spirit to joys, and the sinner is rejected in his cold heart with the apostate angel who said: I will sit on the mount of the covenant in the sides of the north—from Isaiah, chapter fourteen, verse thirteen.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Lignum in quocunque loco ceciderit, sive ad Austrum, sive ad Aquilonem, ibi erit, quia cum humani casus tempore, sive sanctus, seu malignus spiritus egredientem animam claustra carnis acceperit, in aeternum secum sine ulla permutatione retinebit. Ut nec exaltata ad judicium proruat, nec mersa aeternis suppliciis, ultra ad remedium ereptionis ascendat.
chunks.english: The tree, in whatever place it falls, whether to the south or to the north, there it will be, because when at the time of human fall either a holy or an evil spirit receives the soul departing from the enclosures of the flesh, it will retain it with itself in eternity without any change. So that neither does the exalted one fall to judgment, nor does the one submerged in eternal punishments rise beyond to the remedy of rescue.
chunks.notes: Irreversibility of judgment at death; conclusion of entire work
